content_html: God has forgiven the guilt and rebellion mentioned in Psalm 32:1. The Lord’s nature is to reconcile and forgive (Psalms 78:38; 85:2; 130:4), but he requires people to confess and repent.

title: Psalms 32:6
content_html: The godly will learn from the psalmist’s experience to pray for forgiveness before it is too late.

title: Psalms 32:7
content_html: The Lord promises to be a safe place for his people (Psalm 31:20). Instead of feeling terrified (Psalm 31:13), the psalmist hears songs of victory all around.

title: Psalms 32:8
content_html: God interrupts the psalmist's teaching with a message inviting the godly to receive the Lord's wisdom (see Psalms 5:8; 23:3; 25:4–6).

title: Psalms 32:9
content_html: The foolish person lacks wisdom. Ignoring the Lord’s advice harms life and makes a person like an animal (see Isaiah 1:3; James 3:3).

title: Psalms 32:10
content_html: Difficulties common to all of life will come to the wicked, whereas the Lord promises to protect the godly with his care.

title: Psalms 33:1–3
content_html: The psalmist urges the godly to praise the Lord with melodies, with a new song, and with skillfully-played music.

title: Psalms 33:1–22
content_html: This hymn of creation (see also Psalms 8, 19, 104, 145) might be a continuation of Psalm 32. It urges readers to praise God (Psalm 33:1–3), to recognize the power of his word in creation (33:4–7), and to respect the Creator (33:8–11). It gives hope to forgiven sinners (Psalm 32) that they can live a new life in the fear of the Lord and under divine protection.

title: Psalms 33:4–7
content_html: God's word shows his character and power.

title: Psalms 33:7
content_html: The nations surrounding Israel thought that the sea had divine power, but it is under God’s control, within boundaries that he set (see Psalm 104:8–10).

title: Psalms 33:8
content_html: The power of the Lord extends to all the nations (see Psalm 22; 96:123).

title: Psalms 33:8–11
content_html: Fear of the Lord begins with awe for God and for his powerful word. His word created everything and continues to order and sustain creation. He frustrates the plans of people who oppose his will.

title: Psalms 33:10
content_html: The plans of powerful people will only succeed if God allows it (see Isaiah 8:9210).

title: Psalms 33:11
content_html:
• God’s word still orders creation and will do so forever.

  • God's purposes are wonderful (Psalms 40:5; 92:5).

title: Psalms 33:12
content_html: People who fear God are chosen as his inheritance (see Psalm 33:13–19). This does not include rebellious people (Psalm 78:62).

title: Psalms 33:13–19
content_html: From the heavens, the Lord searches out and cares for anyone who fears him (see Psalm 14:2–3).

title: Psalms 33:16–17
content_html: army …great strength. … horse: Even military power is under God's control (see Psalm 33:10–11; Ecclesiastes 9:11).

title: Psalms 33:18
content_html: God watches over all who live by his counsel (Psalm 32:8). He protects, rescues, and stays close to them (Psalm 34:15–22).

title: Psalms 33:20–22
content_html: The faithful see God's power in creation and world events. They respond with trust, commitment, joy, and hope as they pray and wait for God's help.

title: Psalms 33:22
content_html: The psalmist uses wordplay to contrast his people's certain hope (Hebrew yakhal) in the Lord with the supposed strength (Hebrew khayil) of the wicked's horses.

title: Psalms 34:1
content_html: Abimelech is another name for Achish (see 1 Samuel 21:10–15). The body of the psalm makes no explicit connection to this event.

title: Psalms 34:1–3
content_html: The psalmist encourages those in need to join him in praise.

title: Psalms 34:1–22
content_html: This wisdom psalm includes a thanksgiving song of praise (Psalm 34:1–7) that celebrates the Lord for caring for and protecting godly sufferers. It also includes an invitation to wisdom (Psalm 34:8–14) and explains wisdom concerning the Lord’s care for the needy and the suffering of the wicked (Psalm 34:15–22).

title: Psalms 34:5–6
content_html: The psalmist describes his experiences of answered prayer.

title: Psalms 34:7
content_html: The angel of the LORD showed God's presence during the journey through the wilderness. He protected Israel from Egypt's forces (Exodus 14:19–20), and he will guard all those who are godly (Psalm 91:11).

title: Psalms 34:8–14
content_html: The godly encourage everyone to seek wisdom, to fear the Lord, and to place themselves under the protection of the Lord’s angel.

title: Psalms 34:9–10
content_html: Fear of the Lord includes reverence and respect, and it motivates a life of wisdom (see Proverbs 1:7; 9:10).

title: Psalms 34:11
content_html: Anyone who listens, even children, can become wise followers of God.

title: Psalms 34:11–13
content_html:
Wisdom has three parts:

  1. fearing the Lord (Psalm 34:11),

  2. doing good (Psalm 34:14), and

  3. rejecting evil (Psalm 34:13).

title: Psalms 34:12–16
content_html: Peter quotes this passage in his instructions for peaceful living (1 Peter 3:10–12).

title: Psalms 34:13
content_html: Godly people choose their words carefully. Speaking evil and telling lies is unwise (see Psalm 10:7; compare 17:1).

title: Psalms 34:14
content_html: The way of peace leads to harmony and order, whereas evil destroys them (see Psalm 37:11).

title: Psalms 34:15–16
content_html: The Lord distinguishes between wise and foolish people; he shows his concern for the wise by rescuing them from trouble (Psalm 34:15, 17–20). However, God destroys the foolish (Psalm 34:16, 21).

title: Psalms 34:18
content_html: Those who are brokenhearted have a spirit of deep sorrow for sin and a strong dependence on the Lord. God accepts this humble spirit as a proper sacrifice (Psalm 51:17). The wicked hate people who are brokenhearted (Psalm 109:16). But the Lord heals them and restores them (Psalm 147:3; see Isaiah 57:15; 61:1).

title: Psalms 34:19–22
content_html: The godly might suffer, but the Lord will reward them in the end. Similarly, the wicked might prosper for a time, but ultimately they will perish (Psalms 1:6; 34:15–16).

title: Psalms 34:20
content_html:
• Bones represent a person's whole self (see Psalm 6:2).

  • not one of them will be broken: John applied this statement to Jesus at the crucifixion (John 19:36).

title: Psalms 35:1–28
content_html: In this lament, the Lord's troubled servant asks God to notice his situation and defend him.

title: Psalms 35:4–10
content_html: The psalmist desires the Lord's salvation. The language may seem harsh, but it comes from a wish for justice and for the Lord to help the needy and oppressed.

title: Psalms 35:6
content_html:
• A dark and slick path suggests instability and sudden disaster under the Lord's judgment (see Psalms 69:23; 73:18; 82:5; 107:10; 143:3).

  • The angel of the LORD is an agent of judgment as well as salvation.

title: Psalms 35:10
content_html: Nothing in the universe can compare with the Lord (see Psalms 86:8; 89:6, 8; Isaiah 40:18, 25).

title: Psalms 35:11
content_html: False witnesses want nothing but violence (see Psalms 11:4–6; 54:3).

title: Psalms 35:11–18
content_html: The psalmist presents the Lord with the reasons for his suffering. Evil has many faces. He is tired and worn out, and he knows that only the Lord can help him.

title: Psalms 35:16
content_html: The psalmist's attackers gnashed their teeth to show their jealousy.

title: Psalms 35:19
content_html: John applied this claim of innocence (see also Psalm 69:4) to Jesus (John 15:25).

title: Psalms 35:19–25
content_html:
In this prayer for justice, the psalmist faces a crisis so great that only the Lord can resolve it. The psalmist chooses the right path and believes that vengeance belongs to the Lord.

God’s patience allows the wicked to continue in their evil ways. Because of this, their hearts grow harder and they find reasons to justify their behavior.

title: Psalms 35:21
content_html: Aha, aha!: This Hebrew word (he’akh) expresses a feeling of joy.

title: Psalms 35:26–28
content_html: The psalmist calls on the Lord to judge the wicked and to rescue the godly and bring them joy.

title: Psalms 36:1
content_html: no fear of God: Paul uses this phrase to describe how all people are morally corrupt (Romans 3:18).

title: Psalms 36:1–4
content_html: Foolish people reject wisdom and goodness while they chase after evil.

title: Psalms 36:1–12
content_html: This lament contrasts the foolish world made by the wicked with the wise world created and maintained by the Lord. The wicked care only about themselves, while the Lord cares for all creation. He will step in to bring justice to his world. The righteous will enjoy his protection, but the wicked will die.

title: Psalms 36:5–6
content_html: God’s all-encompassing goodness surpasses that of the created order. God graciously meets his people’s practical needs.

title: Psalms 36:5–9
content_html: As the source of wisdom, the Lord is reliable and incomparable. His faithful care extends to all parts of the created order.

title: Psalms 36:6–9
content_html: All creatures live by God's grace (1 Timothy 4:10).

title: Psalms 36:7–8
content_html: God invites everyone to a feast in his house (compare Proverbs 9:1–6).

title: Psalms 36:9
content_html: Light represents salvation and celebration (Psalms 18:25–29; 27:1; compare Psalm 35:6).

title: Psalms 36:10–11
content_html: God's wisdom covers all of creation and protects those who follow him.

title: Psalms 36:12
content_html: The psalmist is sure that the chaos caused by the wicked (Psalm 36:1–4) will stop when they die.

title: Psalms 37:1
content_html: envy: The jealous desire to have what others enjoy leads to further sin (Psalm 73:3; Proverbs 3:31; James 1:14–15; 4:1–2).

title: Psalms 37:1–9
content_html:
The wise respond to evil by trusting in the Lord. Trust has five parts:

  1. Do not become irritated or jealous of those who do evil (Psalm 37:1–2).

  2. Delight in the Lord (Psalm 37:3–4).

  3. Submit to the Lord (Psalm 37:5–6).

  4. Practice patience in hope (Psalm 37:7).

  5. Avoid anger (Psalm 37:8–9).

title: Psalms 37:1–40
content_html:
This psalm addresses the issues raised in Psalm 36:

  • How should the godly react to evil?

  • When will the Lord bring justice?

  • Why does he allow the wicked to harm his creation?

Psalm 37 provides clear answers that reduce the tensions of life (Psalm 37:1–9, 34) and promise that the meek will have a future (Psalm 37:10–11, 39–40).

The contrast between the godly and the wicked highlights the uselessness of foolishness (Psalm 37:12–24, 27–33). The psalmist's personal insights add a human element (Psalm 37:25–26, 35–38). He encourages viewing the land's inheritance with a long-term perspective: The earth belongs to the Lord, who will give it to those he blesses—the humble and godly who trust, hope, and follow him (see Psalm 37:9, 11, 22, 29, 34; see also Isaiah 57:13; Matthew 5:5).

title: Psalms 37:2
content_html: Human life is limited and does not last long (see Psalms 90:5–6; 92:7; 102:4, 11; 103:15; 129:6; Isaiah 40:7–8).

title: Psalms 37:3
content_html: Doing good means developing a commitment to wisdom (see Psalm 34:14; Proverbs 3:5–7).

title: Psalms 37:3–4
content_html: Trusting in the Lord helps reduce irritability and envy (Psalm 37:1).

title: Psalms 37:4
content_html: To delight in the Lord means to follow the Lord’s ways and find joy in him.

title: Psalms 37:6
content_html: Justice occurs when the Lord’s will triumphs and the godly experience his rescue (Isaiah 45:8; 51:5–6; 58:8, 10–11).

title: Psalms 37:7
content_html: Be still: We should let the Lord be God rather than taking matters into our own hands.

title: Psalms 37:8
content_html:
Irritability and anger can quickly damage a person’s character.

When someone becomes easily upset or angry, it affects the way that person thinks and acts. Over time, this can weaken good habits and harm relationships.

Learning patience and self-control helps protect and strengthen character.

title: Psalms 37:9–11
content_html: The wicked will not possess the land; those who trust in the Lord will. The Lord promises an inheritance because he is committed to caring for his people.

title: Psalms 37:11
content_html: The Lord creates peace (Psalm 85:10)—he brings peace to a chaotic world (Psalm 29:11; 37:11; 85:8; 147:14). His wisdom leads the meek to peace (Psalm 119:165; Matthew 5:5).

title: Psalms 37:12–13
content_html: The wicked show their envy by gnashing their teeth (an expression of anger and hostility). They make plans to harm the godly. But the Lord laughs at them because he knows they will fail (Psalm 2:4).

title: Psalms 37:12–17
content_html: The wicked seek to destroy the Lord’s order, but he defeats them.

title: Psalms 37:14–15
content_html: The wicked prepare weapons, but those weapons will turn against them (compare Psalm 7:12–13).

title: Psalms 37:16
content_html: It is better to be wise with little money than to be rich and evil (Proverbs 15:16; 16:8, 19; 17:1; 28:6).

title: Psalms 37:18–20
content_html: The Lord cares for the blameless in this life and for eternity, but the wicked will perish.

title: Psalms 37:20
content_html: Smoke represents the temporary nature of life (Psalm 68:2; 102:3).

title: Psalms 37:21
content_html:
• To borrow and never repay shows a dishonest way of life.

  • Generous givers freely share what they freely receive from the Lord (see Psalm 111:5; 112:5).

title: Psalms 37:23–24
content_html: The honesty and strength of a godly person's life come from the Lord (see Proverbs 4:12; 14:15; 16:9; 20:24).

title: Psalms 37:25–26
content_html: The Lord does not abandon his children or their descendants.

title: Psalms 37:27–33
content_html: The godly are wise; they do good and reject evil. The Lord cares for them, protecting them and ensuring that they receive their rightful portion of the land as their inheritance. The wicked might try to ambush the godly, but they will not succeed.

title: Psalms 37:30
content_html: The righteous base their counsel on the Lord’s wisdom.

title: Psalms 37:31
content_html: The law of his God is in his heart: The godly accept God’s instruction and live by it (see Psalm 119:11; Jeremiah 24:7; 31:31–34).

title: Psalms 37:35–36
content_html: The wicked might appear to be flourishing like a tree (compare Psalm 52:8; 92:12–13), but they eventually die and are forgotten (Psalm 37:2).

title: Psalms 37:39–40
content_html: The Lord provides strength for his people.

title: Psalms 38:1
content_html: The meaning of this phrase is uncertain (also in 70:1).

title: Psalms 38:1–4
content_html: The psalmist’s sins brought the Lord’s anger and rage. Because of this, the Lord disciplined and rebuked him, like arrows and blows striking him. As a result, his whole body is sick.

title: Psalms 38:1–17
content_html: These verses introduce the psalmist's confession (Psalm 38:18) and describe his miserable state.

title: Psalms 38:1–22
content_html: This psalm is a lament and a prayer for healing. The psalmist's suffering is linked to his unconfessed sin. He admits his sin and trusts the Lord with his situation.

title: Psalms 38:3
content_html: Sin can lead to sickness and even death (1 Corinthians 11:30). The psalmist may have felt physically ill, or his sickness was a metaphor for emotional distress. He understood it came from God and endangered his life (see Psalms 32:3; 39:10).

title: Psalms 38:4
content_html: The psalmist feels burdened because of sin (see Psalms 40:12; 41:4; compare Genesis 4:13).

title: Psalms 38:5–8
content_html: The severity of God’s punishment brings anguish that affects every part of the psalmist’s being.

title: Psalms 38:9–12
content_html: The psalmist wants to reconnect with God but feels distant. He feels lost and alone as his friends leave him.

title: Psalms 38:13
content_html: The psalmist suffers quietly before his opponents (see Isaiah 53:7).

title: Psalms 38:15
content_html: The Lord alone will resolve the conflict (Psalms 9:18; 27:14; 37:9, 34).

title: Psalms 38:17–20
content_html: The psalmist, unable to bear his suffering and close to collapse (compare Psalm 15:5), admits his sin (see Psalm 32:5).

title: Psalms 38:19
content_html: The psalmist feels more pain because his enemies hate him for no reason.

title: Psalms 38:22
content_html: In the Psalms, people often ask the Lord to act quickly in times of great need (Psalms 22:19; 31:2; 40:13; 69:17; 70:1; 71:12; 79:8; 102:2; 141:1; 143:7). However, wisdom and experience show that God's people often need to wait for him to act (Psalms 27:14; 37:7; Isaiah 8:17; 40:31).

title: Psalms 39:1
content_html: Jeduthun was a Levitical singer who was appointed by David, along with Asaph (1 Chronicles 9:16; 16:38; 25:1).

title: Psalms 39:1–3
content_html: Suffering in silence makes the psalmist's anguish, pain, and inner distress worse.

title: Psalms 39:1–13
content_html: This prayer for rescue shows the psalmist's discouragement, which comes from not fully understanding his situation.

title: Psalms 39:4–5
content_html: how fleeting my life is: This perspective comes out of suffering (see Psalms 78:39; 89:47–48; 90:3–10). The human lifetime is but a moment to God (Psalm 90:4), as temporary as a breath (see Psalms 39:11; 144:4).

title: Psalms 39:4–6
content_html: Due to his suffering and sin, the psalmist concludes that life is meaningless and short (see Job 7:7–10; Isaiah 40:6–8). He had expected only a wicked life to be like that (see Psalms 37:2, 20; 103:15–16), but now it seems irrelevant whether a person is godly or not.

title: Psalms 39:8
content_html: The idea that rebellion leads to suffering is a common theme in Psalms 32, 38–41.

title: Psalms 39:9–11
content_html: The psalmist links suffering with God’s discipline, assuming that it is punishment for his sin.

title: Psalms 39:11
content_html: The Lord uses discipline to correct his children (Psalms 38:1, 3, 7; 40:12; see Proverbs 3:11–12; Hebrews 12:5–6).

title: Psalms 39:12
content_html: A foreigner could not own land (see Leviticus 25:23). The patriarchs had lived as guests in Canaan (Psalm 105:23).

title: Psalms 39:13
content_html: Earlier, the psalmist asked God to stay close to him (Psalm 8:21–22). Now, he wants God to leave him alone. This is similar to how Job felt (Job 7:17–19).

title: Psalms 40:1–5
content_html: The psalmist thanks the Lord for rescue and affirms that people can trust God.

title: Psalms 40:1–17
content_html: This psalm has a thanksgiving song (Psalm 40:1–10) and a lament (40:11–17). The thanksgiving song explains why the psalmist is thankful (Psalm 40:1–5), shows a promise to stay committed (40:6–8), and shares a public testimony about God's character (40:7–10). The lament talks about the psalmist's troubles due to his sins (Psalm 40:11–12) and includes two prayers asking for justice (40:13–15, 17).

title: Psalms 40:6
content_html:
• The Lord delights in the attitude and character of the person giving an offering more than the offering itself.

  • my ears You have opened: The Greek text reads You have given me a body. In Hebrews 10:5–7, the writer quotes from the Greek version of Psalm 40:6–8. He presents these words as something Jesus said when he left heaven and came into the world.

title: Psalms 40:7
content_html: it is written about me in the scroll: Moses had recorded God’s requirements for Israel’s kings in Deuteronomy 17:14–15.

title: Psalms 40:11
content_html: The psalmist prays that the Lord will be true to his character when answering this plea for mercy.

title: Psalms 40:12–17
content_html:
• The psalmist is troubled by both his adversities and his own sins and prays for rescue.

  • This passage is almost the same as Psalm 70.

title: Psalms 41:1
content_html: The poor who suffer hardship have special protection under God's law (Leviticus 14:21; 19:10). They receive God's justice and guidance (Psalms 72:13; 82:3–4). The word translated as poor often means the godly (Psalm 113:7; see Zephaniah 3:12).

title: Psalms 41:1–13
content_html:
In this wisdom psalm, the psalmist:

  • asks for healing (Psalm 41:4, 10),

  • laments his distress (Psalm 41:5–9), and

  • expresses confidence in the Lord's blessing (Psalm 41:1–3, 11–12).

title: Psalms 41:3
content_html: on his bed of illness: This illness might be physical or spiritual (see Psalm 6:2; Psalm 38).

title: Psalms 41:4
content_html: The psalmist applies the blessing to himself (Psalm 41:12). He has helped the poor (Psalm 41:1), but he has sinned against the Lord in other ways. In his pain, he asks for healing and admits his sin. Only the Lord can provide the restoration that brings true joy (Psalm 30:2; 107:20–21).

title: Psalms 41:5
content_html: When will he die: The psalmist's enemies see his suffering as a judgment from God (see Psalm 1:6).

title: Psalms 41:5–9
content_html: The psalmist’s enemies disguise themselves as friends, while his friends openly become his enemies.

title: Psalms 41:9
content_html: the one who shared my bread: Eating together was a sign of unity (Acts 2:42). Refusing to eat with someone was a sign of hostility (1 Corinthians 5:11). Jesus experienced the same kind of betrayal (John 13:1, 18).

title: Psalms 41:11–12
content_html: The psalmist knows that the Lord has forgiven him and will bless him.

title: Psalms 41:13
content_html: This doxology (song of praise) ends Book One (Psalms 1–41). Compare with Psalm 106:48.

title: Psalms 42:1
content_html: The sons of Korah were members of a Levitical choir. They were chosen by David to serve as singers in the temple (see 1 Chronicles 6:22–38; 9:19–34; 2 Chronicles 20:19). Their ancestor Korah was the rebel who opposed Moses and was swallowed by the earth (Exodus 6:24; Numbers 16).

title: Psalms 42:1–4
content_html:
• The psalmist's deep desire for God arises from being far away from the temple as he remembers the past.

  • The verb translated as "longs" appears only here and in Joel 1:20 (meaning "cry out"). In both instances, it describes extreme thirst in a dry desert (see Psalms 63:1; 143:6; see also 84:2).

title: Psalms 42:1–43.5
content_html:
These psalms, like Psalms 9 and 10, form a unit with a lament and a bittersweet refrain of hope (Psalm 42:5, 11; 43:5). Many Hebrew manuscripts combine these two psalms into one.

The historical context is unclear; these psalms might represent God's people in exile, expressing loyalty to God while he punishes them for their ongoing disobedience.

The psalmist longs for the fellowship with God he had in Jerusalem (Psalm 42:4); his memories only deepen his discouragement. As the psalmist cries out to God, he remembers God's love and faithfulness (Psalm 42:8), which leads him to ask for vindication (43:1–4).

title: Psalms 42:3
content_html:
• Crying so much that tears become food shows deep sadness (Psalms 80:5; 102:9; see 6:6; 56:8; 119:136; 126:5).

  • The wicked try to impose their timing on God and deny his power to intervene.

title: Psalms 42:4
content_html: The psalmist remembers leading a large procession as part of the Levitical choir, possibly during the important holy days.

title: Psalms 42:5
content_html: The psalmist feels sad, but he can still function. He remembers that the living God is his Savior. This verse appears again in Psalms 42:11 and 43:5.

title: Psalms 42:5–6
content_html: The psalmist encourages himself with questions and answers.

title: Psalms 42:6
content_html:
• Mount Hermon is a high mountain range northeast of Israel. The source of the Jordan is by Dan at the foot of Mount Hermon.

  • Mount Mizar might be a peak in the Mount Hermon range.

title: Psalms 42:7
content_html: The raging seas, waves, and surging tides bring to mind God's anger (see Psalm 88:7).

title: Psalms 42:8
content_html: The psalmist sees rays of hope. A life focused on God includes joyful songs and prayers for justice and vindication (Psalm 42:9–10; 43:1–4).

title: Psalms 42:9–10
content_html: The psalmist brings his many doubts, discouragements, and questions directly to God.

title: Psalms 43:1–4
content_html: The psalmist turns to the Lord for rescue from his troubles, for guidance, for restoration, and for vindication.

title: Psalms 43:2
content_html: rejected me: Other psalms also ask if God has rejected his people (Psalm 44:9, 23; 60:1, 10; 74:1; 77:7; 88:14; 89:38; 108:11).

title: Psalms 43:3
content_html:
• This prayer for God's redemption sees light and truth as guides (see Psalms 18:25–29; 27:1; 85:10–13; 89:14–18; see also 119:105; John 3:19; Ephesians 5:8; 1 Thessalonians 5:5–7).

  • The temple on the mountain in Jerusalem symbolized God's holy presence among his people (Psalms 15:1; 43:3; 99:5, 9; 132:7).

title: Psalms 43:5
content_html: This verse is repeated in Psalm 42:5 and 11.

title: Psalms 44:1
content_html: We have heard … the work You did: The story of redemption includes God's favor and power in his past acts of rescue (see Psalm 78:1–4; see also Psalms 105–106).

title: Psalms 44:1–26
content_html:
This national lament after a defeat in battle continues the tone of the previous two psalms. It reflects on an unspecified moment in Israel's history and calls on God for salvation. The people:

  • recite God's past acts of rescue (Psalm 44:1–3),

  • recognize God's power to save (Psalm 44:4–8),

  • describe their humiliation in exile (Psalm 44:9–16),

  • claim their innocence and lament the injustice of their current situation (Psalm 44:17–22), and

  • cry for vindication (Psalm 44:23–26).

title: Psalms 44:2
content_html: God drove out the pagan nations during the conquests recorded in Joshua.

title: Psalms 44:3
content_html: Though the Israelites used swords and other weapons (Psalm 44:6), they would have lost their battles without the light of the Lord’s favor (see Psalms 18:25–29; 27:1; 89:15).

title: Psalms 44:4–8
content_html: God’s people feel confident about his mighty acts; he was with them in the past, and he is their king in the present.

title: Psalms 44:9–16
content_html: The people have experienced defeat (Psalm 44:9–12), so they understand suffering and shame (44:13–16).

title: Psalms 44:11
content_html: scattered us among the nations: This likely refers to the exile (see Psalms 51:18–19; 89:38–51; 106:40–47; 137).

title: Psalms 44:22
content_html: Paul quotes this verse as a parenthetical aside in his list of things that cannot separate us from Christ’s love (Romans 8:36).

title: Psalms 44:23
content_html: Wake up: Biblical authors often call for God’s attention in a crisis (Psalms 35:23; 44:23; 73:20; 80:2; Mark 4:38).

title: Psalms 44:23–26
content_html: In the end, God’s people can turn to the Lord and trust him for rescue. Their petition for God to remedy their situation is as strong as their lament about God’s rejection.

title: Psalms 45:1
content_html: To the tune of "The Lilies" is no longer known (see also Psalm 69).

title: Psalms 45:1–17
content_html: This psalm celebrates a royal wedding; it might have been sung on many occasions and not just at one wedding. As God’s representative, the king carried the responsibility of dispensing justice and maintaining order in God’s world (see Psalm 2).

title: Psalms 45:2
content_html: This general description of the king’s symbolic status anticipates his enduring legacy (Psalm 45:16). The Lord’s blessing and the charge to reflect God’s attributes are symbolized by his handsome appearance and gracious speech.

title: Psalms 45:3
content_html: Glory and majesty belong to the Lord (Psalms 96:6; 111:3).

title: Psalms 45:3–5
content_html: The psalmist describes the royal splendor the king showed when fighting his enemies.

title: Psalms 45:4
content_html: As God's representative of truth, humility, and justice (see Psalm 72:1–2, 4, 7), the king can perform impressive deeds (65:5; 96:4).

title: Psalms 45:6
content_html: Justice characterizes God’s rule, as it should characterize the king’s administration.

title: Psalms 45:6–9
content_html:
• The king is like a god among humans. God anointed him, gave him a powerful throne, and he delivers justice as he rules eternally.

  • The writer of Hebrews uses Psalm 45:6–7 to describe Jesus, arguing that the Son is greater than the angels (Hebrews 1:8–9).

title: Psalms 45:8
content_html:
• Fine spices mixed with oil create unique perfumes.

  • The king's palaces had wood panels with ivory inlays (see Psalm 6:4; Amos 3:15).

title: Psalms 45:9
content_html: Royal marriages often confirmed diplomatic alliances between kings, and the women became part of the royal harem (see 1 Kings 11:1–3).

title: Psalms 45:10–12
content_html: The bride is a representative of the king. Powerful people and nations try to gain her favor in order to get close to the king. This is a great honor, and she is encouraged to give herself fully to her new position.

title: Psalms 45:12
content_html: The Daughter of Tyre: Israel traded with Tyre, a powerful city-state. The city's most noble woman brings a wedding gift to the new queen, who represents an honored kingdom.

title: Psalms 46:1
content_html: According to Alamoth (“maidens”; see also study note on 1 Chronicles 15:20). In worship, a choir or a group of instrumentalists performed this hymn.

title: Psalms 46:1–3
content_html: Even during natural disasters, the citizens of Zion show no fear.

title: Psalms 46:1–11
content_html: This psalm of Zion celebrates God's special presence in Jerusalem (see also Psalms 48, 76, 87, 122). It encourages trust in the Lord because the people of Zion have his protection.

title: Psalms 46:4
content_html: This river provides life, renewal, and healing like the streams flowing through Eden (Psalm 36:8–9; see Genesis 2:10; Ezekiel 47; Revelation 22:2).

title: Psalms 46:4–6
content_html: The strong city, where God lives, protects its people. They enjoy the river flowing within the city. This river is calm, unlike the turbulent waters (representing the powers of the nations) outside the city.

title: Psalms 46:5
content_html: when morning dawns: In the ancient Near East, enemies attacked cities during the day. Even in dangerous times, the Lord will protect the place where his name is.

title: Psalms 46:6
content_html: People and nations live in chaos. They take every chance to benefit themselves (Psalm 83:2), stay busy with many things (39:6), and fill the world with noise. They are unstable and can fall apart, unlike the city of God (compare Psalm 15:5). But when the godly are troubled, they cry out to God for help (Psalm 42:5, 11; 43:5; 55:17).

title: Psalms 46:7
content_html:
• This refrain reminds us who the God of Zion is.

  • with us: Hebrew 'immanu, part of the name Immanuel, meaning “God with us” (Isaiah 7:14; 8:8; Matthew 1:23).

title: Psalms 46:8
content_html: Israel witnessed the Lord's amazing works in the wilderness (Psalms 107:24; 111:2), but the wicked, who do not care about them (28:5), end up desolate (73:19).

title: Psalms 46:8–9
content_html: This invitation to trust in the Lord offers a perspective on God’s interactions with human powers.

title: Psalms 46:10
content_html:
• A message directly from the Lord confirms the truth of the psalmist’s vision (see also Psalms 12:5–6; 32:8).

  • know that I am God: The Lord will fulfill his promises (Isaiah 45:3; 49:23; 60:16).

  • God will be honored (Psalm 47; Isaiah 6:1); he will judge the nations and save his people (see Psalms 57:11; 89:13; 108:5–6; 118:16).

title: Psalms 47:1
content_html: People clap their hands in praise and recognition of wonderful deeds (Psalm 47:3–4; see 98:8; Isaiah 55:12; compare Nahum 3:19).

title: Psalms 47:1–2
content_html: As the ruler of all nations, God deserves praise.

title: Psalms 47:1–9
content_html: This psalm praises God's rule as king over the entire earth. It describes God's victory with Israel and other nations. Both Israel (Psalm 47:3–4) and the nations (47:9) are part of God's kingdom.

title: Psalms 47:3–4
content_html: The Lord gave Israel victory, especially under David (see Psalm 18:40–50). God's powerful acts of redemption show his love for Israel.

title: Psalms 47:4
content_html:
• pride of Jacob: Jacob was the father of all Israelites (Genesis 32:28). The name Jacob often represents the nation of Israel.

  • God loves and commits himself to Jacob (see Psalms 78:68; 127:2; see Romans 11:28).

title: Psalms 47:5
content_html: horn: Or rams’ horns (see Psalm 81:3). People sometimes blew rams’ horns during celebrations (Psalm 98:5–6; 2 Kings 11:14; 1 Chronicles 15:28).

title: Psalms 47:5–6
content_html: The psalmist describes the Lord as returning to heaven, having accomplished his victories. For a description of his descent, see Psalm 18:9–16.

title: Psalms 47:7
content_html: People from all over the world (see Psalm 96:1–3) will worship God.

title: Psalms 48:1–14
content_html: This song of Zion (see also Psalms 46, 76, 87, 122) clearly describes Jerusalem (Zion) as the city of the Great King, where the godly find protection (48:1–3). God rules from Zion with faithfulness, righteousness, and justice, inspiring his people with confidence and joy (Psalm 48:9–11). They dedicate themselves to witnessing Zion's glory so they can share it with the next generation (Psalm 48:8, 12–14).

title: Psalms 48:3
content_html: God lives in Zion to protect it from all enemies (see Psalm 46:5).

title: Psalms 48:7
content_html: The ships of Tarshish were built for long voyages and represent human achievement (see also 2 Chronicles 9:21; Isaiah 2:16). Their destruction might be the event mentioned in 1 Kings 22:48.

title: Psalms 48:9–11
content_html: The psalmist happily celebrates the Lord's presence with the community, thinking about God's faithful, righteous, and just rule.

title: Psalms 48:12–13
content_html: towers … ramparts … citadel: These structures show Zion's strength.

title: Psalms 48:14
content_html: No one is like God (Psalms 35:10; 71:19; 86:8; 89:6, 8; 113:5; see Isaiah 40:18, 25). He is the shepherd King (Psalm 95:7) who will guide (see 5:8; 23:2) the sheep of his flock (77:20).

title: Psalms 49:1–2
content_html: Anyone who will listen can gain wisdom (Proverbs 1:5, 8, 33).

title: Psalms 49:1–4
content_html: The wisdom teacher invites everyone to come and learn wisdom. He promises to teach them through a proverb and a riddle.

title: Psalms 49:1–20
content_html: In this wisdom psalm, a wise teacher warns about enemies who live richly and care only for themselves (Psalm 49:5–9). They cannot save anyone, not even themselves, from death or being forgotten (Psalm 49:10–12). Like fattened animals, they are led to slaughter (Psalm 49:14–20).

title: Psalms 49:4
content_html: The teacher used proverbs and riddles to teach wisdom (see Proverbs 1:5–6).

title: Psalms 49:5–8
content_html: If enemies who live for material rewards cannot sustain themselves, how can they give life to others?

title: Psalms 49:11–12
content_html: The teacher uses irony to teach wisdom. Everyone dies, and even those who give their names to lands or estates will be forgotten.

title: Psalms 49:13–15
content_html: The teachers of false wisdom cannot rescue themselves from death. Only God has that power.

title: Psalms 49:16–20
content_html: The wicked might have rich, luxurious lives, but they forget that they cannot avoid death.

title: Psalms 50:1–3
content_html: The whole universe must pay attention when God approaches to bring justice into the world.

title: Psalms 50:1–23
content_html: God appears as judge. He reminds both the godly and the ungodly that sacrifices and reciting the law are worthless without thankfulness, repentance, and justice.

title: Psalms 50:4–6
content_html: The Lord is coming to bring justice. This hope encourages his faithful people. However, those who have been unfaithful will not receive blessings when the heavens and earth testify against them (see Deuteronomy 30:19; 31:28; 32:1; Isaiah 1:2). The Lord comes to restore order to the world (see Psalms 5:8; 22:31; 33:5; 40:9; 50:6; 71:16; 97:6).

title: Psalms 50:7–13
content_html: The psalmist warns against valuing sacrificial rituals more than having the right attitude in your heart.

title: Psalms 50:9
content_html: Unlike pagan gods, God does not need food. Instead, he wants his people to serve him with sincere hearts (Psalm 50:14–15).

title: Psalms 50:10
content_html: the cattle on a thousand hills: God has no needs because he owns everything.

title: Psalms 50:14
content_html:
• Thankfulness involves recognizing and appreciating God's blessings beyond personal gain (see Psalms 51:15–17; 54:6).

  • True sacrifice means dedicating oneself to honor God (Psalm 50:23; Romans 12:1–2).

title: Psalms 50:15
content_html: God’s response to his people’s cry is to rescue them.

title: Psalms 50:16–21
content_html: This message is against the wicked, who only pretend to listen to God.

title: Psalms 50:17
content_html: My instruction … My words: The wicked may observe rituals, but they reject more important matters such as justice, mercy, and faithfulness (compare Matthew 23:23–24).

title: Psalms 50:21
content_html: Because he is patient, God remained silent.

title: Psalms 50:22
content_html: God will destroy the wicked like a lion tearing its prey (Isaiah 5:29; 31:4; Ezekiel 19:3; compare Psalms 7:2; 22:13).

title: Psalms 50:22–23
content_html: The wicked will experience God’s justice, while the godly will see God’s everlasting salvation.

title: Psalms 51:1
content_html: A prayer for God's mercy and kindness is a type of lament. Sometimes, the psalmist asks for mercy because he recognizes his own weakness and sin (see also Psalm 6:2; 9:13; 25:16; 31:9; 41:4, 10). Other times, the plea comes from the psalmist's dedication to the Lord (see Psalm 26:11; 27:7; 119:29, 58, 132). In every situation, God's mercy leads to inner renewal.

title: Psalms 51:1–2
content_html: The person who regrets their wrongdoing cannot offer anything to God. They need God's favor, mercy, forgiveness, and blessing to experience renewal and restoration.

title: Psalms 51:1–19
content_html: This heartfelt prayer for restoration seeks God's favor, mercy, forgiveness, and cleansing. With a broken spirit, the psalmist admits his sin and takes responsibility (Psalm 51:3–6). He then asks God to remove his guilt and renew him internally (Psalm 51:7–12). The psalmist commits again to living wisely and joyfully while serving God and others (Psalm 51:13–17).

title: Psalms 51:title
content_html: See 2 Samuel 11:1–27. The psalm does not directly mention this event.

title: Psalms 51:1–65.13
content_html: These psalms of David share a common theme. They reflect on the experience of evil. In Psalm 51, the psalmist admits his wrongdoing and asks for God's forgiveness. Psalms 52–64 express sorrow over specific evils David faced. A song of praise (Psalm 65) brings David's laments to an end.

title: Psalms 51:2
content_html: Wash me … cleanse me: The psalmist asks for cleansing and freedom from the guilt of his sin (Psalm 51:7; see 26:6; Isaiah 1:16, 18; 4:4).

title: Psalms 51:3–6
content_html: The psalmist accepts the results of his sin against God. He understands that he has no goodness without God's renewal.

title: Psalms 51:4
content_html: Against You, You only: This is an exaggeration. The psalmist knows he also sinned against people (2 Samuel 11:2–4, 15–17), but his sin against God is more important. He expects God to rebuke him and will accept whatever verdict God gives (see Romans 3:4).

title: Psalms 51:5
content_html: I was brought forth in iniquity: The psalmist is not making excuses but is admitting how deep his sinfulness is. From the time we are conceived, we all share in the human condition of sinfulness.

title: Psalms 51:7
content_html: Purify me with hyssop (literally Purify me with the hyssop branch; see Exodus 12:22; Leviticus 14:4; John 19:29; Hebrews 9:19): The psalmist relies on God to cleanse and renew him (see Isaiah 1:18).

title: Psalms 51:8
content_html:
• Regaining joy is a gracious gift from God.

  • You have crushed: The psalmist feels completely broken (Psalm 6:2; compare 34:20).

title: Psalms 51:10
content_html: A loyal spirit produces inner strength. It helps a person remain faithful and continue even during difficulty.

title: Psalms 51:10–12
content_html: God’s Spirit renews and recreates the human spirit.

title: Psalms 51:11
content_html: Your Holy Spirit: Only the Holy Spirit can change a person's will to make it "right" (Psalm 51:10) and "willing" to obey (51:12).

title: Psalms 51:13
content_html: As he learns from his sin and its consequences, the psalmist wants to teach others.

title: Psalms 51:14
content_html: bloodguilt: This expression refers to any injustice, not only killing someone (Psalm 9:12; 58:10; see 2 Samuel 11:14–26; compare Isaiah 1:15–17).

title: Psalms 51:18–19
content_html: build up the walls of Jerusalem: These verses were likely added as a prayer for national restoration after the exile (see Psalms 42–43). The entire community in exile shared the feelings expressed in this psalm.

title: Psalms 51:19
content_html: Sacrifices made with the right intentions come from a heart that is right with God and others (see Psalms 15:2–5; 24:3–6; 50:14; Matthew 5:23–24).

title: Psalms 52:1
content_html: The accuser, Doeg the Edomite, is an example of a person who chose to do evil.

title: Psalms 52:1–4
content_html: Liars deceive themselves by thinking they can be heroes with their words. They see themselves as innocent, even though they love evil and cause harm. Those who speak curses, sarcasm, bitterness, arrogant boasting, and lies (Psalms 31:18; 34:13; 59:7, 12; 120:2–3; 140:3, 9) create chaos, isolation, and destruction (James 3:5). Their sharp words cut like swords and daggers (Psalms 55:21; 57:4; 64:3), the fangs of a snake (140:3), or a razor.

title: Psalms 52:1–9
content_html: Wicked people use words to destroy relationships and communities. They foolishly think that they can be heroes by their words (Psalm 52:1–4), but their downfall comes suddenly (52:5–7). In contrast, the godly renew their commitment to the Lord (Psalm 52:8–9) and find security in their future with him.

title: Psalms 52:3
content_html: Dishonest speech can change a person's character.

title: Psalms 52:5
content_html: God will strike the wicked down like a pagan altar, idol, or unclean vessel (Exodus 34:13; Leviticus 11:35; 2 Kings 10:27).

title: Psalms 52:5–7
content_html: God’s judgment on the wicked will come suddenly and forcibly. The godly rejoice in God’s justice, not in the misfortunes of the wicked.

title: Psalms 52:6
content_html: see and fear: In Hebrew, these words sound almost alike.

title: Psalms 52:8–9
content_html:
• The psalmist commits himself to a life of trust in the Lord.

  • An olive tree can live a long time with proper care (Psalms 1:3; 128:3). The psalmist wants to be near the Lord, who is his Savior and the source of his blessings (Psalms 18:6; 23:6).

title: Psalms 53:1–3
content_html: Paul uses the Greek translation of this passage to describe the moral corruption of all people (Romans 3:10–12).

title: Psalms 53:1–6
content_html: This psalm is similar to Psalm 14, with differences only in verse 5.

title: Psalms 53:5
content_html: scattered the bones: Israel's enemies would face a shameful defeat and would not have the peaceful rest of a proper burial (compare Psalm 141:7; 2 Kings 9:10; Jeremiah 8:1–2; Ezekiel 6:5).

title: Psalms 54:1
content_html: The Ziphites intended to use violence.

title: Psalms 54:1–7
content_html: This personal lament was caused by violent and godless people (Psalm 54:3). The psalmist prays to God and focuses on God's help (54:4). He asks God to save him from evil and judge his enemies (Psalm 54:5, 7).

title: Psalms 54:3
content_html:
• The strangers were excluded from the covenant community (see Psalm 109:11; Proverbs 5:10).

  • Proud and violent people do not respect God or others (see Psalms 35:11, 25; 86:14; Proverbs 11:16).

title: Psalms 54:6
content_html: A voluntary offering is a sacrifice of praise. Sacrifices of praise are the kind of offering God most desires (see Psalm 50:14–15, 23).

title: Psalms 54:6–7
content_html: In anticipation of God’s response, the mood of the psalm changes from sorrow to thanksgiving.

title: Psalms 55:1–3
content_html: The occasion for this prayer is an attack by an enemy.

title: Psalms 55:1–23
content_html:
The psalmist faces enemies led by a former friend (Psalm 55:12–14). This causes him great distress. He prays to the Lord and trusts him for justice (Psalm 55:22–23).

Jesus went through similar pain when Judas, his friend, betrayed him. He also experienced the psalmist’s prayer being answered:

  1. by taking the place of the wicked and receiving their punishment (Isaiah 53:7–9), and

  2. by trusting the Father, who saved (Luke 23:46) and rewarded him (Isaiah 53:10–12).

title: Psalms 55:4–8
content_html: In the experience of deep anguish, the psalmist confesses that his adversity has become unbearable and he seeks an escape.

title: Psalms 55:12–20
content_html: The psalmist's enemy used to be a friend. Remembering their close relationship causes him pain (see Psalm 31:11).

title: Psalms 55:19–21
content_html: God is good and faithful, but the psalmist’s enemies and so-called friend are deceitful and wicked.

title: Psalms 55:22–23
content_html: The psalmist commits all his cares to the Lord.

title: Psalms 55:23
content_html: To die young means missing out on God's full blessings (compare Proverbs 3:2, 16).

title: Psalms 56:1
content_html:
• A Miktam: This may be a literary or musical term.

  • David was seized by Philistines concerned about his loyalties and brought before King Achish (1 Samuel 21:10–15).

title: Psalms 56:1–13
content_html: In this lament, the psalmist first asks for help and then expresses confidence in God. This pattern occurs two times (Psalm 56:1–4, 5–13).

title: Psalms 56:4
content_html: man: Or mere flesh, in contrast to God, who is immortal and much more powerful (see Isaiah 2:22).

title: Psalms 56:5–7
content_html: The psalmist specifies his charge against his attackers as he prays for their evil to end.

title: Psalms 56:8
content_html: The psalmist uses wordplay with two similar Hebrew words: wanderings (nod) and bottle (no’d).

title: Psalms 56:11
content_html:
man (Hebrew ’adam): The psalm also uses two other words for man:

  • ’enosh (meaning “people,” Psalm 56:1) and

  • basar (meaning “mere mortals,” Psalm 56:4).

All these words point to the short and limited nature of human life.

title: Psalms 56:13
content_html: The light of God’s goodness and salvation will replace the darkness of death (see Psalms 18:25–29; 27:1).

title: Psalms 57:1
content_html: shadow of Your wings: Like a bird protects its young, God protects those who trust in him (see Psalm 17:8).

title: Psalms 57:1–11
content_html: The psalmist asks for mercy and protection, then expresses trust and confidence in the Lord (Psalm 57:1–4). When the wicked fall into their own traps (Psalm 57:6), the psalmist will praise the Lord (57:7–10).

title: Psalms 57:title
content_html: See 1 Samuel 22:1 or Psalm 24:1–8.

title: Psalms 57:2
content_html:
• God Most High (Hebrew Elohim-Elyon) is a common title for God. He is more powerful than all real or imagined supernatural beings (see Psalms 91:1; 97:9; Genesis 14:18–22).

  • who fulfills His purpose for me: Or who will end my [trouble], or who deals kindly with me.

title: Psalms 57:3–4
content_html: These verses are a prayer for rescue from strong enemies. The metaphor of fierce lions describes human foes. The psalmist feels unable to escape them without God's help (see also Psalms 35:11–21; 124:6).

title: Psalms 57:5
content_html:
• This verse appears again in Psalm 57:11 and 108:5.

  • God's glory, which is his visible presence that keeps out all evil (Psalm 8:1; 19:1–4; 24:7), will fill the entire creation (Isaiah 6:3).

title: Psalms 57:7–8
content_html: The psalmist is sure that God will stop the night of evil (compare Psalm 56:13).

title: Psalms 57:7–11
content_html: This section is repeated with a few changes in Psalm 108:1–5.

title: Psalms 57:10–11
content_html: God's glory reaches the entire universe, and people will praise his constant love and faithfulness.

title: Psalms 58:1
content_html: rulers (or gods): People used this term for high officials and administrators of justice (Psalm 82:1, 6). In other contexts, it might refer to angelic creatures (Psalm 89:7) or the gods of other nations (82:1; Daniel 11:36).

title: Psalms 58:1–5
content_html: The administrators of justice were furthering their own interests rather than those of the needy. The absence of justice gradually resulted in a violent society.

title: Psalms 58:1–11
content_html: The administration of justice is a mark of good government. God expects nothing less from his people. The psalmist condemns Israel’s leaders for abusing their power, and he calls on the Lord to rain down his curses upon the wicked. The demonstration of divine justice will reassure the godly that God is just.

title: Psalms 58:3
content_html: All humans are born with a tendency to sin (see Psalm 51:5). However, the wicked follow their sinful desires, while the godly struggle against them (Romans 7:19–23; James 4:1–10).

title: Psalms 58:4
content_html: Venom here means harmful speech (see Psalm 140:3).

title: Psalms 58:6
content_html: The psalmist calls on God to break the destructive power of the wicked.

title: Psalms 58:8
content_html: When moving over dry ground, slugs dry up.

title: Psalms 58:9–10
content_html: God’s judgment will come suddenly. The certainty of vindication brings joy to the godly.

title: Psalms 58:11
content_html: Being shown to be right is the reward for those who live for God.

title: Psalms 59:1
content_html: See 1 Samuel 19:11–18.

title: Psalms 59:1–2
content_html: protect me (or lift me up high): When surrounded by evil, the godly pray for God to set them apart for himself and protect them.

title: Psalms 59:1–17
content_html: The psalmist laments the power of enemies who conspire with other nations.

title: Psalms 59:4–5
content_html: The psalmist asks God not to show mercy to the treacherous, deceptive, and wicked traitors (see Psalm 25:2).

title: Psalms 59:6–8
content_html: The wicked threaten like aggressive dogs, but the Lord shows that he does not fear them.

title: Psalms 59:11–13
content_html: The psalmist prays that the wicked will suffer the same pain they have caused others.

title: Psalms 59:14–16
content_html: The wicked will remain unsatisfied, but the psalmist will praise the Lord's strength and loving devotion.

title: Psalms 60:1
content_html:
• This psalm was taught to young people as part of their education.

  • When: See 2 Samuel 8:13–14.

  • Aram-naharaim and Aram-zobah refer to the Arameans of northwest Mesopotamia and Syria (see study note on Genesis 24:10).

  • The tune “Lily of the Testimony” is unknown today.

title: Psalms 60:1–4
content_html: When God’s people experience his discipline, they affirm their loyalty and await God’s answer.

title: Psalms 60:1–12
content_html: The psalmist laments a national defeat and cries out to God for rescue.

title: Psalms 60:3
content_html: we are staggered from the wine: Yayin, the Hebrew word for "wine," is often associated with drunkenness (2 Samuel 13:28; Isaiah 5:11; 28:7–8; see also Psalm 104:15; Deuteronomy 14:26; Isaiah 55:1). It symbolized God's judgment on the wicked (Psalm 75:8; Jeremiah 51:7).

title: Psalms 60:4
content_html:
• God raised a banner over his people to serve as a rallying point and as a source of pride and confidence.

  • The fear of God that leads to wisdom (Psalm 111:10) is not weakness. It gives the perspective and strength needed to do what is right.

title: Psalms 60:5
content_html: The psalmist prays for rescue based on the special relationship between God and his people.

title: Psalms 60:5–12
content_html: This section is repeated in Psalm 108:6–13.

title: Psalms 60:6
content_html:
Shechem and Succoth represent the heartland of Israel.

These places were located in the central region of the land. Because of this, they can represent the center of Israel’s life and strength.

title: Psalms 60:6–8
content_html: The Lord responds with a message of promise. He shares his land with his people as spoils of war.

title: Psalms 60:9
content_html: Destroying the capital (the fortified city) of a defeated invader would help prevent future attacks. Edom's capital was Bozrah, located to the southeast of Israel. Only God could grant victory.

title: Psalms 61:1–8
content_html: The psalmist asks God to protect him and guide him into his presence (Psalm 61:4–5). He prays for God to extend and protect the king's rule and promises to praise God.

title: Psalms 61:1–63.11
content_html: In these royal prayers, the psalmist longs for God’s presence. All three psalms concern themselves with the king’s security.

title: Psalms 61:2
content_html:
• the ends of the earth: This phrase suggests a cry that comes from a place far away from God.

  • The psalmist feels deep sorrow and distress.

title: Psalms 61:4
content_html:
• The poet desires God's presence and seeks entry into God's earthly home for protection and care. Being welcomed into God's sanctuary (tent) is the greatest blessing (see Psalms 15:1; 23:6; 24:3–6). The Hebrew word for tent refers to the tabernacle and the tent David built for the ark (1 Chronicles 15:1). See also study note on John 1:14.

  • The shelter of your wings represents God's protection and care (see Psalms 9:9; 17:8).

title: Psalms 61:5
content_html:
• You have heard my vows: The psalmist's vows might be part of ceremonial sacrifices (Psalms 66:13–15; 76:11; 116:14, 18). He praises and thanks God for rescuing him.

  • The inheritance refers to the promise of the land (see Psalm 60), the sacredness of the temple, the joy of God's presence (Psalm 119:111), or any similar reward (Psalms 16:6; 37:9, 11, 18, 22, 29, 34; 127:3).

title: Psalms 62:1
content_html: See 1 Chronicles 25:1; also in Psalms 39 and 77.

title: Psalms 62:1–12
content_html:
This psalm expresses trust in the king and includes a prayer for him. The king relies on God even during tough times.

His tricky and strong enemies challenge him, but he stays strong. He urges himself and his people to trust God.

He wants them to view their human enemies from God's perspective, where they seem weak, temporary, and deceitful (Psalm 62:9).

title: Psalms 62:3–4
content_html: The enemies want to overthrow the king using lies and tricks.

title: Psalms 62:4
content_html: his lofty perch: This means the royal throne.

title: Psalms 62:5–8
content_html: The psalmist encourages himself and others to place their hope in God.

title: Psalms 62:9
content_html: are but a lie: People often try to appear stronger and better than they truly are.

title: Psalms 62:9–12
content_html: Humans are temporary, unlike God. Even when successful, their prosperity does not last long.

title: Psalms 62:10
content_html: Extortion involves using threats to force someone to give up their belongings. Stealing means taking what belongs to someone else.

title: Psalms 62:11–12
content_html: Only God balances power and loving devotion. He cares for his people by removing those who do evil from the world (see Psalm 1:6).

title: Psalms 63:1
content_html:
• Together, soul and body refer to a person's entire being.

  • dry and weary land: When David lived in the desert, he felt spiritually empty, away from God's people and the sanctuary.

title: Psalms 63:1–11
content_html: The king deeply desires God's presence. He experiences God's goodness in everything he does. Despite being tired and troubled by the wicked, the king finds strength in God and praises him. God is his source of life (Psalm 63:3), while the wicked face destruction (63:9).

title: Psalms 63:2
content_html: The psalmist remembers that he had found God to be present in his sanctuary (see Psalm 18:6; 61:4; 62:5, 11–12).

title: Psalms 63:3
content_html: better than life: The best life comes from a relationship with God (see Psalms 4:7; 63:5; Proverbs 16:8).

title: Psalms 63:6
content_html: Meditating on God requires focus, concentration, thought, and reflection (see Psalm 1:2).

title: Psalms 63:6–8
content_html: The psalmist thinks about and feels God's presence during the night. When it is dark, God is the light. The quiet night and the wait for morning offer time to reflect on the true meaning of life with the Lord.

title: Psalms 63:9
content_html:
• The wicked planned to ruin the psalmist's life with lies and deceit, but they would fail.

  • the depths of the earth: This refers to the place of the dead.

title: Psalms 63:10
content_html: Wild foxes and jackals are often associated with places that are ruined and abandoned (Isaiah 13:20–22; 34:10–15; Jeremiah 9:11; 49:33; 51:37; Lamentations 5:18; Malachi 1:3). The wicked will not receive honor in death. Scavenging animals will consume their bodies (Ecclesiastes 6:3; Jeremiah 22:18–19; 36:29–30).

title: Psalms 64:1–6
content_html: These verses form a prayer for rescue from bold and powerful liars.

title: Psalms 64:1–10
content_html: This lament speaks against the destructive plans and arrogant attitude of the wicked (Psalm 64:1–6). The psalmist prays to God and finds comfort in God's justice because the righteous judge will undo the wicked's actions. The godly will rejoice and gain wisdom as they think about God's powerful deeds.

title: Psalms 64:3
content_html: their tongues … bitter words: Liars use their words to harm others. This description might also refer to practicing magic.

title: Psalms 64:6
content_html:
• The enemies make a plan to harm the innocent without getting caught.

  • Human beings can act with extreme evil through words, actions, or plans (Psalm 83:3; see Jeremiah 17:9).

title: Psalms 64:7–10
content_html: God acts righteously. God stops wickedness and gives the wicked what they intended for others.

title: Psalms 65:1
content_html: God alone deserves praise and is worthy to have vows made to him.

title: Psalms 65:1–3
content_html: Praise is the right response to God's great acts in redemption (Psalm 65:3–5) and in nature (65:5–13).

title: Psalms 65:1–13
content_html: This psalm recounts the reasons why all people should praise and revere God for his almighty power and merciful care for his universe.

title: Psalms 65:2
content_html: all people: This means all humans.

title: Psalms 65:3
content_html: You atone: God graciously removes the guilt of sin (see Psalm 32:5).

title: Psalms 65:4
content_html:
• the one You choose and bring near: This includes people from Israel and other nations.

  • Only those God sees as blameless live in his holy courts (Psalms 15 and 24).

  • At the temple, God's earthly palace, all nations can gather to experience God's holy presence. No structure can separate humans from God (see Revelation 21:22).

title: Psalms 65:6–7
content_html:
• God brought order to the mountains, the roaring oceans, and the pounding waves (see Psalms 95:4–5; 104:6–9).

  • The psalmist compares the chaotic forces of nature with the rebellion of the nations (see Psalms 2:1–12; 33:6–11; 46:6).

title: Psalms 65:8
content_html: All over the world (Job 38:13; see Psalm 50:1–3), people should stand in awe of the Lord.

title: Psalms 65:9
content_html: The streams of God and all rivers show God's power and goodness through nature's order and the regularity of the harvests (see Psalms 1:3; 36:8; 46:4; Ezekiel 47:6–12; Zechariah 14:8; Revelation 22:1).

title: Psalms 65:9–13
content_html: The created world clearly shows God's power (Psalm 65:6–8). He shows his love by taking care of nature. Everything praises the Lord as all parts work together smoothly.

title: Psalms 65:13
content_html: Nature praises the Creator (see Psalm 79:13; 96:11–13; 148:3–5, 7–12; Isaiah 55:12–13).

title: Psalms 66:1–4
content_html: The psalmist urges nature to join a powerful song to praise God's glory, name, and power.

title: Psalms 66:1–20
content_html: This anonymous psalm includes two hymns (Psalm 66:1–12, 13–20) that praise God for saving his people.

title: Psalms 66:2
content_html: The glory of his name represents the perfection of God’s character.

title: Psalms 66:3
content_html: God defeats his enemies (see Psalm 18:44; 59:11; 81:15).

title: Psalms 66:5–7
content_html: The story of the exodus and God's victory over the sea causes the people to be amazed (Psalm 66:5) and to worship (66:6). God performs miracles to redeem people for himself.

title: Psalms 66:7
content_html: God's power brings redemption. The same word appears in Psalm 65:6 to describe God's power in creation (see also Psalm 80:2).

title: Psalms 66:8–10
content_html: The orderly lives of the redeemed reflect the order of God’s world. The Lord protects them from evil and purifies them.

title: Psalms 66:9
content_html: God guides his people into wisdom and away from foolishness (Psalms 17:5; 37:31; 121:3).

title: Psalms 66:10
content_html: For You, O God, have tested us: Like a silversmith heats silver to remove impurities, God's discipline removes sin from his people (see Psalm 12:6).

title: Psalms 66:11
content_html: God decrees and permits the many trying circumstances his people experience.

title: Psalms 66:12
content_html: You let men ride over our heads: Or You made people rule over us. This likely refers to a foreign ruler who controlled the Israelites.

title: Psalms 66:15
content_html: Vows often included sacrifices of thanksgiving, including burnt offerings (see Psalms 20:3; 40:6; 50:8; 51:19).

title: Psalms 66:16–20
content_html: The private testimony at the end of the psalm matches the public praise at the beginning (Psalm 66:1–4).

title: Psalms 66:18
content_html: Confession leads to restoration (see Psalm 32:5).

title: Psalms 67:1–2
content_html: This prayer is inspired by the priestly blessing in Numbers 6:24–26. God's kindness to Israel influences his reputation among other nations (see Psalm 96:1–3).

title: Psalms 67:1–7
content_html: This anonymous prayer asks for God's blessing. The psalmist speaks about knowing and worshiping God among all nations (compare Psalms 64:9; 65:8). The prayer asks for God's name to remain holy and that his kingdom come (compare Matthew 6:9–13).

title: Psalms 67:3
content_html:
• God’s international reputation leads to his praise among the nations (see Psalm 138:4). Their chaos and rebellion will stop when they join Israel in praising God.

  • One God rules over everyone, and the nations will give thanks for God’s just rule (see Psalm 67:4).

title: Psalms 67:4
content_html: God delivers the ultimate justice that people seek (Psalms 96:10; 98:9).

title: Psalms 67:6–7
content_html:
• Large harvests show God’s blessing (Genesis 26:12; Isaiah 30:23).

  • all the ends of the earth: See Psalms 2:8; 96:1–3.

  • People from every nation will join Israel in acknowledging the God of Israel.

title: Psalms 68:1–3
content_html: These verses form a prayer for God to rise and scatter his enemies, like when Israel entered the promised land (compare Numbers 10:35–36; Joshua 6–12).

title: Psalms 68:1–35
content_html: This prayer for God's victorious rule describes God's journey from Sinai to Zion (Psalm 68:7, 16–18, 24). He saved Israel from Egypt, led them through the wilderness, brought them to the promised land, and established his kingdom. He is a caring and victorious God, whose nature does not change. Therefore, the faithful hope and rejoice in the promise of God's universal rule.

title: Psalms 68:2
content_html: Both smoke and wax represent a temporary existence (Psalms 22:12–18; 37:20; 102:3).

title: Psalms 68:3
content_html: The fate of the godly is different from that of the wicked (see Psalm 1:6).

title: Psalms 68:4
content_html: Him who rides on the clouds: This is an ancient description of Baal, a Canaanite god. The psalmist uses it to describe the Lord as the true God "who rides upon the highest heavens" (see Psalms 68:33; 104:3). God has power over the clouds and the rain.

title: Psalms 68:4–6
content_html: The psalmist praises God’s powerful and compassionate reign.

title: Psalms 68:5
content_html:
• God, the Father of all marginalized and needy people, shows them compassion and protects their rights (see Psalms 10:14, 18; 69:33; 82:3).

  • Like his dwelling, the Lord is holy. The supreme example of the holy God coming down to rescue humanity is Jesus Christ.

title: Psalms 68:6
content_html:
• God freed Israel from slavery in Egypt (Exodus 20:2; see Psalm 69:33), and he continues to free prisoners today.

  • The sun-scorched land represents being far from God and separated from blessing.

title: Psalms 68:8–10
content_html:
• The earth shook as it does during an earthquake (see Exodus 19:18).

  • the heavens poured down rain: Wells in the desert and rain showers in Canaan provided plenty of water.

title: Psalms 68:11
content_html: a great company of women proclaim it: A large group of women singers celebrates the victory in song (see Psalm 68:25; Exodus 15:20).

title: Psalms 68:13
content_html: The people who cared for flocks and moved from place to place were surprised to receive silver and gold ornaments from the battle.

title: Psalms 68:14
content_html:
• The Almighty (Hebrew Shaddai) is an ancient name for God (see Psalm 91:1; Genesis 17:1).

  • The exact location of Mount Zalmon is unknown; it might have been a mountain in Bashan (see Psalm 68:15).

title: Psalms 68:15
content_html: Bashan is a plateau northeast of Jerusalem and east of the Sea of Galilee. It was famous for its forests and pastureland (see Psalm 22:12; Isaiah 2:13). The mountains might have included Mount Hermon to the north of the plateau or Mount Zalmon, possibly Jebel ed-Druze to the east.

title: Psalms 68:16
content_html: Why do you gaze in envy: Bashan is more impressive than the mountains around Jerusalem, but it was not the Lord's home. Only Mount Zion had the honor of being God's dwelling place.

title: Psalms 68:17
content_html: Chariots were the top weapon of the military in the psalmist’s time. They represent the Lord’s power to give victory (Psalms 20:7; 44:3).

title: Psalms 68:18
content_html:
• You have ascended on high: The Most High victoriously established his reign in Zion.

  • captives … gifts: This image shows a victorious ruler leading a victory march after battle. Paul applied this image to Jesus Christ (Ephesians 4:8–13).

title: Psalms 68:19
content_html: Like a caring father or a shepherd, God carries his people in his arms (see Psalm 55:22; 68:5–6).

title: Psalms 68:19–20
content_html: God cares for his people like a shepherd, and the people praise him.

title: Psalms 68:21
content_html: God will crush the heads of His enemies: Compare Genesis 3:15.

title: Psalms 68:22
content_html: God will deal with all enemies, whether from high places (represented by Bashan) or low (the depths of the sea).

title: Psalms 68:24–27
content_html: A great company joins together in the pilgrimage to Zion.

title: Psalms 68:28–31
content_html: The people pray for God's help and victory over enemy nations (Psalm 68:1–3).

title: Psalms 68:29
content_html: The conquered kings will bring gifts to the Lord.

title: Psalms 68:30
content_html: Hostile and rebellious people delight in warfare and insurrection (see Psalm 2:1–3).

title: Psalms 68:32
content_html: The phrase kingdoms of the earth refers to all nations and power structures.

title: Psalms 68:32–35
content_html: The psalmist urges the people to praise God, who triumphs over his enemies.

title: Psalms 68:34
content_html: When the Lord shows his power, majesty, and strength to the nations, he makes his people stronger (see Psalm 96:4–6).

title: Psalms 69:1
content_html: Psalm 45 also mentions this tune, but Psalm 45 praises the glory of kingship. In contrast, Psalm 69 is the cry of a tired king who feels abandoned by the Lord.

title: Psalms 69:1–2
content_html: The psalmist sees himself as almost drowning but still surviving chaotic forces and his sense of separation from God (see Psalm 42:7; 69:14, 15; Isaiah 8:8).

title: Psalms 69:1–36
content_html: In this personal lament, the poet shares his vulnerability, humiliation, and deep pain. He asks God to defend him for the sake of the righteous.

title: Psalms 69:1–72.20
content_html: These last four psalms of Book Two contrast with the earlier psalms that describe God’s mighty acts in creation and redemption, ending with God’s march to Zion (Psalms 65–68). Psalms 69–71 describe the king's suffering, and Psalm 72 responds with a prayer for God to bless the king.

title: Psalms 69:4
content_html: John applied this lament to Jesus (John 15:25).

title: Psalms 69:5–6
content_html: The psalmist confesses his sins and prays that God’s response to him will encourage others in similar conditions to place their hope in the Lord.

title: Psalms 69:7–12
content_html: Mistreated and abandoned by family, friends, and community, the psalmist seeks comfort, relief (Psalm 69:13–18), and justice (69:22–28) from the Lord.

title: Psalms 69:9
content_html: The psalmist is devoted to God and what is holy, yet people repay him with insults. Later, this verse was applied to the anger of Jesus at the temple’s money changers (John 2:17) and his undeserved suffering (Romans 15:3).

title: Psalms 69:10–12
content_html: When the psalmist denies himself normal comforts to seek God in prayer, the wicked ridicule him.

title: Psalms 69:13–18
content_html: The psalmist can only pray for rescue and relief from his enemies.

title: Psalms 69:19–21
content_html: The psalmist feels alone and misunderstood. He cannot bear his doubts, and his troubles overwhelm him.

title: Psalms 69:21
content_html: vinegar to quench my thirst: This description applies to the suffering of Jesus (Matthew 27:34, 48; Luke 23:36; John 19:28–29).

title: Psalms 69:22–23
content_html: The psalmist asks God to give his suffering (Psalm 69:3) to his enemies. Paul applied these words to the unbelieving Jews of his day (Romans 11:9–10).

title: Psalms 69:25
content_html: Peter quoted this verse after the death of Judas Iscariot (Acts 1:20).

title: Psalms 69:28
content_html: May they be blotted out of the Book of Life: Compare Psalm 1:5–6; 9:5; Revelation 3:5; 20:15; 21:22–27.

title: Psalms 69:29–33
content_html: The psalmist prays that his pain will turn to praise.

title: Psalms 69:31
content_html: Making a vow often included a sacrifice (Psalms 20:3; 51:19; compare 50:13–14; 51:16).

title: Psalms 69:32–33
content_html: despise: See Psalm 22:24; compare Matthew 25:36; Hebrews 13:3.

title: Psalms 69:34–36
content_html: The psalmist prays for Zion, offering a new petition from a new circumstance. He applies the prayer (Psalm 69:1–33) to the desperate condition of Judah (see 51:18–19).

title: Psalms 69:36
content_html: God’s people will inherit the land because the wicked will perish (see Psalm 37:8–9).

title: Psalms 70:1
content_html: To bring remembrance: The meaning of this phrase is not known (also found in Psalm 38:1).

title: Psalms 70:1–5
content_html: This psalm is almost exactly the same as Psalm 40:13–17. It has an urgent tone (Psalm 70:1, 5) that matches the nearby psalms (compare 69:17; 71:12).

title: Psalms 71:1–4
content_html: The poet recognizes God as a dependable protector who will act justly towards him and his oppressors. See also Psalm 31:1–3.

title: Psalms 71:1–24
content_html:
This untitled lament by an aging believer is more a confession of confidence and hope than a cry of abandonment by God. The psalmist experienced God's power and protection in his youth (Psalm 71:5–8) and now prays for rescue from enemies who pursue him in his old age (Psalm 71:9–13).

Even in danger, he continues to speak of God's past faithfulness and looks forward with hope to sharing with the next generation that God is faithful and righteous.

title: Psalms 71:5–6
content_html: The psalmist has trusted God throughout his life (see Psalm 22:9).

title: Psalms 71:9
content_html: The poet prays for continued fellowship with God (see Psalm 9:10; 51:11). In his weakness, the poet needs the Lord's strength even more (see Psalm 143:7).

title: Psalms 71:9–18
content_html: The aging believer turns to the Lord for hope. As questions from his adversaries get under his skin and his own strength fails, he asks God to answer his prayers. He does not seek answers to prayer for himself but so that he can tell the story to the next generation.

title: Psalms 71:10
content_html: The psalmist's enemies plan and scheme together (Psalms 2:2; 21:11; 31:13; 35:4; 63:9–10).

title: Psalms 71:14
content_html: The psalmist compares his goodness to his enemies' evil. He focuses on the Lord, even in difficult times (see Psalms 9:17–18; 27:13–14).

title: Psalms 71:19–21
content_html: God’s righteous character provides encouragement and strength in the midst of suffering. The wounded psalmist confesses faith in God’s ability to transform his misery and weakness into abundant life.

title: Psalms 71:20
content_html: You will bring me back up: The psalmist hopes for the renewal of an abundant earthly life. This later became grounds for belief in the resurrection of the dead.

title: Psalms 71:21
content_html: Knowing God provides comfort also assures the poet of God's presence, care, and goodness (Psalms 23:4, 6; 86:17). God's comfort increases the psalmist's sense of being alive (see Psalm 119:50).

title: Psalms 71:22–24
content_html: As he awaits God’s rescue, the psalmist prepares himself to declare with instruments and voice that God is faithful and righteous.

title: Psalms 72:1
content_html: justice … righteousness: In the ancient Near East, kings were expected to represent justice. For Israel's kings, the standard was higher because they followed the Lord's example.

title: Psalms 72:1–7
content_html: Justice brings balance to nature, leading to harmony, prosperity, and fertility.

title: Psalms 72:1–20
content_html: This royal psalm concludes Book Two, with Psalm 72:18–20 serving as an epilogue for the entire book. The psalmist considers the future of David's royal line and Zion (see Psalms 46, 48). He prays that Israel's kings will be good and successful, spreading the Lord's blessing to all people worldwide. The exceptional righteousness and rule desired in this prayer hint at the future arrival of Jesus, the Son of David.

title: Psalms 72:3
content_html: Justice makes the world fruitful, abundant, harmonious, and good (see Isaiah 32:16–17).

title: Psalms 72:4
content_html: A just ruler will defend the poor against injustice, rescue them from violence, and bring their oppressors to account for their wrongdoing.

title: Psalms 72:5
content_html: May they fear him: In Hebrew, this phrase appears to be a response to the enforcement of justice. The Greek version reads May they endure, which makes this a prayer for long life (Psalm 21:4; 72:15, 17).

title: Psalms 72:6–7
content_html:
• The administration of justice is refreshing like spring rain, allowing the godly to thrive (see Isaiah 45:8) like flowers.

  • until the moon is no more: This phrase means forever (see Psalm 89:36–37).

title: Psalms 72:8–9
content_html:
• from sea to sea: This phrase represents the entire world.

  • Solomon's kingdom stretched from the Euphrates River in the east to the Philistine territory in the west (1 Kings 4:21; see Psalms 80:8–11; 89:19–25), where the Mediterranean Sea appeared to be at the ends of the earth (see 2:8).

    Everyone will bow before this king, including desert nomads (Psalm 72:9), his enemies, and all the world's kings (see Micah 7:17).

title: Psalms 72:8–11
content_html: The anointed king will rule the entire world. Even enemy nations will obey him. In the ancient Near East, giving gifts to the king showed his success and reputation (see 1 Kings 10).

title: Psalms 72:10
content_html:
• The western kings of Tarshish came from a major colony in the western Mediterranean. The eastern kings of Sheba came from southern Arabia, home of the queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10).

  • Seba was likely south of Egypt, possibly on the western shore of the Red Sea across from Sheba.

  • The gifts given to the king were a tribute and a sign of submission.

title: Psalms 72:11
content_html: All kings and nations must submit to the messianic king (God's chosen leader). The hope in Psalm 2:10–11 will come true (see 96:1–3; 1 Corinthians 15:25) when other leaders and peoples bow and serve this king.

title: Psalms 72:12
content_html: To deliver the needy means to bring an end to the rule of their oppressors.

title: Psalms 72:12–14
content_html: The anointed king will help those in need. He is both a judge and a father to the poor (Psalms 10:14, 17; 68:5).

title: Psalms 72:13
content_html: Like a father, the king feels pity for those in need (see Malachi 3:17).

title: Psalms 72:15
content_html: The people will bless him, fulfilling God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:3; see Psalm 133:3).

title: Psalms 72:15–17
content_html: The just kingdom will be long-lasting and universal. The summary combines the themes of abundance of crops, longevity, tribute of the nations, and the prayer of the nations.

title: Psalms 72:16
content_html: the forests of Lebanon: Lebanon was famous for its wood (see study note on 2 Chronicles 2:8).

title: Psalms 72:18–19
content_html: This doxology (song of praise) ends Book Two of the Psalms.

title: Psalms 72:20
content_html: This parenthetical note ends Book Two, which consists mostly of prayers of David. Books Three to Five were probably compiled after Book Two; they include additional psalms of David (Psalms 86, 101, 103, 108–110, 122, 124, 131, 133, 138–145).

title: Psalms 73:1
content_html: People with pure hearts (people who are sincere and devoted to God) are renewed by God’s Spirit (Psalm 51:10). They choose to live in a godly way, turn away from sin, and enjoy God’s presence (see Psalm 24:3–4).

title: Psalms 73:1–28
content_html:
This wisdom psalm looks at the injustice of the prosperity of the wicked. The psalmist states that God is good to the godly, but his own experience is different (Psalm 73:2–12).

Almost overwhelmed by doubt (Psalm 73:13–16), the psalmist meets the Lord in the sanctuary. This meeting gives him a broader view and renews his trust in God (Psalm 73:17–26). His troubling doubts lead to a stronger desire for truth. He knows he can trust God and that God will save him (Psalm 73:27–28).

title: Psalms 73:title
content_html: Asaph was a Levitical singer appointed by David (1 Chronicles 6:39). His descendants were singers and instrumentalists (1 Chronicles 15:16–17). Many of the Psalms of Asaph (Psalms 50, 73–83) were likely written by Asaph’s descendants later in Israel’s history (for example, Psalm 74).

title: Psalms 73:1–75.10
content_html: The seeming success of the wicked (Psalm 73) and God's apparent rejection of his people (Psalm 74) raise questions about his justice. God rules the entire world and decides when to judge (Psalm 75).

title: Psalms 73:2–3
content_html:
• The poet thinks it is unfair that wicked people prosper (see Psalm 72:7) and feels upset about it.

  • The psalmist almost strayed from the path of wisdom and godliness (see Psalm 17:5; 37:31), but God prevented him from completely losing his way (38:16; 66:9; 94:18; 121:3).

  • when I saw the prosperity of the wicked: Compare Psalm 72:3, 7.

title: Psalms 73:4–12
content_html: The psalmist gives an exaggerated picture of the happiness of the wicked (compare Psalm 1).

title: Psalms 73:6
content_html: Proud people choose pride and cruelty over wisdom (Proverbs 1:9).

title: Psalms 73:9
