content_html: The proud boast, claiming that they own everything and are not subject to anyone (see Psalm 2:1–3).

title: Psalms 73:13–14
content_html:
• These rhetorical questions expect a discouraging “yes” as an answer. The psalmist wonders if godliness has become a meaningless ritual (see Malachi 3:14–15).

  • my heart: This refers to his whole being.

  • The psalmist’s pain comes from discipline and rebuke (Psalm 39:11; Proverbs 1:23, 25; 12:1).

title: Psalms 73:13–17
content_html: When the psalmist sees the effect of his discouragement and doubt, he turns to the Lord and gains new understanding.

title: Psalms 73:15–16
content_html: If the psalmist had spoken about his doubts regarding the value of living a godly life, he might have discouraged God's people and given God's enemies a chance to speak against God (compare John 13:20). Instead, the psalmist keeps the problem to himself and thinks about it.

title: Psalms 73:17–20
content_html:
• During this period, God's sanctuary was the temple in Jerusalem. In God's presence, the psalmist receives special insight (compare Psalm 18:6).

  • God deals with the wicked over the long term. He waits patiently and gives them time to repent (Ezekiel 33:11; 2 Peter 3:9). Those who do not repent will get what they deserve.

title: Psalms 73:21–22
content_html: The psalmist’s feelings about the prosperity of the wicked had given him a bitter spirit.

title: Psalms 73:24
content_html: The Lord leads the godly through their troubles and changes their misery into splendor.

title: Psalms 73:25–26
content_html: Nothing in heaven or on earth is better than being near God.

title: Psalms 74:1
content_html:
• God rejected his people because their sin made him angry (Psalm 106:34–46).

  • Like a caring shepherd, God looks after his people, the sheep of his pasture (Psalms 23; 100:3).

title: Psalms 74:1–2
content_html: In the aftermath of Jerusalem’s fall, it seemed that God had forgotten his special covenant relationship with his people.

title: Psalms 74:1–23
content_html: The psalmist laments the destruction of the temple in Jerusalem. This happened in 586 BC. The psalmist uses vivid imagery, asks questions, recalls fresh memories, and directly asks the Lord to act (Psalm 74:19–23).

title: Psalms 74:2
content_html:
• The Lord chose and redeemed Israel from Egypt in the exodus (compare Exodus 15:13).

  • Your congregation: This is another expression for "your people" (Psalms 28:9; 78:62; 94:14; 106:40).

title: Psalms 74:3–8
content_html: Like a guide, the psalmist points out to the Lord what took place during the destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC.

title: Psalms 74:12
content_html: my King from ancient times: The psalmist acknowledges God’s power and his historical bond with Israel (Psalms 5:2; 44:4; 84:3).

title: Psalms 74:14
content_html: Leviathan: See Psalm 104:26; Job 3:8; 41:1, 12, 22, 31; Isaiah 27:1. Here, the creature’s multiple heads suggest a mythical monster.

title: Psalms 74:15
content_html: ever-flowing rivers: God gave water in the desert to his people (see Psalm 68:8–9; Exodus 17:6). He dried up the Red Sea (Exodus 14:21) and the Jordan River (Joshua 3:17).

title: Psalms 74:19–23
content_html: The poet urges God to act.

title: Psalms 75:1–10
content_html: This hymn of thanksgiving includes two direct messages from God that assure us of his justice (Psalm 75:2–3, 10). The Lord warns arrogant people who defy him, promising to humble them by force (Psalm 75:4–8). Together, the community and the psalmist give thanks for God's character (Psalm 75:1, 9).

title: Psalms 75:2–8
content_html: The Lord will bring justice in his own time.

title: Psalms 75:4–5
content_html: The wicked proudly defy heaven, where God lives. By boasting, they claim that they are wiser than God.

title: Psalms 75:6
content_html: No creature has the right to rebel against its Creator.

title: Psalms 75:7–8
content_html: The Lord alone is the Judge. He has the power to raise people up or bring them down (see 1 Samuel 2:7; Luke 1:52).

title: Psalms 76:1
content_html: A song: The Greek version reads for the Assyrian.

title: Psalms 76:1–3
content_html: God's victory over Zion's enemies hints at his future victory over all his enemies worldwide.

title: Psalms 76:1–12
content_html: This psalm appears to celebrate a victory. The victory might have been recent, such as over the Assyrians (see note on 76:1; see also Isaiah 37). Or, it might be remembering God's great acts of rescue in the past.

title: Psalms 76:3
content_html: God brings an end to the attacks of his enemies (see Psalm 46:9).

title: Psalms 76:4
content_html: The victorious king’s splendor, glory, and majesty are greater than anything in the created order (Psalm 8:1; 29:4; 111:3).

title: Psalms 76:4–6
content_html: The psalmist praises God for his achievement and for his glorious majesty.

title: Psalms 76:7
content_html: feared: The Hebrew word nora’ sounds like the Hebrew word na’or, translated "resplendent," in Psalm 76:4.

title: Psalms 76:7–9
content_html: The needy await their victorious God, while the wicked stand in fear of him.

title: Psalms 77:1–3
content_html: The night provides a special opportunity for prayer and meditation (Psalms 77:6, 11; see 6:6; 16:7; 17:3; 30:5; 63:6; 119:55, 62, 148).

title: Psalms 77:1–20
content_html: The psalmist laments that God has turned away from him, even though he feels innocent. He reflects on his situation (Psalm 77:3, 6, 12) and finds hope (see 25:5) in remembering God's past actions (77:11).

title: Psalms 77:3
content_html:
• I remembered You, O God: God's people often face hard times. Remembering God's powerful actions at the beginning of their relationship assures them of God's commitment.

  • Feeling overwhelmed, the psalmist is unable to deal with the crisis (Psalms 142:3; 143:4).

title: Psalms 77:4–6
content_html: As the psalmist meditates at night, he feels so disturbed that he cannot sleep or speak. He has pleasant memories of the past, but he cannot look beyond the present moment into the future.

title: Psalms 77:7
content_html: Feeling rejected (see Psalm 43:2), the psalmist asks if the situation is permanent.

title: Psalms 77:7–9
content_html: The psalmist questions his status before God.

title: Psalms 77:10
content_html: The psalmist is afraid that God's hand of protection (Psalm 44:3; 77:15; Exodus 6:6) and favor (Psalms 16:7–8; 110:1) are now against him for no clear reason.

title: Psalms 77:10–12
content_html: The memories of God’s mighty works in the past fill the psalmist’s mind.

title: Psalms 77:13–15
content_html: This central section of the poem reflects on God as the holy and powerful redeemer.

title: Psalms 77:16
content_html: the waters saw You and swirled: The Canaanites attributed divine power to the sea, but God is the one who made the sea and uses it for his own purposes.

title: Psalms 78:1–8
content_html: The introduction is an invitation to learn wisdom.

title: Psalms 78:1–72
content_html: This wisdom psalm urges people to learn wisdom and share it faithfully.

title: Psalms 78:2
content_html:
• The psalmist tells Israel’s story (Psalm 78:5–72) as a parable to teach wisdom and insight.

  • Jesus quoted this verse to explain why he taught in parables (Matthew 13:35).

title: Psalms 78:3–4
content_html: This story of what God has done should be told from generation to generation (see Deuteronomy 6:20–25; Proverbs 4:1–4).

title: Psalms 78:5–6
content_html: The telling of the story should motivate God’s people to obey what he commanded (Deuteronomy 6:4–9).

title: Psalms 78:7–8
content_html: God holds each generation responsible for its own actions. They should maintain faith and hope (see Psalm 9:18; 27:14) and avoid being stubborn, rebellious, and unfaithful (see Deuteronomy 9:6–7; 10:16; 30:6; 31:27).

title: Psalms 78:9–11
content_html: Ephraim was the second son of Joseph. He received a special blessing from Jacob (Genesis 48:15–20; 49:22–26; Deuteronomy 33:13–17). His descendants inherited land with fertile valleys and important roads (Judges 8:2). However, the people of Ephraim did not follow God's covenant.

title: Psalms 78:12
content_html: The wonders are the ten plagues against Egypt that God performed during Israel’s captivity (Exodus 7–12).

title: Psalms 78:12–16
content_html: The focus shifts from Ephraim (Psalm 78:9–11) to all of Israel. The psalmist thinks about God's power in Egypt (see Psalm 81:4–7) and in the wilderness.

title: Psalms 78:13
content_html: God split the sea when Israel crossed through the Red Sea (see Exodus 14–15).

title: Psalms 78:14
content_html: God used a cloud and a light of fire to guide and protect Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 13:21–22).

title: Psalms 78:17–31
content_html: Israel rebelled in the wilderness despite God’s abundant provision.

title: Psalms 78:23–25
content_html:
• Abundance comes through the doors of heaven (Genesis 8:2; 2 Kings 7:2; Malachi 3:10).

  • The Israelites ate manna for 40 years in the wilderness (Exodus 16:31–36; John 6:31).

title: Psalms 78:26–31
content_html: God powerfully satisfied the people’s desires, but they did not respond with gratitude or faith. Therefore, God exercised his justice.

title: Psalms 78:32–39
content_html: Israel’s rebellion resulted in God’s judgment, but even their short-lived and insincere repentance motivated God to exercise forbearance.

title: Psalms 78:33
content_html: An entire generation died in the wilderness (Numbers 14:22–23, 28–35).

title: Psalms 78:34
content_html: they repented: This strong show of effort was not sincere.

title: Psalms 78:34–39
content_html: Israel continued to exist because of God's compassion, despite the people's rebellion and lack of belief (Psalm 78:7–8).

title: Psalms 78:35–39
content_html: The Israelites remembered the correct teaching about God (Psalms 9:9; 91:1), but they did not let God change the way they thought and lived (Leviticus 26:41).

title: Psalms 78:40–55
content_html: The Israelites rebelled even though the Lord had rescued them from powerful plagues.

title: Psalms 78:56–64
content_html: Israel’s rebellion defiled the promised land. During the time of the judges, the people built shrines to false gods and worshiped idols (Psalm 78:58; see also Judges 2:11; 3:7, 17–18).

title: Psalms 78:60
content_html: God abandoned his dwelling at Shiloh because of the people’s idol worship (1 Samuel 4:11; Jeremiah 7:12).

title: Psalms 78:67
content_html: The tribe of Ephraim was not completely rejected, but God favored Judah (Psalm 78:68).

title: Psalms 78:68–69
content_html: In the choice of Mount Zion instead of Shiloh as God’s sanctuary on earth, God moved from the north to the south (see Psalm 69).

title: Psalms 78:70–72
content_html: The God who had turned against his own people (Psalm 78:62) chose David, a shepherd (Genesis 46:34; 1 Samuel 16:10–11), to lead them as their shepherd (Psalm 78:52; compare 23:1).

title: Psalms 79:1
content_html: Your inheritance: This phrase might refer to the land, the temple (see Psalm 61:5), or the people (see 79:2; 78:71).

title: Psalms 79:1–4
content_html: The poet laments the defilement of the temple and the success of the enemy.

title: Psalms 79:1–13
content_html:
This lament describes the fall of Jerusalem in 586 BC. Israel’s early history of rebellion, described in Psalm 78, angered God. As a result, Zion was destroyed. The temple was defiled, and the bodies of the dead lay across the land.

The psalmist then prays for forgiveness and rescue (Psalm 79:8–9) and asks God to repay the nation's enemies (Psalm 79:12–13). Other psalms lamenting the exile include Psalms 42–44, 102, 107, 126, and 137.

title: Psalms 79:5–8
content_html: The intensity of God’s judgment surprises the people; they cry out in despair for compassion, forgiveness, and relief. Verse 6–7 are nearly identical to Jeremiah 10:25.

title: Psalms 79:6
content_html: The repentant among God’s people ask him to vent his anger not on them but on their wicked and unrepentant enemies.

title: Psalms 79:8
content_html: The people’s many sins in the past helped lead to the exile (2 Kings 17:7–23; 24:3–4). However, each person also suffered because of his or her own sin (Jeremiah 31:30; Ezekiel 18:20).

title: Psalms 79:9–11
content_html: The people ask God to rescue them. Although Israel’s sins caused the tragedy, the pagan nations must also be held responsible for the bloodshed.

title: Psalms 79:12
content_html:
• The exile resulted in national suffering and in individual searching for the Lord.

  • Pay back: See Psalm 1:6; 94:2; Jeremiah 32:18.

  • The phrase sevenfold means something is complete.

  • Israel's neighbors included Edom, Moab, and Ammon (see Psalm 137:7; 2 Kings 24:2; Obadiah 1).

title: Psalms 80:1
content_html: The tune of “The Lilies of the Covenant” is unknown today.

title: Psalms 80:1–2
content_html:
• O Shepherd of Israel: See also Psalm 23:1; 28:9; 78:52–53.

  • shine forth … mighty power: These expressions (Psalm 145:11) refer to God's visible presence.

title: Psalms 80:1–3
content_html: The psalmist urgently calls upon the Lord to act.

title: Psalms 80:1–19
content_html:
This psalm of lament may come from the remnant (the small group that survived) of the northern kingdom after it fell in 722 BC. The people call on God as their Shepherd and as the God of Hosts (a title that describes God as the ruler over the armies of heaven). They hope that he will return to them and restore them.

They remember the Exodus and their special relationship with the Lord. The psalm ends with a promise of renewed commitment to him (Psalm 80:18).

title: Psalms 80:3
content_html: This verse is repeated (with a growing intensity) in Psalm 80:7 and 19.

title: Psalms 80:4–7
content_html: The people cry out for rescue from the shame of their recent defeat. The enemy’s victory is less devastating, however, than the knowledge that they themselves are objects of God’s wrath.

title: Psalms 80:8–9
content_html: Israel had been like a fruitful grapevine (Genesis 1:28; Galatians 5:22–23) that God transplanted to Canaan at the time of the conquest. However, the people forgot the source of their prosperity and chose to do evil (Isaiah 5:1–7; Hosea 10:1).

title: Psalms 80:8–11
content_html: Israel has a glorious past that began when God rescued the people from Egypt.

title: Psalms 80:10–11
content_html: Solomon’s kingdom had extended west to the Mediterranean Sea and east to the Euphrates River (see Psalms 72:8–9; 89:25; 1 Kings 4:21).

title: Psalms 80:12
content_html: broken down its walls: God no longer defended Israel (Isaiah 5:5).

title: Psalms 80:13
content_html: The surrounding nations were ceremonially unclean, like a wild boar or vicious wild animals.

title: Psalms 80:15
content_html: Israel was the son that God had brought out of Egypt (see Hosea 11:1) and planted him in the Holy Land.

title: Psalms 80:16–19
content_html: The community prays for God to change their desperate situation and restore them. They are still the Lord’s chosen people.

title: Psalms 80:17
content_html: The son God chose was probably the northern kingdom of Israel. But it may also refer to the king from David’s line (Psalm 110:1).

title: Psalms 81:1–3
content_html: Before giving a warning, the psalmist calls Israel to think about their God through song and dance.

title: Psalms 81:1–16
content_html: This warning message invites Israel to worship only the Lord and to enjoy the fullness of his blessing.

title: Psalms 81:2
content_html:
• An Israelite tambourine was a hand drum with a round frame and skin cover. People used it as a percussion instrument during celebrations, often to accompany dancing (Psalms 68:25; 149:3; 150:4).

  • The sweet-sounding harp and lyre, both stringed instruments, are the most frequently mentioned instruments in the psalms (for example, Psalms 33:2; 57:8; 71:22; 150:3).

title: Psalms 81:3
content_html:
• The ram’s horn gathered armies and announced special holy days (Psalms 47:5; 98:6).

  • New Moon: The first day of each lunar month was a festival.

title: Psalms 81:4–5
content_html: The festival, a time to celebrate and listen to the Lord, took place at God’s appointed time.

title: Psalms 81:5
content_html: for Joseph: The tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh (the sons of Joseph) are used here to refer to the entire nation of Israel.

title: Psalms 81:6–7
content_html: The Lord heard Israel’s cry in Egypt and rescued them from oppressive slavery.

title: Psalms 81:8–10
content_html: God pleads with his people to remember the covenant and the Ten Commandments.

title: Psalms 81:10
content_html:
• God emphasizes that he and no one else rescued Israel.

  • The nation should eagerly accept God's word (compare Deuteronomy 8:3; Ezekiel 3:1).

title: Psalms 81:11–16
content_html: The Lord helps Israel to understand that their failure was not because of his weakness.

title: Psalms 81:13–14
content_html:
• God wants to give his goodness to his children, but they must listen (Isaiah 1:18–19; 28:12; 48:18–19; see Deuteronomy 6:3).

  • God promises to defeat the enemies (Deuteronomy 9:3) of those who follow the paths of obedience and loyalty. He will also provide abundantly for his people (Isaiah 1:19).

title: Psalms 82:1
content_html:
• God is ready to give judgment (see Isaiah 3:13).

  • divine assembly: See Job 1:6.

title: Psalms 82:1–8
content_html: God condemns those who mistreat the helpless.

title: Psalms 82:2
content_html: Because justice defines God’s rule (Psalm 89:4), he does not support the wicked. The psalmist is confused by this situation and cries out to God to bring justice (Psalm 82:3–4, 8).

title: Psalms 82:5
content_html: Unjust oppressors cannot bring or restore order. They wander about in darkness, unmoved by circumstances, even when the whole world order falls apart around them (compare Psalm 11:3).

title: Psalms 82:6–7
content_html: The gods (see study notes on Psalm 29:1–2; 58:1), like mere humans, cannot escape the judgment common to human rulers. Jesus quoted from this passage in John 10:34.

title: Psalms 82:8
content_html: The nations belong to God. He will take over the realms formerly ruled by local gods and give them to his people, just as he did with the promised land.

title: Psalms 83:1–4
content_html: The enemy has successfully intimidated God’s people, so the poet prays that the Lord will hear and act.

title: Psalms 83:1–18
content_html:
This community lament asks God to rescue his people and give them victory. The short and direct poetry suggests a time of great trouble. A strong group of nations plans to destroy Israel (Psalm 83:1–4) and joins together against God (83:5–8).

The psalmist remembers what happened to other enemies who attacked Israel in the past (Psalm 83:9–12). He prays that the same thing will happen to these enemies (83:13–16), so that they will recognize God’s power and give him honor (83:17–18).

It is not clear whether this psalm refers to one specific historical event or whether it gathers together several nations as a poetic picture of Israel’s enemies.

title: Psalms 83:5–8
content_html: they form an alliance: The allies agreed to fight against God and his people. The nations named in this alliance lived around Israel and Judah.

title: Psalms 83:6–7
content_html:
• The Edomites were descendants of Esau. They lived southeast of Judah (see Genesis 25:30; 32:3; 36:1–8).

  • The Ishmaelites were descendants of Hagar. They lived in the desert.

  • The Moabites and Ammonites were descendants of Lot. They lived east of Israel and Judah.

  • The Hagrites might have lived north of the Ammonites (1 Chronicles 5:10).

title: Psalms 83:7
content_html:
• Gebal, also known as Byblos, was on the coast of Lebanon.

  • The Amalekites lived south of Israel in the Negev desert.

  • Philistia was southwest of Judah, near the Mediterranean Sea.

  • Tyre was a port city on the Mediterranean Sea, northwest of Israel.

title: Psalms 83:8
content_html:
• Assyria was located northeast of Israel and north of the Persian Gulf.

  • Lot's descendants were the Moabites and the Ammonites (Psalm 83:6–7).

title: Psalms 83:9–11
content_html: The Midianites, led by Oreb and Zeeb and ruled by Zebah and Zalmunna, were Israel's enemies during Gideon's time (Judges 7:1–8:5). Barak's alliance defeated Sisera and Jabin (Judges 4).

title: Psalms 83:9–18
content_html: The psalmist prays that God will miraculously destroy the new coalition as he did three other enemies; doing so would bring him glory.

title: Psalms 83:12
content_html: The coalition wanted to destroy Israel to take over the land (see Jeremiah 3:19).

title: Psalms 83:13
content_html: tumbleweed … chaff: This dry plant material blows away easily (see Isaiah 17:13).

title: Psalms 83:14–15
content_html: fire … flame … tempest: These images describe how God appears when he brings judgment (see Isaiah 29:6).

title: Psalms 84:1–4
content_html: The psalmist is far from the temple. He turns toward it in hopeful reflection.

title: Psalms 84:1–12
content_html:
The psalmist deeply longs for God's presence. He feels weak with longing as he thinks about the temple and the pilgrims traveling to Jerusalem (Psalm 84:1–7). He prays for himself and the community (Psalm 84:8–9).

The end of the psalm (Psalm 84:10–12) shows that the temple represents God. The psalmist truly desires God's presence. He understands that God's goodness is better than life and that only God can grant his people favor and honor (Psalm 84:10–11).

title: Psalms 84:3
content_html: The poet envies the birds that have nesting places in and around the temple.

title: Psalms 84:4
content_html: The priests and those who worked in the temple also lived there.

title: Psalms 84:5
content_html: Travelers needed strength to meet the challenges of pilgrimage by foot along unpaved paths, in all kinds of weather and with the possibility of assaults.

title: Psalms 84:5–7
content_html: The pilgrimage to Jerusalem reminded people of the exodus, when God provided for his people in a wonderful way (see Psalm 78:15–16; 105:41). The psalmist describes how the hardships of the journey turn into joy when the travelers arrive.

title: Psalms 84:6
content_html:
• The Valley of Baca is also called the Valley of Weeping or the Valley of Tears. It likely symbolizes the sadness that lonely pilgrims felt (see Psalm 23:4), rather than a real place.

  • spring … autumn rain: Even when pilgrims feel completely worn out, the hope of getting closer to the Lord encourages them (compare Isaiah 35:6–10; 41:18–20; 43:17–20; 49:10).

title: Psalms 84:9
content_html: Both kings and priests were anointed to dedicate them for service (see Psalm 132:17; Exodus 28:41; 1 Samuel 9:16; 16:1–13; compare Isaiah 61:1; Acts 10:38).

title: Psalms 84:10
content_html: The psalmist viewed being in God’s presence as much better than being anywhere else.

title: Psalms 84:11
content_html: Some people worshiped the sun as a god, but the true God provides all that anyone needs.

title: Psalms 85:1
content_html: God brought Israel back from exile and restored its fortunes (see Psalm 14:7; 126:1).

title: Psalms 85:1–5
content_html: Though the psalmist gratefully acknowledges God’s goodness, he also asks God whether his wrath will last forever.

title: Psalms 85:1–13
content_html:
The psalmist leads the community that returned from exile in a lament and a prayer for complete restoration:

  • He remembers how God rescued Israel from exile (Psalm 85:1–3),

  • asks for clearer signs of God’s goodness, and

  • leads the people in prayer for full renewal (Psalm 85:4–7).

God answers with a message of peace (Psalm 85:8–9). The psalm ends with a poetic description of the greatness of God’s salvation (85:10–13).

title: Psalms 85:2
content_html: When God forgave the guilt of their sins, he gave the people the possibility of fellowship with him (see Psalm 32:5).

title: Psalms 85:6–7
content_html: This prayer for renewal is based on God’s unfailing love for his people.

title: Psalms 85:8–9
content_html:
• When God speaks, his faithful people listen. He promises them peace, meaning wholeness and well-being (see Psalm 37:11). They respect the Lord and know that he and his salvation are near.

  • The Lord does not tolerate foolish ways, such as those that resulted in the exile.

title: Psalms 85:10–13
content_html: In the renewed world of salvation, all the qualities of God’s care (love, truth, righteousness, and peace) come together. They transform creation and make it new (see Isaiah 32:15–20).

title: Psalms 85:13
content_html: Righteousness is like a messenger preparing the world for God’s coming in response to the prayers of his people (Psalms 85:4–7; 89:14).

title: Psalms 86:1
content_html: This is the only Psalm connected to David in the third book.

title: Psalms 86:1–4
content_html: God’s devoted servant prays for mercy. The psalmist sees himself as a member of the covenant community and as a citizen of the world.

title: Psalms 86:1–17
content_html:
The psalmist cries out to God for help and praises God’s character while facing evil (Psalm 86:14, 17). He says that all nations will one day worship the gracious and powerful God (Psalm 86:8–10). Because of this hope, the psalmist commits himself to live in a right and honest way (Psalm 86:11) and to praise the Lord (86:12–13).

The final section contrasts the behavior of evil people with the character of the Lord (Psalm 86:14–17).

title: Psalms 86:5
content_html: The psalmist lists God's perfect qualities (Exodus 34:6–7), especially God's readiness to forgive sin (see Psalms 32:5; 85:2; 145:7–10).

title: Psalms 86:5–7
content_html: The psalmist appeals to God to remain true to his character, which is the basis for hope.

title: Psalms 86:8–10
content_html: All the nations must submit themselves to the Lord and worship him (Psalms 45:17; 117:1; 148:7–11).

title: Psalms 86:11
content_html:
• The poet responds to the Lord by seeking faithful instruction in the truth.

  • A person with an undivided heart has integrity and wholeness (see Psalm 73:1).

title: Psalms 86:12–13
content_html: In anticipation of God’s rescue, the psalmist praises him and trusts in his goodness.

title: Psalms 86:14–17
content_html: Arrogant people exist both inside and outside the community. The psalmist asks God to show his character. God is so great that even a small glimpse of his goodness will silence the wicked.

title: Psalms 86:15
content_html:
God shows mercy to his people (Psalm 51:1). God is compassionate (Psalm 25:6). God is slow to become angry (Psalms 103:8; 145:8). God is full of loving devotion and faithfulness (Hebrew khesed and ’emeth; Psalm 143:1)

This verse repeats a theme first expressed in Exodus 34:7; Numbers 14:18 (compare Psalms 103:8; 145:8; Nehemiah 9:17; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2).

title: Psalms 87:1
content_html: Jerusalem was Israel’s capital and the location of the temple, God’s earthly palace (see Psalm 84).

title: Psalms 87:1–7
content_html: This psalm presents Jerusalem as the city of God, where people from all nations are counted as citizens.

title: Psalms 87:4
content_html:
• Zion is great, but it will be even greater as an international city where believers from all nations are citizens.

  • God keeps a record of everyone who confesses him as their Lord (Daniel 7:10; Revelation 13:8).

title: Psalms 87:5–6
content_html: Jerusalem becomes the international city where all God’s children are citizens.

title: Psalms 88:1
content_html:
• A Maskil: This may be a literary or musical term.

  • Heman the Ezrahite: Heman might have been:

    • a wise person from the time of King Solomon (1 Kings 4:31),

    • a descendant of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:6), or

    • a member of the Levite family responsible for singing at the temple (1 Chronicles 15:17; 2 Chronicles 5:12).

title: Psalms 88:1–5
content_html: The psalmist calls on the Lord for salvation.

title: Psalms 88:1–18
content_html: In this lament, the psalmist sees himself as cast out from God’s presence. At first, he calls on the Lord. When he finds no relief from his suffering, he presents three charges against God (Psalm 88:6–7, 8–12, 13–17).

title: Psalms 88:6–7
content_html:
• In his first complaint, the psalmist blames the Lord for his suffering. God’s overwhelming anger does not make sense to him.

  • The phrase the darkest of the depths might refer to:

    • the place of the dead (Psalm 143:3; see Lamentations 3:6), or

    • the deep sea (Psalms 68:22; 69:1–2).

title: Psalms 88:8–12
content_html: In his second charge, the psalmist claims that God has isolated him and caused his loneliness (compare Psalms 31:11; 38:11). In that culture, loneliness was a curse because relationships mattered more than personal success.

title: Psalms 88:13–17
content_html: In his third charge, the psalmist accuses the Lord of rejecting him. The force and apparent cruelty of the psalmist’s suffering overwhelm him.

title: Psalms 88:18
content_html: The psalmist restates his second and first charges.

title: Psalms 89:1
content_html: God’s loving devotion gives reason to celebrate forever.

title: Psalms 89:1–52
content_html: Book Three (Psalms 73–89) starts and ends with serious questions. Psalm 89 begins with praise for how the Lord established David’s throne and kingdom (89:1–37). But later the psalmist protests that the Lord seems to have rejected his covenant with David (Psalm 89:38–51; see 2 Samuel 7:8–16).

title: Psalms 89:title
content_html: Ethan the Ezrahite was a wise man (1 Kings 4:31; compare Psalm 88:1).

title: Psalms 89:3
content_html: The Lord has sworn by his own holiness (Psalm 89:35; see 110:4).

title: Psalms 89:3–4
content_html: The psalmist repeats God's covenant with David (see also Psalm 89:19–37).

title: Psalms 89:5
content_html: Those in heaven praise God's greatness (see Psalm 8:1) because they witness his faithfulness.

title: Psalms 89:5–8
content_html: God is the absolute standard of faithfulness, commitment, and order. The angelic beings and the heavens testify to his constancy.

title: Psalms 89:6
content_html: heavenly beings: Literally son of God (see study note on Psalm 29:1–2).

title: Psalms 89:9–10
content_html:
• The Lord rules over all parts of the created order, including evil.

  • You scattered Your enemies: The Lord will defeat (Luke 1:51) evil forces that oppose him (Psalm 2:1–3).

  • with Your mighty arm: This phrase represents God’s strength (Psalm 89:13).

title: Psalms 89:9–13
content_html: In this hymn, the psalmist celebrates God’s complete and powerful rule over nature.

title: Psalms 89:12
content_html: Mount Tabor was in northern Israel. Mount Hermon formed the extreme northern border of the nation.

title: Psalms 89:14–18
content_html: God rules wisely over creation. The qualities of righteousness, justice, and faithfulness fit the description of a wise ruler. The happy people who live under his rule enjoy his protection.

title: Psalms 89:15–16
content_html: Those who are wise worship God, walk in his light, rejoice in their relationship with him, and delight in his character.

title: Psalms 89:17–18
content_html: The Lord protects his people with the same power by which he established the created order.

title: Psalms 89:19–37
content_html: The Lord chose David as his son and king of Israel (see Psalm 2:4–6; 2 Samuel 7:8–16) to protect his people.

title: Psalms 89:20
content_html: I have anointed him: This is the verb from which comes anointed one (Hebrew mashiakh, which means “messiah”).

title: Psalms 89:26–29
content_html: The Lord adopted David as his son, appointed him as his commander over the nations, and promised to support him forever. Verse 26 recalls a verse from David’s song of praise (2 Samuel 22:47).

title: Psalms 89:27
content_html: David would become the greatest of earthly kings. The Hebrew word translated highest is the same word used for God as “Most High” (‘elyon).

title: Psalms 89:29
content_html: The Lord displays his faithfulness in the heavens, across his whole creation (see Psalm 89:2). In the same way, he confirms his commitment to the kings that would come from David's family.

title: Psalms 89:30–37
content_html: The Lord disciplines his children when they go astray, but he does not reject them. He knows they will break their trust and will need correction, yet he remains committed to keep his covenant. His faithfulness to David is like his faithfulness to the created world.

title: Psalms 89:35
content_html: holiness: The Lord is completely different from the created world (Psalm 22:3). His character is entirely pure. He acts according to his will (Psalm 115:3) and chooses to be faithful (89:1; 115:1). Therefore, he cannot lie.

title: Psalms 89:38
content_html: You are enraged: The psalmist is confused by the severity of God's anger.

title: Psalms 89:40
content_html: broken down all his walls: The Lord’s people are completely vulnerable.

title: Psalms 89:42
content_html: Although the Lord promised to strengthen David, he has made David’s enemies strong

title: Psalms 89:46–47
content_html: The psalmist wonders whether he will live long enough to see the Lord’s anger end. Human life is short and passes quickly before the Lord (Psalm 90:4, 10).

title: Psalms 89:50
content_html: The phrase Your servants refers to the community loyal to the Lord.

title: Psalms 89:52
content_html: This doxology (a short praise to God) ends Book Three of the Psalms (Psalms 73–89). It was not meant to be read as part of Psalm 89. Its words of praise stand in strong contrast to the pain and questions expressed in Psalm 89. Compare Psalm 41:13; 106:48.

title: Psalms 90:1
content_html:
• Moses led God's people from slavery in Egypt to Mount Sinai. There, he received the law and established God's covenant with Israel.

  • the man of God: Moses had a special relationship with God (Deuteronomy 33:1).

title: Psalms 90:1–2
content_html: The Lord is the dwelling place for the godly. He has provided shelter for many generations. The word translated as dwelling place is rare in the Psalms. It describes a safe place where the Lord meets and cares for those in need (Psalms 68:5; 71:3).

title: Psalms 90:1–17
content_html:
Although the Lord’s people have lived in him for many generations, their sin creates distance between them and their eternal Lord. The Lord’s anger against human sin can seem to last a lifetime. Even then, it is only a small sign of his full displeasure with sin. This sad situation leads the community to seek the Lord’s light.

The first step is to ask the Lord to restore them because of his compassion and faithful love (Psalm 90:13–14). He alone can make his servants glad. He alone can bless the generations that come after them (Psalm 90:16–17).

title: Psalms 90:2
content_html:
• The Lord is the eternal God.

  • The Lord created the earth.

title: Psalms 90:3–6
content_html: Unlike God, humans are mortal and temporary.

title: Psalms 90:7–10
content_html: Sinners cannot escape God’s anger against sin because the Lord sees their sins. Their lives pass quickly and are full of suffering.

title: Psalms 90:9–10
content_html:
• A groan of sorrow (Ezekiel 2:10) represents a life facing the consequences of sin.

  • Humans create trouble (see Psalm 10:6) and receive pain as a result.

title: Psalms 90:11–12
content_html: In the search for wisdom, no one can fully understand the Lord (see Isaiah 40:13). The right human response to the Lord is godly fear (see Psalm 60:4). The Lord alone can teach humans to follow the path of wisdom (Psalm 25:4–6).

title: Psalms 90:13
content_html: The phrase Your servants refers to the community of those loyal to the Lord.

title: Psalms 90:13–17
content_html: The psalmist prays for the Lord’s favor and restoration.

title: Psalms 90:15
content_html: The psalmist calls upon the Lord to give the people gladness to replace their mourning (see Psalm 92:4–5).

title: Psalms 90:16
content_html: The children represent future generations in contrast to the generations past (Psalm 90:1).

title: Psalms 90:17
content_html: Humans waste their efforts unless the Lord makes them successful (Psalm 44:3).

title: Psalms 91:1
content_html: The Most High is an ancient title that expresses the Lord’s exalted status as the ruler and protector of the godly (see Genesis 14:19).

title: Psalms 91:1–2
content_html: The Lord is an ancient shelter (see Psalm 90:1), open to those who seek refuge in him.

title: Psalms 91:1–16
content_html: This wisdom psalm expresses confidence in the Almighty God, who provides a shelter for those who take refuge in him. They receive redemption, life, and glory from the Lord, who loves and cares for those who seek him.

title: Psalms 91:3
content_html: God protects the godly from deadly disease and even a destructive word.

title: Psalms 91:3–4
content_html: The psalmist invites the godly to trust in the Lord and find protection in him.

title: Psalms 91:4
content_html: The shield might refer to a large shield that covers the body. The rampart likely refers to a smaller shield, though the exact meaning of the Hebrew word is uncertain.

title: Psalms 91:5–8
content_html: The godly should not be afraid, because the Lord watches over them. These promises do not guarantee an escape from trouble, but they create an expectation of the Lord’s goodness.

title: Psalms 91:6
content_html: The word pestilence here means any kind of suffering or trouble.

title: Psalms 91:9–13
content_html: The poet again invites the godly to seek refuge in the Lord and enjoy the benefits of divine protection.

title: Psalms 91:11
content_html: He will command His angels: Satan quoted these words when he tempted Jesus (Matthew 4:6; Luke 4:10–11). However, God makes this promise for those who obey him (Psalm 91:14), not for those who arrogantly test him (Matthew 4:4, 7).

title: Psalms 91:14
content_html: Those who love the Lord will follow his commands (Deuteronomy 30:20; 1 John 5:3) and look for his protection (Psalms 91:1; 145:18).

title: Psalms 91:14–16
content_html: The godly can trust the Lord’s protection and provision.

title: Psalms 91:15
content_html:
• The Lord promises to answer the prayers of his people.

  • The Psalms often mention people honoring the Lord (Psalm 50:15). Here, God promises to honor the faithful who risk everything for him (Psalms 62:7; 71:21; 84:11).

title: Psalms 91:16
content_html: A long life on earth represents just a small part of the Lord’s goodness and eternal friendship (Psalm 23:6).

title: Psalms 92:1
content_html:
In Jewish tradition, each day of the week has an assigned psalm:

  • Sunday (Psalm 24)

  • Monday (Psalm 48)

  • Tuesday (Psalm 82)

  • Wednesday (Psalm 94)

  • Thursday (Psalm 81)

  • Friday (Psalm 93)

  • Saturday, which is the Sabbath (Psalm 92)

This is the only psalm that indicates this use in its title.

title: Psalms 92:1–3
content_html: The psalmist offers praise and thanksgiving for God’s loving devotion and faithfulness.

title: Psalms 92:1–15
content_html:
This psalm begins with thanksgiving and praise. The Lord, the Most High, is fair and just in judging the godly and the wicked. The godly will have a bright and strong future, while the wicked will die.

Wise people see and understand the Lord's actions, which show his justice (Psalm 92:6–7, 11, 15). The wicked, like grass, are quickly destroyed (Psalm 92:7–10).

title: Psalms 92:4–5
content_html: The Lord acts and the psalmist responds in praise (Psalm 92:4). God’s actions come from his thoughts (Psalm 92:5).

title: Psalms 92:4–7
content_html: Fools perish because they do not understand the Lord’s great works and his eternal plans.

title: Psalms 92:5–6
content_html: The Lord’s thoughts confuse those who lack understanding or act foolishly, and those who do not seek wisdom (Proverbs 1:22; 10:23).

title: Psalms 92:7
content_html: Even when the ungodly appear to thrive (Psalm 73:3–5), they have no future (92:9; see 1:6; 90:5–6; Isaiah 40:6–8).

title: Psalms 92:8
content_html:
The Lord is above the world of human experience. From this exalted place, he rules and judges (Psalm 7:7). From there, he:

  • celebrates his victories (Psalm 68:18; see 47),

  • comes to rescue the needy (Psalm 102:19),

  • brings an end to the wicked (Psalm 92:7–8), and

  • receives praise (Psalm 148:1).

title: Psalms 92:8–10
content_html: The glory of the Lord is the reason the poet receives new strength and honor. It is also the reason the enemy will soon be defeated.

title: Psalms 92:10
content_html: like that of a wild ox: The Lord allows the psalmist to express his joy (Psalm 92:8). Because of this, the psalmist is strong and will overcome his enemies.

title: Psalms 92:11–15
content_html: The psalmist understands the Lord’s mighty acts. He shares in the glory of the wise of heart.

title: Psalms 92:12
content_html: Carvings of palm trees (1 Kings 6:29, 32, 35; 7:36) decorated the temple. Builders used many cedars (1 Kings 5:6). The trees represent the godly, who are planted in the very courts of the temple.

title: Psalms 92:13
content_html: The temple area consisted of the Lord’s own house and the courts that surrounded it.

title: Psalms 92:15
content_html: in Him there is no unrighteousness: The Lord is always just. He rightly judges everyone (see Zephaniah 3:5).

title: Psalms 93:1
content_html: robed in majesty: The Lord stands victorious both before and after battle (see Psalm 45:3).

title: Psalms 93:1–5
content_html: The everlasting Lord gloriously establishes his kingship (see Psalms 47, 99–100).

title: Psalms 93:1–100.5
content_html:
These psalms challenge the doubts created by the exile (see Psalm 89) and answer the questions asked in Psalm 90:11, 13. The Lord has established a kingdom (Psalm 93) that is characterized by:

  • punishment of the wicked (Psalm 94),

  • respectful obedience among his people (Psalm 95),

  • justice for the poor (Psalm 96),

  • celebration in Zion (Psalm 97),

  • salvation for Israel (Psalm 98),

  • holiness (Psalm 99), and

  • praise (Psalm 100).

title: Psalms 93:3–4
content_html: God’s victory over natural chaos gives the psalmist confidence that the Lord will also defeat his present enemies (compare Jeremiah 46:8; 47:2).

title: Psalms 93:5
content_html:
• Through his stable rule and royal laws, the Lord orders the world.

  • Your house: The house from which God reigns might be the Lord’s heavenly palace or the earthly temple.

title: Psalms 94:1–7
content_html: After the community suffers at the hands of the wicked (Psalm 94:5–7), they pray for the Lord to bring justice (see Psalm 7:11; 9:12).

title: Psalms 94:1–23
content_html: This plea for the Lord’s justice includes both communal and individual expressions of sorrow (Psalm 94:1–7, 16–23). The psalmist speaks about the pain and suffering caused by the wicked. He also describes the comfort and stability of the Lord’s protection. He calls on the Lord to avenge the needy against the arrogant and foolish, he rebukes such people for their foolishness (Psalm 94:8–11), and speaks a blessing on the wise (94:12–15).

title: Psalms 94:2
content_html: When the Lord's enemies rise against him and his people (Psalm 3:7; 93:3), he must rise and punish the enemy (see 79:12; Isaiah 65:6).

title: Psalms 94:6
content_html: widow … foreigner … fatherless: The Lord’s law protects those in need (Deuteronomy 24:17; 27:19), but they remain vulnerable in society.

title: Psalms 94:7
content_html: The wicked think that they can get away with their evil acts and speech, foolishly assuming that God is not looking or does not care.

title: Psalms 94:8–11
content_html: The writer of the psalm corrects people for acting foolishly.

title: Psalms 94:9
content_html: It makes no sense for people who have ears and eyes to think that their Creator is deaf and blind.

title: Psalms 94:10
content_html: discipline: This word can also mean "admonished" (Psalm 2:10), "discipline" (6:1; 94:12), or "instructs" (16:7). The Lord disciplines his people to improve their character (Proverbs 3:11–12; Hebrews 12:6–11). He punishes his enemies by giving them what they deserve (Psalm 1:6; Romans 6:23).

title: Psalms 94:11
content_html: While the Lord’s thoughts are deep, people’s thoughts are worthless (compare Psalm 92:5; 1 Corinthians 3:20).

title: Psalms 94:12–15
content_html: Because the Lord is faithful to his character, he rewards those who live by his wise instruction. He makes them happy, just as he promised.

title: Psalms 94:13
content_html: God’s relief includes his comfort and rescue.

title: Psalms 94:14
content_html: Even when it seems that he has, the Lord will not reject his people.

title: Psalms 94:16
content_html: Only God has the power to stand up to the wicked (see Psalm 2:2).

title: Psalms 94:16–23
content_html: The psalmist resumes their prayer (see Psalm 94:1–7) with a desperate plea for the Lord's justice. It ends with the hope that evil will cease.

title: Psalms 94:18–19
content_html: The psalmist feels like he is slipping, but God promises to stand with him (Psalm 94:12–13; see 15:5; 93:1).

title: Psalms 95:1–11
content_html: This call to worship urges the people to respond to the Lord, the Shepherd, and to follow him faithfully. The previous generation, who refused to obey, did not receive the blessings of the promised land.

title: Psalms 95:3
content_html: above all gods: The psalmist argues against idol worship and pagan beliefs (Psalms 96:4–6; 135:15–18; see also Isaiah 46:5–7).

title: Psalms 95:3–5
content_html: The Lord is the great King over the whole universe.

title: Psalms 95:4–5
content_html: All creation exists because of the Lord alone, not because of many gods.

title: Psalms 95:6–7
content_html:
• The great King cares for his people like a shepherd cares for sheep (Isaiah 40:11).

  • The Lord, our Maker, the Creator of heaven and earth, also created a people for himself (Isaiah 45:9–12; 51:12–16).

title: Psalms 95:8
content_html: To harden your hearts means to be stubborn and resist the Lord's grace (see Numbers 20:2–13; see also Matthew 13:3–23; John 12:37–43; Ephesians 4:17–32; Hebrews 3:6–19; 4:6–11).

title: Psalms 95:8–11
content_html: If people want to enter into the Lord’s rest, they need to respond immediately (see Hebrews 3:7–4:11).

title: Psalms 95:9
content_html: Israel's ancestors saw the Lord's wonders but did not understand their importance (see Psalm 92:5–6).

title: Psalms 95:11
content_html:
• The generation of Israel that wandered in the wilderness for 40 years did not enter the place of rest that Israel enjoyed after conquering the land (1 Kings 8:56; see Deuteronomy 12:9). The Lord promised them the land and gave it to them (Isaiah 28:12), but they did not obey him (Isaiah 28:1). This warning reminds every generation of the need to obey with faith.

  • The rest that God provides includes peace, provision, and protection (Psalm 132:14–18). The author of Hebrews challenged a new generation to enter the rest provided by Jesus Christ (Hebrews 3:7–4:13).

title: Psalms 96:1
content_html: Three psalms begin by urging people to sing a new song (Psalms 96, 98, 149). The song celebrates the Lord's universal kingship (see Psalm 33:3).

title: Psalms 96:1–13
content_html:
• This psalm establishes the Lord’s universal kingship (see Psalm 93) and prepares for the coming of the Lord to bring justice and righteousness to the world. The psalmist invites everyone to worship the Lord (Psalm 96:1–3), the glorious Creator of the earth (96:4–6), because only he deserves worship (96:7–9). The universe joins in praise as the righteous Judge establishes a new order (Psalm 96:10–13).

  • This psalm (along with Psalm 105:1–15; 106:1, 47–48) is included in David’s song of praise recorded in 1 Chronicles 16:1–43.

title: Psalms 96:4
content_html: great is the LORD … greatly to be praised: The poet praises the Lord above all gods (Psalm 95:3) and nations (99:2). The Lord inspires more awe than anyone or anything else (Psalm 76:7, 12; 89:7; 99:3; 111:9). He is beyond human possession or comprehension. The story of what he does to redeem his people causes them to praise him.

title: Psalms 96:4–6
content_html: The gods of the nations are as nothing before the glorious and awesome Creator of the earth.

title: Psalms 96:5
content_html: The Lord made the heavens, an even greater task than creating the earth. The idols, made by human hands (Psalm 115:4), created nothing.

title: Psalms 96:6
content_html: Splendor and majesty: The Lord is glorious (Psalm 96:6; 104:1; 148:13), and so are his works in nature (8:1) and in redemption (111:3; 145:5).

title: Psalms 96:7
content_html:
• families of the nations: Families and clans represent subdivisions of the nations (see Genesis 12:3).

  • The nations must respond to the Lord’s glory (Psalms 8:1; 19:1–4; 24:7).

title: Psalms 96:8
content_html: The courts of God’s sanctuary were usually not open to non-Jewish people. In this psalm, the nations of the world (people who are not Jews) are invited to come in (Psalm 65:4; 84:2, 10; 100:4; 116:19; 135:2; Isaiah 1:12).

title: Psalms 96:9
content_html: Worshipers should tremble in awe (Psalm 96:4; see 18:7–15; 97:4) at the splendor of the Lord’s great power and holiness (Hebrews 12:25).

title: Psalms 96:10
content_html:
• The Lord’s coming means the end of injustice. He rules fairly (Psalm 9:8; 96:13; 98:9). He brings a time of peace and order like the world has not known since Adam and Eve lived in the garden of Eden.

  • The Lord’s rule over the world stands on the strong pillars of “righteousness and justice” (Psalm 89:14), so it cannot be shaken (compare 11:3).

title: Psalms 96:11–13
content_html: The psalmist urges all of creation to respond with joy to the news of the Lord’s coming. Compare a similar theme in Psalm 98:7–9.

title: Psalms 96:12
content_html: All of nature will experience renewal in God’s presence (Psalms 65:13; 72:6, 16; Isaiah 44:23; 55:12; Romans 8:21–22).

title: Psalms 97:1
content_html: The distant shores represent the most remote parts of the world.

title: Psalms 97:1–12
content_html: God is greatly exalted over the gods and over all the earth. Awe-inspiring phenomena accompany his coming. Even the created order is visibly affected and the wicked perish, but the people of Zion love his coming and rejoice.

title: Psalms 97:2–4
content_html: Clouds … Fire … lightning: This language shows that the Lord is coming to judge.

title: Psalms 97:5–7
content_html: All creation responds to the coming of its King. He alone deserves worship.

title: Psalms 97:6
content_html:
• When the Lord comes into the world, the heavens announce his righteousness (Psalm 50:6).

  • Every nation will see the clear display of the Lord’s glory (see Psalm 8:1; 19:1–4a; 24:7; Isaiah 40:5).

title: Psalms 97:7
content_html: At the Lord’s coming, when every god must bow to him, those who worship other gods will face their own foolishness (see Psalm 95:3–5).

title: Psalms 97:8–12
content_html: Jerusalem rejoices at the news of the Lord’s coming, for he brings justice and rescues the upright of heart.

title: Psalms 97:11
content_html:
Godly people who live with integrity (see Proverbs 2:7, 21; 3:32; 11:6, 11; 14:2) have hearts that are right before the Lord. They maintain a relationship with the Lord and live rightly with others (Psalms 94:15; 125:4).

The wicked plan to destroy the godly (Psalm 11:2), but the Lord rescues them (11:1; 36:10). Then the sorrows of the godly turn into joy and praise (Psalms 32:11; 64:10; 111:1; 119:7).

title: Psalms 98:1–9
content_html:
This psalm celebrates the Lord as King. The Lord is the true King of Israel, its champion and savior. His people sing a new song to celebrate his victory and his faithfulness to them.

The Lord shows his righteous rule to all the nations of the earth. One day, this will lead to a worldwide kingdom of justice (Psalm 98:2, 9). Because of this, all the earth should worship and rejoice together, for the Lord is coming (Psalm 98:4–6).

title: Psalms 98:2–3
content_html: The Lord makes known his righteousness and faithfulness (see also Isaiah 52:10).

title: Psalms 98:4–6
content_html: The psalmist invites the whole earth to worship the King (see Psalm 96:1).

title: Psalms 98:6
content_html: The temple musicians played trumpets to gather people for meetings (compare Psalm 81:3).

title: Psalms 98:7–9
content_html: The psalm speaks as if all creation rejoices when the righteous Judge comes (see Psalms 83:3–4; 96:11–13).

title: Psalms 99:1
content_html:
• It is appropriate to tremble and quake in response to the Lord’s holiness (see Psalm 96:9).

  • The throne between the cherubim refers to the cover of the ark of the Testimony (Exodus 25:17–22).

title: Psalms 99:1–5
content_html: The Lord is the holy and righteous King (Isaiah 6:1–5). Mount Zion, where the temple of the Lord is located, metaphorically stands as the highest point on earth (Psalm 113:4; Isaiah 2:2; 6:1). From this high place, the "great King" (Psalm 47:2) can see, rule over, and receive praise from all nations and peoples.

title: Psalms 99:1–9
content_html: The psalmist calls the nations to worship the Lord, the king of the earth who rules from Zion.

title: Psalms 99:4
content_html:
The qualities of the Lord’s rule include:

  • strength,

  • justice,

  • love,

  • fairness, and

  • righteousness (see Psalms 93:4; 97:2, 6–7, 12; 98:2–3, 9; 138:6).

title: Psalms 99:6
content_html: Moses and Samuel were great models and leaders who had prayed on behalf of the Lord’s people (Exodus 32:31–32; 1 Samuel 12:23).

title: Psalms 99:6–8
content_html: God showed favor by responding to his people in the past, while holding sinners accountable.

title: Psalms 99:7
content_html: The Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron from the pillar of cloud (Exodus 33:9; Numbers 12:5–6). For a while, the people responded to the revelation.

title: Psalms 100:1–5
content_html: This psalm calls all nations to come to Jerusalem to worship the Lord and acknowledge his goodness to Israel.

title: Psalms 100:3
content_html:
• The Lord is the one who determines who his people are.

  • the sheep of His pasture: God will guide and protect his people (Psalm 23:1, 4).

title: Psalms 100:4
content_html: Gates and courts were part of the temple in Jerusalem.

title: Psalms 101:1–8
content_html: This song of praise expresses a commitment to live with integrity and to avoid evil. Jesus meets this ideal.

title: Psalms 101:2
content_html:
• in my house: This commitment to integrity might refer to private life at home. Some interpreters see my house as the king’s palace. The commitment would then refer to the policies of his administration.

  • Living with integrity means choosing a wise lifestyle.

title: Psalms 101:4–5
content_html:
• The psalmist hates what the Lord hates (Proverbs 6:16–19; see Psalms 5:5; 11:4–5).

  • Perverse ideas come from corrupt human hearts (compare Psalm 18:26).

title: Psalms 101:6
content_html: The psalmist surrounds himself with people who are also committed to integrity (Psalm 101:2).

title: Psalms 101:7–8
content_html: The psalmist promises to remove all evil from his house and the city of the Lord, which represents the entire kingdom.

title: Psalms 102:1–2
content_html: The psalmist asks the Lord not to turn away (or hide your face). Israel experienced God turning his face away when they were exiled.

title: Psalms 102:1–28
content_html: A weak and suffering person prays for the Lord's mercy on Jerusalem and for his presence with its people.

title: Psalms 102:3
content_html:
• Smoke is an image of the temporary nature of life (Psalms 37:20; 68:2).

  • The psalmist’s bones represent his whole being (see Psalms 6:2; 34:19–22).

title: Psalms 102:4
content_html: Like withered grass, the psalmist senses his life fading away.

title: Psalms 102:5
content_html: skin hangs on my bones: The psalmist has become very thin due to his deep sorrow (see Psalm 102:9; 109:24).

title: Psalms 102:6
content_html: Owls often live in deserted places, such as ruined cities. Because of this, the Bible sometimes uses the owl as a sign of ruin and desolation (Isaiah 34:11, 15).

title: Psalms 102:7–8
content_html: The psalmist cannot sleep (see Psalm 63:6–8). Like a lone bird on a housetop, he feels alone and separated from both friends (see Psalm 88:8) and enemies.

title: Psalms 102:10
content_html: The psalmist blames the Lord for his troubles.

title: Psalms 102:11
content_html: The psalmist feels that life is empty and short (Psalms 102:4; 109:23; 144:4).

title: Psalms 102:12
content_html: forever … to all generations: Human life is short and fragile (Psalm 102:3–11). In contrast, the Lord remains forever and rules through every generation.

title: Psalms 102:12–17
content_html: The topic changes from the psalmist’s troubles to the Lord’s glory. A vision of the Lord’s compassionate kingship gives him confidence in Zion’s future.

title: Psalms 102:13
content_html: Now is the time for the Lord to restore Jerusalem from exile (see Psalm 75:2; Jeremiah 29:10; Daniel 9:1–19).

title: Psalms 102:15–16
content_html: When the Lord arises (Psalm 102:13), the nations will tremble with fear because they will see his glory (see Psalm 8:1; 19:1–4a; 24:7). Rebuilding Zion and the temple shows God's glory and fulfills this promise. An even greater fulfillment will come with the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21).

title: Psalms 102:17
content_html: The people of Israel were very poor and vulnerable (destitute) during the exile.

title: Psalms 102:18
content_html: Let this be written: The written record serves as a reminder of the Lord's goodness.

title: Psalms 102:18–22
content_html: The Lord will show mercy. Both Jews and Gentiles will worship him.

title: Psalms 102:19
content_html: His sanctuary refers to God's holy place in heaven (see Psalm 92:8–10).

title: Psalms 102:22
content_html: Many people (both Jews and non-Jews) will worship the Lord together (see Psalm 96:7, 9; 106:48; Isaiah 2:2–4; 43:10–13; 57:15; 60:3–7).

title: Psalms 102:23–28
content_html: Although the psalmist suffers in the present, he bases his hope for the future on the Lord’s past faithfulness.

title: Psalms 102:24
content_html: The psalmist compares his short life (Psalm 102:23) to God's eternal existence (compare 90:4).

title: Psalms 102:25–27
content_html:
• wear out like a garment: Unlike the eternal Creator, the created world will wear out like a piece of clothing (see Isaiah 40:28).

  • The writer to the Hebrews describes this passage as having been addressed to Jesus, the Son (Hebrews 1:10–12).

title: Psalms 102:28
content_html: The Lord’s presence provides the basis for the security and happiness of his people (Psalm 23:6).

title: Psalms 103:1–2
content_html: all that is within me, bless His holy name: Psalms 103 and 104 both start and end with this promise (103:1–2, 22; 104:1, 35; see also 146:1).

title: Psalms 103:1–6
content_html: The psalmist offers praise for the Lord’s goodness.

title: Psalms 103:1–22
content_html: This psalm celebrates the Lord’s perfection, compassion, forgiveness, and goodness.

title: Psalms 103:7–8
content_html: The Lord revealed himself to Moses in special ways to strengthen his relationship with his people (see Exodus 34:6–7; Numbers 14:18).

title: Psalms 103:9
content_html: If the Lord were to constantly accuse or remain angry forever, no one would be left (see Isaiah 54:7–8; 57:16).

title: Psalms 103:11–12
content_html: God’s love and forgiveness have no limits and are complete.

title: Psalms 103:13
content_html: The Lord is like a father to his children. This idea is central to the teachings of Jesus about God's fatherhood (see Matthew 5:43–48; 6:1; 10:19–20; 12:50; Luke 6:36; 12:29–32; John 8:31–59; 15:1–8; see also 2 Samuel 7:14; Jeremiah 3:19; 31:9; Malachi 1:6; 2 Corinthians 6:16–18).

title: Psalms 103:14–16
content_html: The Lord compassionately cares for weak humans (see Psalm 37:2; 90:5; 92:7; 102:4; Isaiah 40:6–8).

title: Psalms 103:17–19
content_html: from everlasting to everlasting the loving devotion of the LORD extends: The Lord’s kingdom is everlasting, so he commits himself to his people across many generations.

title: Psalms 103:18
content_html: To obey God means to be faithful and loyal to him.

title: Psalms 103:20–21
content_html: The psalmist urges the Lord's angels, servants, and messengers to praise the Lord.

title: Psalms 103:22
content_html:
• God's kingdom extends over all things, so all things are called to praise the Lord.

  • The last phrase is similar to Psalm 103:1–2.

title: Psalms 104:1
content_html:
• Bless the LORD, O my soul!: Psalms 103 and 104 both begin and end with this commitment to praise (103:1–2, 22; 104:1, 35).

  • You are very great: These words praise God for rescuing his people (see also Psalms 35:27; 40:16; 70:4).

title: Psalms 104:1–35
content_html: This creation hymn (see also Psalms 8, 33, 145) praises God's goodness and greatness. The psalmist thinks about the current world, the original creation, and a future new creation. He views both creations as wonderfully and wisely made (compare Psalm 139:14), as the work of the Lord's Spirit (104:30; Genesis 1:2; 2 Corinthians 3:6).

title: Psalms 104:2–4
content_html: The psalmist refers to two days of creation (see Genesis 1:1–8), when God created the heavens and the light.

title: Psalms 104:3
content_html: This poetic description pictures the clouds as beams that support the heavens.

title: Psalms 104:4
content_html:
• Wind and lightning (flames of fire) are described as God’s messengers. The Lord controls the wind, rain, and storms and uses them as he desires (Psalm 77:17–18; 78:26; 135:7; 148:5–6).

  • The psalmist refers to the third day of creation (Genesis 1:9–13). The Lord is master of water and earth.

title: Psalms 104:6
content_html: At first, water covered the earth.

title: Psalms 104:7
content_html: The Lord created dry ground on day three of creation (Genesis 1:9–13).

title: Psalms 104:8
content_html: The earth was made because God willed it.

title: Psalms 104:9
content_html: The Lord set a firm boundary for the seas to protect the land. This shows that the Lord rules both the sea and the land. This idea also challenged the beliefs of the Canaanites, who celebrated Baal as the god who defeated the sea (see Psalm 29).

title: Psalms 104:9–10
content_html: The Lord rules over all waters, including seas, rivers, and springs on the land. He brought order to the waters that were once chaotic. As a result, the earth became a rich place for animals and people. Water refreshes the ground and provides life for birds and other animals.

title: Psalms 104:11–18
content_html: The psalmist celebrates animal, plant, and human life, as well as the Lord’s abundant provision for all of it (Genesis 1:9–30).

title: Psalms 104:14
content_html: for man to cultivate: People are the managers of the created order, and they receive nourishment from the work of their hands.

title: Psalms 104:15
content_html: Both wine and olive oil can be misused (compare Proverbs 21:17), but the Lord gives them as gifts to improve life. Life is sustained by basic food, represented by bread.

title: Psalms 104:17–18
content_html:
• The storks migrated from northern Europe and stayed in Palestine during the winter.

  • rock badgers: These animals are about the size of a rabbit and live in rocky areas.

title: Psalms 104:19
content_html:
• the moon to mark the seasons: Ancient Israel based its calendar on the cycles of the moon.

  • the sun knows when to set: This poetic description reflects what people normally see. The sun goes down at a regular time each evening.

title: Psalms 104:19–23
content_html: The psalmist rejoices in the order of night and day, marking day four of creation (Genesis 1:14–19). This cycle gives all creatures an opportunity to live.

title: Psalms 104:20–23
content_html: Cycles of darkness and dawn mark the days on earth.

title: Psalms 104:24–26
content_html: The Lord’s works testify to his wisdom. All parts fit together (land and sea, night and day, land creatures and sea creatures). The Lord’s wisdom makes life orderly and wonderful.

title: Psalms 104:25
content_html: teeming with creatures beyond number: The ocean is full of all kinds of life (see Genesis 1:20–21). This shows the Lord's creative work.

title: Psalms 104:26
content_html:
• ships pass: Commercial ships used for sea trade were one of the wonders of the ancient Near East (see Isaiah 2:16; 23:1, 3).

  • Leviathan: The psalmist describes this feared sea creature as something the Lord created and controls.

title: Psalms 104:27–30
content_html: All living things depend on the Lord, who provides what they need and who has power over life and death (compare Matthew 6:11).

title: Psalms 104:29–30
content_html: When You send Your Spirit: Life on earth is only possible as the Lord’s gift, symbolized by his breath (Hebrew ruakh, which means "breath, wind, or spirit"). The Lord’s Spirit is the agent of renewal (Isaiah 32:15–20).

title: Psalms 104:31
content_html: The Lord reveals his presence in the created order (see Psalms 8:1; 19:1–4; 24:7) and delights in his new creation (see Isaiah 65:19).

title: Psalms 104:31–35
content_html: The psalmist moves from describing the known creation to anticipating the new creation, when the Lord will renew the created order into a world without sinners.

title: Psalms 104:32
content_html: The trembling earth and the smoking mountains represent the Lord’s coming in judgment against sinners and salvation for his faithful people (see Psalm 18:7–15).

title: Psalms 104:35
content_html:
• The Lord will remove all forms of evil that defile his creation.

  • The final phrase returns to the opening words of Psalm 104:1 (see also 103:1–2, 22; 146:1).

title: Psalms 105:1–5
content_html: The poet begins with a call to give thanks (see also Psalms 106:1; 107:1; 118:1; 136:1). The other psalms continue with the refrain "for He is good!" The rest of Psalm 105 tells of God's goodness.

title: Psalms 105:1–45
content_html:
• This hymn praises the Lord’s faithfulness to Abraham and his descendants in all their journeys:

    • from Ur to Canaan (Psalm 105:12),

    • to Egypt (Psalm 105:17, 23),

    • through the wilderness (Psalm 105:37, 41), and

    • back to Canaan (Psalm 105:44).

    These events fulfilled the Lord's promise to Abraham (Psalm 105:9, 42), which was the basis for Israel’s very existence. Joseph, whose suffering turned into glory, serves as a model of encouragement for Israel (Psalm 105:16–25).

  • Psalm 105:1–15 (along with 96; 106:1, 47–48) is included in David’s song of praise recorded in 1 Chronicles 16.

title: Psalms 105:6–11
content_html: The story of Israel is based on the Lord’s commitment to Abraham. Even when a question arises about the future of God’s covenant with David (Psalm 89), God’s covenant with Abraham stands, as does the spiritual bond between God and Israel, Abraham’s descendants.

title: Psalms 105:8
content_html: The covenant is the Lord’s unbreakable promise to be the Father of Abraham and his descendants (see Genesis 12:1–9; 15:1–21).

title: Psalms 105:11
content_html: God promised the land of Canaan to Abraham's descendants as their special possession. They could live there as God's people (Psalm 105:44–45; Genesis 12:7; 15:7–21).

title: Psalms 105:12–15
content_html: The Lord protected Israel’s patriarchs, the early fathers of the nation.

title: Psalms 105:15
content_html: Abraham was one of the nation's prophets (Genesis 20:7).

title: Psalms 105:16–22
content_html: These verses tell the story of Joseph (see Genesis 37–41).

title: Psalms 105:19
content_html:
• his prediction: Literally, his word.

  • the word of the LORD proved him right: See Genesis 39:6–12.

title: Psalms 105:23
content_html: Egypt (Hebrew Mitsrayim) is called the land of Ham.

title: Psalms 105:23–25
content_html: These verses describe Israel's suffering in Egypt (see Exodus 1:1–14).

title: Psalms 105:26–36
content_html: The psalmist tells the story of Moses and the plagues in Egypt (see Exodus 7:20–12:30).

title: Psalms 105:37
content_html: none … stumbled: The Lord protected all of his people (compare Psalm 9:3).

title: Psalms 105:37–45
content_html: The exodus and the conquest of Canaan fulfilled God's promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Psalm 105:9–10). Unlike Psalm 78, the poet describes the Lord's goodness without mentioning Israel's history of rebellion (compare Psalm 106).

title: Psalms 105:39
content_html: The Lord’s presence was as evident to all as a fire in the darkness (Exodus 13:22).

title: Psalms 105:42–45
content_html: The story of redemption is also the story of the Lord’s faithfulness.

title: Psalms 105:44
content_html: Canaan was a land of pagan nations (see Psalm 111:6). The psalmist might also be thinking about God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 24:60) regarding the lands given to Isaac (Genesis 26:3).

title: Psalms 105:45
content_html: The Lord planned for Israel to show who he is by obeying him. Israel was to be his example among the nations.

title: Psalms 106:1
content_html:
Hallelujah! is a Hebrew word (haleluyah) that means “Praise the Lord.” This phrase appears in four small groups in the psalms:

  • Psalm 104–106

  • Psalm 111–113

  • Psalm 115–117

  • Psalm 146–150

title: Psalms 106:1–48
content_html:
• The Israelite community in exile confessed the Lord’s goodness and justice and their own past sins. From their humble state in exile, they asked for redemption and restoration. They hoped to be gathered back from all the places the Lord had scattered them in his anger.

    The psalm ends with a memory of the Lord’s past mercy and hope for renewed mercy (Psalm 106:44–48). Israel failed, but the Lord remains constant.

  • Verses 1, 47–48 (along with Psalms 96; 105:1–15) are included in David’s song of praise recorded in 1 Chronicles 16:1–43.

title: Psalms 106:2–3
content_html: Wise people who practice justice and righteousness receive encouragement to proclaim the Lord’s great acts.

title: Psalms 106:4–5
content_html: The poet prays individually for the Lord’s favor before leading the community confession that follows.

title: Psalms 106:6
content_html: The story of Israel's redemption includes their sinful actions from the time of their ancestors to now (Psalm 106:43). The themes of sin and God's anger towards sin start and end Book Four (Psalms 90:1–9; 106:6, 23, 43).

title: Psalms 106:6–13
content_html: The community joins together to confess their sins and their ancestors’ many acts of unbelief.

title: Psalms 106:9
content_html: Red Sea: See Exodus 14:15–31.

title: Psalms 106:13
content_html: they … failed to wait: God’s impatient people did not trust him to work according to his will (for example, see Exodus 15:24; 16:2–3; 17:1–3; Numbers 14:26–45; 21:4–9).

title: Psalms 106:15
content_html: He … sent a wasting disease: See Exodus 32:35 and Numbers 11:33.

title: Psalms 106:16–18
content_html:
• The people became jealous because they wanted the same role as the priests God had chosen (see Numbers 16:1–35).

  • The earth opened up: See Numbers 16:28–33.

title: Psalms 106:19–23
content_html: they … worshiped a molten image: The people forgot their Savior and worshiped idols (see Exodus 32:1–6).

title: Psalms 106:22
content_html: the land of Ham: This means Egypt (Psalm 106:21).

title: Psalms 106:24–25
content_html:
• The people disobeyed God’s command when they refused to enter the pleasant land (the promised land (see Jeremiah 3:19).

  • they did not believe His promise: See Numbers 13:25–14:12.

title: Psalms 106:26–27
content_html: God’s judgment when Israel refused to obey was death and exile (Numbers 14:26–30). These judgments came because the people rejected God’s word (Psalm 106:24–25) and provoked the Lord's anger (106:28–46).

title: Psalms 106:28
content_html: they yoked themselves to Baal of Peor: See Numbers 25:3; Deuteronomy 4:3.

title: Psalms 106:28–39
content_html: When the Israelites entered the land, they brought their idol worship with them. Their behavior stayed the same, and they angered the Lord with their actions.

title: Psalms 106:30–33
content_html: Phinehas killed an Israelite man who openly turned away from the Lord (Numbers 25:3–8). Because his action stopped the plague, the psalm credits him with righteousness. In this psalm he is presented as a stronger example of zeal for the Lord than Moses, who failed at Meribah (Numbers 20:11–13).

title: Psalms 106:34
content_html: Israel continued to disobey the word of the Lord while living in the promised land. Instead of destroying the nations, Israel accepted them and their customs (see Judges 2:1–3, 11–15; 3:5–6).

title: Psalms 106:37
content_html: The gods of other nations were really demons (see Deuteronomy 32:17).

title: Psalms 106:39
content_html: Idolatry is like adultery. Both make God’s people unclean before the Lord (see Leviticus 18:24; Hosea 5:3).

title: Psalms 106:40
content_html: God hated Israel's sin, just as he hates all sin.

title: Psalms 106:40–46
content_html: The psalmist again talks about the Babylonian exile (Psalm 106:26–27). The Lord judged Israel harshly, but he also restrained his anger, remembering the covenant he had made with Abraham.

title: Psalms 106:43–44
content_html: When Israel fell, the destruction was not complete. The Lord had compassion on his people and left a remnant (a small group of people who remained).

title: Psalms 106:45
content_html: He remembered … and relented: Unlike Israel, the Lord is faithful to his covenant (Psalm 105:8).

title: Psalms 106:46
content_html: Even though the exile was harsh, the Lord still protected his people (see 2 Kings 25:27–29).

title: Psalms 106:47
content_html: gather us: The community prays for God to bring them back from exile (see Psalms 102:19–22; 107:3; see also Isaiah 11:12; 40:11; 43:5; 54:7; 56:8; 66:18).

title: Psalms 106:48
content_html:
• This doxology (a short song of praise) ends Book Four (compare Psalms 41:13; 89:52).

  • from everlasting to everlasting: The reference to God’s eternal nature links to the opening of Book Four (Psalm 90:1–2).

  • Let all the people say, “Amen!”: The Hebrew word ’amen means “It is true.” The faithful accept the Lord’s will during exile and his promise to be their God.

title: Psalms 107:1–43
content_html: This psalm is a hymn of praise to God the Redeemer. The psalmist begins by urging the redeemed to praise God for his goodness (Psalm 107:1–3). The psalmist then presents four examples of people whom the Lord saved from trouble (Psalm 107:4–32). He summarizes the Lord’s control over everything (Psalm 107:33–42) and ends with an invitation to recognize God’s loving devotion in all parts of life (107:43).

title: Psalms 107:2
content_html: The redeemed might be all who came out of exile.

title: Psalms 107:3
content_html: People came from all nations where the people of Israel and Judah had been scattered.

title: Psalms 107:4–9
content_html: Those who were lost in the desert wastelands thank God for his rescue. The desert wastelands might be a metaphor for leaving the path of wisdom (Psalm 1:1; Proverbs 4:10–15).

title: Psalms 107:6
content_html: The psalmist does not say what caused his trouble and distress. Because the problem is not specific, people in many different situations can identify with the laments.

title: Psalms 107:7–8
content_html:
• God led them like a shepherd (see Psalm 23:1).

  • Verse 8 is repeated in Psalm 107:15, 21, 31.

title: Psalms 107:10–16
content_html: The psalmist calls for thanksgiving and praise from people in despair over the consequences of their rebellion.

title: Psalms 107:11
content_html: God’s counsel includes his instruction and will.

title: Psalms 107:12
content_html: Exhausted from their hard work (Lamentations 5:13), the people stumbled. While this might be expected of the wicked (Psalms 9:3; 27:2), it was different from the Israelites' escape from Egypt (105:37).

title: Psalms 107:17–22
content_html: Fools who received what they deserved but were rescued from death offer thanksgiving to the Lord.

title: Psalms 107:20
content_html: The Lord creates (Genesis 1:3) and sustains the universe (Hebrews 1:3) simply by speaking (Psalm 147:18). When God speaks, people are also healed (Matthew 8:8).

title: Psalms 107:23–32
content_html: Sailors give thanks to the Lord when he saves them from the chaos and peril of the sea.

title: Psalms 107:25–29
content_html: The same God who speaks and brings about the sailors’ distress can also calm the storm with just a word (compare Jonah 1:4; Matthew 8:26).

title: Psalms 107:32
content_html: This call for public praise expands on the repeated phrases in Psalms 107:21–22 and 107:31.

title: Psalms 107:33–35
content_html: The Lord can choose to make the earth fertile or barren (Psalm 74:15; Isaiah 35:6–7).

title: Psalms 107:33–42
content_html: These two sections (Psalm 107:33–38, 39–42) expand the theme of the Lord’s complete authority and power over things that humans cannot control.

title: Psalms 107:38
content_html: God’s presence can be seen in the abundance of his people’s families and herds (Psalms 127:3; 133:3; compare 109:8).

title: Psalms 107:39
content_html: People often become poor when their leaders are oppressive.

title: Psalms 107:40
content_html: The Lord rejects human rulers who act unjustly. He corrects their wrongdoing and brings justice on the earth.

title: Psalms 107:41
content_html: The large families of the poor are evidence of God’s blessing.

title: Psalms 107:42–43
content_html: Justice wins in God’s world (Psalm 1:6), and Israel’s history shows God’s loving devotion.

title: Psalms 108:1–13
content_html: This psalm combines two parts from David's other psalms (compare Psalms 57:5, 7–11; 60:5–12).

title: Psalms 109:1
content_html: The psalmist finds no evidence that God cares (see Psalm 28:1).

title: Psalms 109:1–31
content_html: The psalmist starts this lament by accusing his accusers of repaying good with evil (Psalm 109:1–5). At the end of the psalm, he prays to the Lord, seeking protection (Psalm 109:21–25) and vengeance (109:26–31).

title: Psalms 109:4–5
content_html: The psalmist shows kindness and godliness (see Psalm 35:12–14), but faces stubborn opposition and hatred.

title: Psalms 109:6–19
content_html: The NLT translates these verses as the words of the psalmist’s accusers; Hebrew lacks They say, which makes it unclear who is speaking.

title: Psalms 109:8
content_html: The wicked person apparently held a position of leadership. Peter referred to this psalm in regard to Judas Iscariot (Acts 1:20–26).

title: Psalms 109:8–11
content_html: Family members of people guilty of capital crimes sometimes died with the offender (Numbers 16:1–33; Joshua 7:1–25) or had their property taken away (1 Kings 21:13–16).

title: Psalms 109:13
content_html: If one’s family name was blotted out, it meant there was no offspring to continue the family name or remember its history (see Proverbs 10:7).

title: Psalms 109:14
content_html: Children share in the sinful condition of their parents (Psalm 51:5; Exodus 20:5; Ephesians 2:3). The speaker asks the Lord to punish this guilty person also for the sins of his family who lived before him (compare Matthew 23:35–36).

title: Psalms 109:16
content_html: The defendant committed very serious crimes that carried the death penalty and did not show even basic decency.

title: Psalms 109:18
content_html: Cursing is the accused person’s whole way of life.

title: Psalms 109:20
content_html: The psalmist asks God to act fairly and give people what they deserve (see Psalm 94:23).

title: Psalms 109:21
