content_html: The Lord's reputation is important. If the wicked win, people might think the Lord is also wicked and be tempted to act wickedly (Psalm 1:1).

title: Psalms 109:21–25
content_html: Feeling vulnerable and miserable, the psalmist asks his Lord for help. The accusations have crushed his spirit and drained his physical strength.

title: Psalms 109:23
content_html: A lengthening shadow suggests a temporary, empty life (Psalms 102:11; 144:4). A single locust can be easily brushed off clothing.

title: Psalms 109:26–31
content_html: These verses form a prayer for God’s presence and for vindication. The psalmist wants everyone to see that he is vindicated by the Lord, not by any human agent.

title: Psalms 109:28
content_html: The Lord’s servant demonstrates loyalty and obedience.

title: Psalms 109:29
content_html: The psalmist was accused of being clothed with disgrace (Psalm 109:18). Now, he asks that his accusers wear their shame like a piece of clothing.

title: Psalms 110:1
content_html:
• The king of Israel had the great privilege of being the Lord’s adopted son (Psalm 2; 1 Chronicles 22:10), but he failed (Psalm 89). This decree restores and elevates his position.

  • The LORD: This title represents the Hebrew name Yahweh, which is God’s personal name (see Exodus 3:6–14; 6:2–8; 20:2).

  • my Lord: The Hebrew word ’adoni means “my master” or “my superior.” The psalmist understood God’s messianic ruler (the king chosen by God) to be greater than himself. The New Testament often quotes this psalm to show that Jesus is the Messiah (Matthew 22:44–45; Mark 12:36–37; Luke 20:42–44; Acts 2:34–35; Hebrews 1:13).

  • The Lord’s right hand represents his authority, strength, presence, and benefits. The Lord chose the descendant of David and raised him up to be close to him (Psalm 80:17).

    Jesus claimed this position for himself (Matthew 26:64; Mark 14:62; 16:19; Luke 22:69), and the apostles announced that Jesus, having gone up to heaven, sits at the right hand of the Father (Acts 2:33; 5:31; 7:55–56; Romans 8:34; Ephesians 1:20; Colossians 3:1; Hebrews 1:3; 8:1; 10:12; 12:2).

  • Ancient Near Eastern kings were sometimes shown placing their feet on the backs of their defeated enemies as on a footstool (see Hebrews 10:12–13).

title: Psalms 110:1–7
content_html: Jesus and the apostles quote this psalm to explain the unique role and authority of Jesus as the Messiah (Matthew 22:43–45; Acts 2:32–36). The psalm also confirms God’s covenant with David. It gives hope that God will fulfill his promise in the future (see 2 Samuel 7:8–16).

title: Psalms 110:2–3
content_html: The kingdom will expand and increase, beginning from Jerusalem, until all enemies are conquered.

title: Psalms 110:4
content_html:
• God had made an unbreakable oath and covenant with David. When the exile seemed to end the line of kings from David's family (Psalm 89:38), it created a crisis for God’s people. The placement of Psalm 110 in Book Five points again to the future of David’s line. David’s descendant must live according to God’s standard of integrity (Psalm 101). His rule will also be shaped by God’s kingship (Psalms 93–100).

  • You are a priest forever: The role of God’s messianic ruler changes. The kings from David’s family had a limited priestly role. They supported and organized the worship of the temple. For example, David brought the ark to Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6:15) and organized temple services (1 Chronicles 6:31–48; 15:11–26; 16:4–42; 23:4–32; 25:1). Solomon oversaw the construction of the temple (1 Kings 5:1–7:51).

    Even so, the offices of king and priest were separate in Israel. In the messianic kingdom, the king will also serve as a priest. He will be like Melchizedek, the king of Salem who was also a priest (Genesis 14:18). The New Testament explains that Jesus Christ fulfills this new order of priesthood (see Hebrews 5:6–10; 6:20–7:25).

title: Psalms 110:5–6
content_html:
• at Your right hand: This position brings help and protection (see Psalms 16:7–8; 108:13).

  • The Lord will strike down rebellious kings. Rebellion against the Lord leads to death (Psalm 2; Romans 6:23).

title: Psalms 110:5–7
content_html: The new priestly role of David's descendant does not cancel his old role as a conquering ruler. He still needs to bring the nations under his rule (Psalm 110:2–3; see Psalm 2).

title: Psalms 110:7
content_html: God’s messianic ruler wins victory with renewed strength, like a traveler who drinks from streams along the way (Psalm 110:3).

title: Psalms 111:1–5
content_html: The godly praise God and delight themselves in his great works. His mighty acts reflect his righteousness, grace, and compassion (see Psalm 116:5–7). All who submit themselves to him experience his glory.

title: Psalms 111:1–10
content_html:
This wisdom psalm contains many references to the works of the Lord that reveal his character. God is:

  • righteous,

  • gracious,

  • compassionate,

  • faithful,

  • just,

  • trustworthy,

  • upright,

  • holy, and

  • awe-inspiring (Psalm 111:3, 4, 7, 8, 9).

God's character gives hope in the covenant relationship (Psalm 111:5). God rescued Israel from Egypt (Psalm 111:9). God gave them the land (111:6). God provides for his people (111:5). However, God expects those in this relationship to also be righteous and have integrity.

title: Psalms 111:1–117.2
content_html: The word Hallelujah! is repeated throughout this group of psalms.

title: Psalms 111:5
content_html: God takes care of his people’s needs, even providing food. He remembers his covenant with Abraham (see Psalm 105:8–11).

title: Psalms 111:6–8
content_html: Giving Israel the land of other nations was just and good. The Lord was judging those nations and showing grace to Israel. However, if the Israelites do not live faithfully and with integrity, they will also lose the land. The Lord’s people must reflect his character.

title: Psalms 111:9
content_html: The people of the exodus generation went free because of God’s grace.

title: Psalms 111:10
content_html: True wisdom is the source of life (Proverbs 3:18; 9:11; Ecclesiastes 7:12). It starts with the fear of the Lord (Proverbs 9:10).

title: Psalms 112:1–5
content_html: The fear of the Lord leads to a wise life that honors God and brings blessing to the family and the community. The godly follow God with joy. Their obedience does not come from fear like a slave obeying a master.

title: Psalms 112:1–10
content_html: This psalm repeats the themes of wisdom and the fear of the Lord (Psalm 111). Wise people have reasons to be happy (Psalm 112:1–5) and strong (112:6–10).

title: Psalms 112:2–3
content_html: blessed: Everyone will recognize the children of godly people (see Psalm 127:4) because the godly leave a legacy for their children (see 1:3; compare Matthew 6:20).

title: Psalms 112:4–5
content_html: Godly people imitate God by being generous, compassionate, and righteous (Psalms 51:1; 111:1–10; 2 Corinthians 9:9). They lend money to the poor without charging interest (Psalms 15:5; 112:9; Exodus 22:25).

title: Psalms 112:6
content_html: The wise avoid mistakes because they are honest and compassionate (Psalms 15:5; 37:23–24). People will remember them for a long time (Proverbs 10:7).

title: Psalms 112:8
content_html: Because the godly trust that God will rescue them and give them victory, they face their enemies with confidence (Psalms 59:10; 112:4; 118:7).

title: Psalms 112:9
content_html: Paul quotes this verse in his instructions about generous giving (2 Corinthians 9:9).

title: Psalms 112:10
content_html:
• The wicked gnash their teeth in anger due to their bitter envy (Psalms 35:16; 37:12). The strength and reputation of the godly create conflict with those who wish them harm.

  • The wicked will retreat in fear and shame.

title: Psalms 113:1–3
content_html: the name of the LORD: God's personal name (Hebrew Yahweh; see Exodus 3:15) reveals who he is (his nature, character, and reputation). He is exalted at all times (now and forever) and in every place (from east to west).

title: Psalms 113:1–9
content_html: This song of praise celebrates the greatness of the Lord's name and glory. God is exalted and glorious in heaven (Psalm 113:4). He also stoops down to care for the lowly and needy on earth (Psalm 113:5–9).

title: Psalms 113:1–118.29
content_html: These psalms are called the Egyptian Hallel. Hallel means “praise.” Jewish people use these psalms during Passover, which began when God brought Israel out of Egypt in the exodus. The people recite Psalms 113–114 before the Passover meal, and Psalms 115–118 are recited afterward.

title: Psalms 113:4
content_html: The Lord’s glory extends to the heavens (Psalms 57:5, 11; 99:2; 108:5; 138:6; Isaiah 6:1; 57:15) and throughout the world of human relationships.

title: Psalms 113:5–9
content_html: The Lord is unlike anyone else, yet he personally cares for people on earth.

title: Psalms 113:6
content_html: The Lord humbles himself to connect directly with his people (see Psalm 138:6; Philippians 2:6–8).

title: Psalms 113:7–8
content_html: These phrases are a quotation from Hannah’s prayer of praise (1 Samuel 2:8–9a).

title: Psalms 113:9
content_html:
• People in this culture often treated a woman without children with shame or contempt (Genesis 16:4). But the Lord has the power to make her a happy mother. For example:

    • Sarah (Genesis 21:2),

    • Rebekah (Genesis 25:21),

    • Rachel (Genesis 30:23), and

    • Hannah (1 Samuel 1:20).

  • God's greatness and goodness give people many reasons to praise him.

title: Psalms 114:1–2
content_html:
• At the time of Israel’s exodus from Egypt and their entrance into the promised land, Israel was one holy kingdom (Exodus 19:5–6).

  • The mention of Judah and Israel as separate lands shows the psalm was written after the kingdom was divided (1 Kings 12:16–17; compare 1 Samuel 11:8; 1 Kings 1:35), and probably after the exile. Judah became God's sanctuary because the people worshiped God there. His tabernacle, and later his temple, were located there.

title: Psalms 114:1–8
content_html: This song celebrates the Lord's power in nature. It remembers Israel's start as a nation during the exodus (Psalm 114:1–2) and their entry into the promised land (114:3–6). It encourages respect for the God of Jacob (Psalm 114:7–8).

title: Psalms 114:3–4
content_html:
• fled: God parted the waters so that Israel could escape Egypt (Psalm 77:17–18; Exodus 14).

  • The Jordan River turned back when Israel arrived to cross it (see Joshua 3).

  • skipped like rams: God's presence made the mountains quake (Exodus 19:16–20).

title: Psalms 114:8
content_html: The Lord miraculously provided for his people in hard times after the exile, just as he had done in the wilderness after the exodus.

title: Psalms 115:1–3
content_html: The community confesses their faith that God will rescue them for the glory of his name.

title: Psalms 115:1–18
content_html: This is a song of thanksgiving for God's blessings. The community gives all glory to the Lord, the true source of hope and blessing. Those who trust in idols feel great disappointment. The Creator's blessing extends to future generations (Psalm 115:14), especially to the priests (115:12).

title: Psalms 115:2–3
content_html: Where is their God?: People question God's presence or power when he does not act as they expect (compare Psalms 14:1; 42:3, 10). However, God is in the heavens. He has all power, authority, and knowledge. He does as he wishes, not what people think he should do. He carries out his plans according to his will (Ephesians 1:11) and acts in his own time and ways.

title: Psalms 115:4–11
content_html:
• This entire section is repeated in Psalm 135:15–20.

  • Unlike the Lord (Psalm 95:3–5), idols cannot do anything. They are useless.

title: Psalms 115:8
content_html: Worshiping idols leads one astray and corrupts those who trust in them (Isaiah 44:20).

title: Psalms 115:9–11
content_html: Israel … O house of Aaron … You who fear the LORD: The psalmist urges everyone in the God-fearing community to trust in the living God. This threefold address assures them that the Lord is their true helper and shield (compare Psalm 118:2–4).

title: Psalms 115:12–13
content_html: The groups mentioned in Psalm 115:9–11 receive assurance of the Lord's blessing, no matter their social status (see 113:8).

title: Psalms 115:14–15
content_html:
• The psalmist blesses (Psalm 107:37–38, 41) all who trust in and fear the Lord. The Creator will care for the families of those who fear him.

  • the Maker of heaven and earth: This phrase speaks about how the Lord created everything and has power over all creation.

title: Psalms 115:16–18
content_html:
• The highest heavens belong to the LORD: God's exaltation starts in heaven (Psalm 113:4), far above the gods of other nations. The earth also belongs to him (Psalm 47:9; 95:4).

  • The dead cannot participate in God’s blessings. The living praise God in thanksgiving for his blessings (Psalm 79:13).

title: Psalms 116:1
content_html: I love the LORD: This unique opening expresses affection for and commitment to the Lord (see Psalm 18:1; Deuteronomy 6:5) on the basis of what the Lord has done.

title: Psalms 116:1–4
content_html: The psalmist praises the Lord for answering his prayers and rescuing him from death.

title: Psalms 116:1–19
content_html: This song of thanksgiving rejoices in God’s character and in rescue from death (Psalm 116:1–11). It gives an assurance of God’s protection in life and reminds the godly that the Lord watches over them even at death. The psalmist commits to living as God’s servant and resolves to honor him publicly (Psalm 116:12–19).

title: Psalms 116:2
content_html: Because the Lord responds to the cry of his people(Psalm 107:41; 113:6–8), the psalmist now strongly believes in prayer.

title: Psalms 116:3
content_html: It is not clear if the psalmist’s crisis was a literal near-death experience or if he was using the term figuratively.

title: Psalms 116:4
content_html: This is the first of three times that the psalmist calls on the Lord’s name (which represents God's reputation).

title: Psalms 116:5–7
content_html: The gracious, righteous, and compassionate Lord (see Psalm 111:1–5) rescued and gave rest to the psalmist.

title: Psalms 116:6
content_html: People with childlike faith readily receive God's wisdom and instruction (Psalm 19:7–11; Matthew 18:3–4).

title: Psalms 116:8–11
content_html: The psalmist’s near-death crisis made him more aware of life and of walking before the Lord. Though he had suffered, the Lord was faithful. However, people are all liars.

title: Psalms 116:9
content_html: I will walk before the LORD: The psalmist lived with integrity. After experiencing deep distress, he now enjoys open fellowship with the Lord (see Psalm 56:13; compare Job 42:5).

title: Psalms 116:10
content_html: I believed: Paul quotes this phrase in 2 Corinthians 4:13.

title: Psalms 116:12–14
content_html:
• The Lord’s gracious gifts are so great that the psalmist can do nothing to repay his debt. Keeping his promises publicly demonstrates his gratitude.

  • All he has done for the psalmist includes answers to prayer, rescue, and life.

  • The cup of salvation overflows with God’s blessings (Psalm 23:5), unlike the cup of judgment (75:8).

  • The psalmist makes vows (Psalm 116:18) to praise the Lord publicly (116:17–18).

title: Psalms 116:15–19
content_html: No one lives or dies outside of God’s will. Having been given a second chance at life, the poet readily commits himself to the Lord with renewed acts of public devotion.

title: Psalms 117:1
content_html: Paul quotes this verse to show that Gentiles would eventually praise the God of Israel (Romans 15:11).

title: Psalms 117:1–2
content_html: This psalm is the shortest in the Psalter. It invites all people of the earth to praise the Lord for his loving devotion and faithfulness.

title: Psalms 118:1–29
content_html: The psalmist joyfully thanks the Lord for his goodness and urges others to trust in his loving devotion (Psalm 118:1–4, 29). This poem is the last of the Egyptian Hallel (Psalms 113–118).

title: Psalms 118:2–4
content_html: Israel … the house of Aaron … those who fear the LORD: These three groups represent the entire covenant community (compare Psalms 115:9–11; 135:19–20).

title: Psalms 118:5–9
content_html: The Lord alone is the psalmist’s helper.

title: Psalms 118:6–7
content_html:
• The LORD is on my side: Compare Romans 8:31; Hebrews 13:6.

  • God's presence removes the need to fear people (Psalm 56:11). The psalmist joyfully expects God’s rescue (Psalm 59:10; Psalm 112:8).

title: Psalms 118:8–9
content_html: Trusting in the Lord is better than placing confidence in even the most powerful human leaders (Psalms 118:6; 146:3).

title: Psalms 118:10–12
content_html:
• Strength comes from the name of the LORD. This is shown by the threefold repetition of the attackers, the name of the LORD, and the victory he gives.

  • Even when hostile people surrounded the psalmist, the Lord extinguished them like burning thorns (see Isaiah 9:18; 10:17).

title: Psalms 118:14–18
content_html: The psalmist celebrates the victory of the Lord’s strong right hand, which kept him from death.

title: Psalms 118:17–21
content_html: God has restored the psalmist from death to life, so he praises the Lord publicly (see Psalm 116:6, 12–19).

title: Psalms 118:22
content_html:
The cornerstone shapes and supports a building or arch. It is the most important of all the stones. The Lord selects and places this stone for his sanctuary (Isaiah 28:16).

Jesus referred to himself as this cornerstone (Matthew 21:42; Mark 12:10–11; Luke 20:17; see Acts 4:11; Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:6–7).

title: Psalms 118:22–24
content_html: The psalmist sees his own salvation as the beginning of a new day in which the Lord will do great things.

title: Psalms 118:24
content_html: This is the day that the LORD has made: The Lord's victory has begun, but it will be completed on another day (Psalm 118:25).

title: Psalms 118:25–29
content_html: The psalmist ends with prayer and thanksgiving, anticipating an even greater revelation of God’s victory and rescue.

title: Psalms 118:26
content_html:
• The one who comes in the name of the Lord brings the Lord’s complete victory (see Psalm 118:10–12).

  • When Jesus triumphantly entered Jerusalem, the crowds shouted blessings. They used phrases from this verse (Matthew 21:9; Mark 11:9; Luke 19:38; John 12:13).

    Jesus quoted this passage as he wept over Jerusalem (Matthew 23:39; Luke 13:35).

title: Psalms 119:1–4
content_html: Wise people are happy. They have integrity (see Psalm 84:11) and follow God's guidance. They obey God's teachings and seek them with complete devotion (see Psalm 27:7; 119:10, 45, 94, 155). They reject all evil forms. Compare Psalm 1:1–2.

title: Psalms 119:1–8
content_html:
Aleph (א) is the first letter of the Hebrew alphabet. Psalm 119 is written as an acrostic poem. Each section has eight verses, and every verse in that section begins with the same Hebrew letter. The first section begins with Aleph.

God, the author of these instructions, commands that his followers live by them. The psalmist prays to be given the grace to live by that revelation.

title: Psalms 119:1–176
content_html:
This psalm blends wisdom, lament, praise, thanksgiving, and confession. It often uses eight Hebrew words translated as:

  • “Instructions”

  • “law”

  • “words”

  • “commandments”

  • “statutes”

  • “judgments”

  • “promises”

  • “precepts“

These words encourage love for and obedience to God's instructions in the Scriptures (see 2 Timothy 3:14–17). A deep commitment to these instructions unifies the psalm.

title: Psalms 119:4
content_html: To keep God’s commandments means to guard them by obeying them.

title: Psalms 119:9
content_html:
• A young person often acts foolishly (Proverbs 22:15) and can easily make mistakes that ruin their life (Proverbs 7:6–23). However, youth is the best time to build self-discipline and seek wisdom (see Psalm 34:11; Proverbs 1:4).

  • keep his way pure: This means avoiding corruption by evil and sin and developing the integrity described in Psalm 15.

title: Psalms 119:9–16
content_html:
Beth (ב) is the second letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It makes the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist is determined to stay pure and encourages gaining wisdom through God's principles (Psalm 119:9) and instructions (119:10–16).

title: Psalms 119:10
content_html: The student asks God the teacher to discipline him so that he will not stray.

title: Psalms 119:14
content_html: God's wisdom is more valuable than any wealth people desire (Psalm 119:72, 127; Proverbs 3:14–15).

title: Psalms 119:15
content_html: The path of wisdom involves thinking deeply about God's teachings (see Psalm 119:1, 3). Wise people consider the meaning of God's word.

title: Psalms 119:17–18
content_html: The psalmist wants to live a life full of praise and obedience to please the Lord (Psalms 118:17; 119:25, 77, 116, 144, 175). Only the Lord can remove his enemies and help him understand the wonderful truths in God's teachings.

title: Psalms 119:17–24
content_html:
Gimel (ג) is the third letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

Like a stranger in a hostile land, God's servant needs God's guidance.

title: Psalms 119:20–21
content_html: The arrogant are probably those who mock the psalmist (Psalm 119:22–23).

title: Psalms 119:23–24
content_html: Those who meditate on God’s instructions receive wise advice from them.

title: Psalms 119:25–32
content_html:
Daleth (ד) is the fourth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The poet feels deep pain and sadness but asks the Lord to give him strength. He wants to follow the Lord. Suffering and death may result from the Lord’s discipline (Psalms 118:17–18; 119:67, 71, 75), but his instruction renews the weary soul (see 119:12).

title: Psalms 119:29
content_html:
• path of deceit: Self-deception comes from sinful human nature (Jeremiah 17:9). It leads to foolishness.

  • Access to God’s word is a privilege, not a right.

title: Psalms 119:33–40
content_html:
He (ה) is the fifth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

As a student, the poet asks his teacher, God, to renew life and to transform disgrace into righteousness.

title: Psalms 119:36
content_html: The love for money can lead to sin (compare Exodus 18:21; 1 Timothy 3:1–5; 6:10).

title: Psalms 119:41–43
content_html: The psalmist asks for protection from enemies who speak harshly (see Psalms 42:10; 44:16; 89:50–51; 102:8).

title: Psalms 119:41–48
content_html:
Waw (ו) is the sixth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist prays that God’s word would continue to be the source of his love and salvation.

title: Psalms 119:44–46
content_html: The psalmist is determined to obey the Lord.

title: Psalms 119:47–48
content_html: The psalmist finds joy in God's word despite experiencing pain (Psalm 119:92, 143).

title: Psalms 119:49–56
content_html:
Zayin (ז) is the seventh letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist asks God to remember his word as the psalmist also remembers God’s word and his name. Remembrance expresses devotion.

title: Psalms 119:52
content_html: judgments of old: This refers to God’s just rulings or decisions (his laws). These are found in the Torah, the books of Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy.

title: Psalms 119:57–64
content_html:
Heth (ח) is the eighth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist seeks God’s favor and loving devotion. Although surrounded by evildoers, he remains a loyal servant of the Lord.

title: Psalms 119:61
content_html: Wicked people try to lead him into sin, but the psalmist remembers God's word and stays strong (see Psalm 119:16).

title: Psalms 119:64
content_html: God's goodness fills the earth. The entire creation shows evidence of his goodness (see Isaiah 6:3; 11:9).

title: Psalms 119:65–68
content_html:
• Good judgment and knowledge come from wisdom (Psalm 111:10).

  • The psalmist admits he used to wander like a lost sheep (Psalm 119:176).

  • now I keep your word: See Psalm 119:25–29.

  • You are good, and You do what is good: God's character is perfect (Psalm 97:2; James 1:17; 1 John 1:5).

title: Psalms 119:65–72
content_html:
Teth (ט) is the ninth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist admits his sin and gratefully accepts the Lord's discipline.

title: Psalms 119:73–76
content_html: As God’s child, the psalmist asks for divine wisdom.

title: Psalms 119:73–80
content_html:
Yodh (י) is the tenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

In this prayer for comfort and compassion, the psalmist commits himself to his Creator, who has afflicted him. He wants God to pay back his enemies for the wrongs they have done. He also prays to receive the gift of life. He not only prays for himself but also considers the whole community of the godly, asking that God’s answers to prayer would encourage them.

title: Psalms 119:81–88
content_html:
Kaph (כ) is the eleventh letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist feels exhausted from waiting for the Lord, but he still hopes for God's future rescue.

title: Psalms 119:85–88
content_html: God's word is trustworthy. This contrasts with the lies of the enemies who hunt the psalmist without reason (see Psalm 119:78).

title: Psalms 119:89–96
content_html:
Lamedh (ל) is the twelfth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The stability of God’s wisdom gives understanding. God’s word provides order and a framework for living, even when under pressure from adversaries.

title: Psalms 119:95
content_html: The wicked planned to kill the psalmist, but they faced destruction themselves (Psalm 1:6).

title: Psalms 119:96
content_html: I have seen a limit to all perfection: Humans cannot fully understand God's ways (see Ecclesiastes 3:11).

title: Psalms 119:97–102
content_html: make me wiser: God’s word provides wisdom and perspective. God is the true source of wisdom.

title: Psalms 119:97–104
content_html:
Mem (מ) is the thirteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The instruction and wisdom of God through his word are far better than the best that human teachers can offer.

title: Psalms 119:103–104
content_html:
• Wisdom is better than the finest food (Psalm 19:10; 81:16).

  • The psalmist had already turned away from the false promises of foolishness (see Psalm 119:18, 78, 86, 163).

title: Psalms 119:105–106
content_html: The Lord’s word is like a lamp. It offers perspective, hope, and guidance in darkness (Psalm 18:25–29; 119:130). This light also gives life (Proverbs 6:23).

title: Psalms 119:105–112
content_html:
Nun (נ) is the fourteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist's deep commitment to God's word comes from within (Psalm 119:112) and directs his actions.

title: Psalms 119:108–112
content_html:
• freewill offerings of my mouth: This Hebrew expression is unique in the Old Testament (compare Psalm 50:23).

  •  The psalmist is vulnerable to his opponents because of his commitment to a wise life.

title: Psalms 119:113
content_html: The godly hate the wicked and their actions (Psalm 5:4–6; compare Ezekiel 33:11).

title: Psalms 119:113–120
content_html:
Samekh (ס) is the fifteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet and marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist declares his hatred of evil and his love for God and his ways.

title: Psalms 119:121–128
content_html:
Ayin (ע) is the sixteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet and marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The servant of the Lord struggles to endure the evil around him. He confidently asks the Lord to rescue him right away!

title: Psalms 119:125
content_html: Simply possessing God’s law does not provide any benefits. The psalmist knows that only as the Lord gives discernment will he understand it.

title: Psalms 119:129–136
content_html:
Pe (פ) is the seventeenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet and marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist prays earnestly for God to speak to him as he suffers from his adversities.

title: Psalms 119:137–144
content_html:
Tsadhe (צ) is the eighteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet and marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

God is righteous, and his word is right and just, especially in contrast to the deceptions of the wicked.

title: Psalms 119:139
content_html: The psalmist feels angry (compare Psalm 69:9) because he sees wicked people ignoring God's word.

title: Psalms 119:141
content_html: The psalmist feels very troubled by those who oppress him (see Psalm 119:143; compare Isaiah 53).

title: Psalms 119:145–148
content_html: I rise and anticipate translate the same Hebrew verb.

title: Psalms 119:145–152
content_html:
Qoph (ק) is the nineteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist prays to the Lord at night. Even when God seems distant, he is actually close.

title: Psalms 119:150–151
content_html: Coming to attack me (or draw near) is a Hebrew wordplay on You are near. The wicked stay far from God's instructions, but the psalmist stays close, so he does not need to fear.

title: Psalms 119:153–160
content_html: Resh (ר): This request for life appears throughout Psalm 119, but it is focused in this stanza.

title: Psalms 119:161–164
content_html:
• my heart fears … I rejoice: Fear, trembling, and joy show the psalmist’s devotion to God.

  • God’s word is a treasure (great spoil).

  • In the Old Testament, the number seven represents wholeness.

title: Psalms 119:161–168
content_html:
Shin (ש) is the twenty-first letter of the alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

The psalmist looks beyond his adversaries to the peace of God. This perspective allows him to praise God throughout the day.

title: Psalms 119:169–176
content_html:
Taw (ת) is the twenty-second letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It marks the beginning of the next section of this psalm.

God's servant asks the Lord for help one last time. He still feels like a lost sheep.

title: Psalms 119:176
content_html: God, the Great Shepherd, seeks out his lost sheep (Psalm 23:1; Luke 15:4–6).

title: Psalms 120:1–2
content_html: The psalmist is surrounded by liars, so he makes his lament to the Lord, the only one who can help him.

title: Psalms 120:1–7
content_html: This short, individual lament expresses the psalmist’s trust that the Lord will deal with deceptive evildoers. It also laments living as a stranger among hateful liars. The psalmist’s picture of his life in a foreign land might have been a reality as he set out for Jerusalem, or it might depict his feelings and concerns as he begins his pilgrimage. Overwhelmed with injustice, he trusts God and looks forward to arriving in the Lord’s presence.

title: Psalms 120:1–134.3
content_html: These short and memorable songs were sung by pilgrims as they traveled to Jerusalem for the national festivals held three times each year (Deuteronomy 16:16). The title of these psalms includes a Hebrew word that can mean “steps” or “stairs.” Some scholars understand it to mean “going up to Jerusalem.” These psalms, often called the Songs of Ascents, lead the listener on a journey to Jerusalem to praise God for his goodness to Israel through history.

title: Psalms 120:3–4
content_html:
• The psalmist speaks with the liars around him. He feels confident that the Lord will judge them.

  • The Divine Warrior (see Psalms 7:12–13; 11:4–6) will attack the liars with sharp arrows and burning coals.

title: Psalms 120:5
content_html:
Meshech was a Japhethite territory far to the north of Canaan. Kedar was an Ishmaelite territory in Arabia. Violent people lived in both places.

Meshech was known for its slave trade (Ezekiel 27:13; see also Exodus 21:16), and Kedar was known for its warriors (Isaiah 21:16–17). These places represented the rebellious and violent land where the psalmist suffered.

title: Psalms 120:5–7
content_html: The psalmist feels the pain of being alone, isolated, and in constant conflict.

title: Psalms 120:6–7
content_html: peace: See Psalms 37:11; 122:6–9; 125:5; 128:6.

title: Psalms 121:1
content_html:
The mountains were known for idol worship (Deuteronomy 12:2), and Israel was drawn to the hilltop shrines (Hosea 4:13). This verse can be seen as a rhetorical question expecting a "no" answer: The gods of the hilltop shrines cannot save anyone.

Others understand the mountains to be a symbol of the Lord’s strength (Psalm 95:4). In that case, the question would be taken literally (From where does my help come?), with the answer in the next verse.

title: Psalms 121:1–8
content_html: The leader of the group of pilgrims uses word pictures to assure the group of the Lord’s presence during the journey.

title: Psalms 121:4
content_html: God watches over his own, even and especially when they face many troubles (Psalms 12:5, 7; 69:29; 91:14; 146:9).

title: Psalms 121:4–6
content_html:
• The Maker of heaven and earth (Psalm 121:2) protects Israel.

  • God stands with Israel, offering support and care to the faithful (see Psalm 16:7–8).

  • Both the sun and the moon were worshiped as gods in the ancient Near East. But God limits their power. They are part of creation and also praise the Lord (Psalm 148:3).

title: Psalms 122:1
content_html: The central sanctuary was the house of the LORD. This phrase first referred to the tabernacle (Exodus 23:19; 34:26).

title: Psalms 122:1–9
content_html: Jerusalem is presented here as the city of God, the city of David, and the center of faith. A group of pilgrims joyfully arrives in the city (Psalm 122:1–2). The psalmist praises Jerusalem (Psalm 122:3–5) and prays for the city’s peace (122:6–9).

title: Psalms 122:2
content_html: Several psalms celebrate or look forward to being inside the gates of the city (Psalms 9:14; 87:2; 100:4; 118:19).

title: Psalms 122:3
content_html: In the time of David and Solomon, people knew Jerusalem as a safe place (1 Kings 10:6–7).

title: Psalms 122:3–5
content_html: Jerusalem can represent God’s heavenly dwelling place. David became the model for the rulers God chooses.

title: Psalms 122:4
content_html:
• Each person in Israel belonged to a tribe, and all tribes belonged to the Lord.

  • Giving thanks means presenting an offering acceptable to the Lord (Psalms 50:14, 23; 100:4; 118:19).

  • as a testimony for Israel: See Exodus 23:17; Deuteronomy 16:16.

title: Psalms 122:5
content_html: the thrones of judgment stand: In the time of David and Solomon (1 Kings 3:28), Jerusalem was known for wisdom and justice. It will be like that again (Isaiah 9:7).

title: Psalms 122:6–9
content_html: Prayers for the peace of Jerusalem are motivated by love of family and God’s sanctuary.

title: Psalms 123:1–4
content_html: This lament about the psalmist's humble position shows strong trust in the Lord (Psalm 123:1–2). The psalmist prays for God's favor on the community because evil surrounds them (Psalm 123:3–4). The poet looks beyond Jerusalem (Psalm 122) to God's throne in heaven.

title: Psalms 124:1–2
content_html: The negative rhetorical questions emphasize the positive idea that the Lord was indeed on their side.

title: Psalms 124:1–8
content_html: This thanksgiving song describes what might happen if the Lord were not present with his people.

title: Psalms 124:4–5
content_html: the raging waters: The enemies could have defeated the Israelites and destroyed them if the Lord had not supported his people (compare Psalm 69:15; Jeremiah 46:8).

title: Psalms 124:6–8
content_html: The enemies of God's people were like lions or bears that tear prey with their teeth (see Psalm 57:4; Proverbs 28:15). The Lord shut their mouths (Daniel 6:22).

title: Psalms 125:1–5
content_html: The psalmist expresses the community's trust in the Lord's protection (Psalm 125:1–2) and prays for peace (125:3–5).

title: Psalms 125:2
content_html: surrounds: God guards and protects like a wall of fire (Zechariah 2:5).

title: Psalms 125:3
content_html: The godly may be tempted to abandon God’s ways because of pressure from others or from the surrounding culture.

title: Psalms 125:4–5
content_html: People who do not follow the "straight paths" are foolish (Proverbs 2:12–15). God gives his goodness to the godly (see Psalm 122:6–9).

title: Psalms 126:1–3
content_html: The Lord brought about Israel’s restoration from exile, just as he had brought Israel out of Egypt (Psalm 114:1). The restoration transformed the weeping of the Lord’s people into laughter and showed the Lord’s character to the nations (Psalm 9:11; 64:9; Isaiah 12:4).

title: Psalms 126:1–6
content_html: This psalm celebrates Israel's return from exile (Psalm 126:1–3). The psalmist then prays that God will fully restore the people (Psalm 126:4–6).

title: Psalms 126:4–6
content_html:
• The prophets had spoken of the restoration as a time when the wilderness would change into the garden of Eden (Isaiah 51:3; Ezekiel 47). However, the reality was far from paradise. Here, the restoration community prays for complete redemption (see Haggai 2:19).

  • like streams in the Negev: Seasonal rains filled the dry riverbeds (wadis), making the land green again. The prayer seeks a similar change in Israel’s fortunes, fulfilling the imagery of the "Second Exodus" in Isaiah (see "The Promise of a Second Exodus" Theme Note).

  • carrying sheaves of grain: This harvest represents God's complete restoration.

title: Psalms 127:1–2
content_html:
• Unless: These conditional statements emphasize that the Lord is the one who protects the city (compare Psalm 124:1–2).

  • Human effort achieves nothing unless the Lord blesses it (see Psalm 44:3).

  • you rise early and stay up late: This phrase describes people who work constantly and live with pressure and competition.

  • Those whom God loves can rest and sleep in peace (Psalm 3:5; 4:8).

title: Psalms 127:1–5
content_html: Blessings and protection come from God. Without his presence, success is not possible (Psalm 127:1–2). This is especially true for children, who are gifts from God (Psalm 127:3–5).

title: Psalms 127:3–5
content_html: Children are one of God’s blessings. In Hebrew, the word for children (banim) sounds like the word for builders (bonim; Psalm 127:1). This wordplay shows that having children helps build a family.

title: Psalms 127:4–5
content_html: Like arrows: The image compares children to arrows in a warrior’s hand. Children strengthen and support the family and help defend its honor when others accuse or oppose them (compare Psalms 120:4; 7:12–13).

title: Psalms 128:1–2
content_html: eating the fruit of your labor: This means gaining benefit from whatever work one does.

title: Psalms 128:1–6
content_html: The godly, who fear the Lord, will know true happiness (Psalm 128:1–2). The Lord is with them (see Psalm 127:1–2), strengthens their family life (128:3–4), and blesses them for generations (128:5–6).

title: Psalms 128:3
content_html: Children are like strong young olive trees because they are full of potential. Olive oil symbolizes God's blessing (Psalm 104:15; Numbers 18:12; Jeremiah 31:12; Haggai 2:19).

title: Psalms 128:4–6
content_html:
• God will continually bless those who fear him (Psalm 133:3) from his dwelling in Zion (20:2; 50:2; 110:2), no matter where they live.

  • children's children: God's blessings continue through generations for those who are faithful (Psalms 79:12–13; 89:1; 100:4–5; 103:17–19).

title: Psalms 129:1–2
content_html: Israel’s earliest years as a nation took place while the people were living in Egypt.

title: Psalms 129:1–8
content_html: The psalmist briefly reviews Israel’s history of trouble (Psalm 129:1–2) and redemption (129:3–4). He ends with curses against the enemies and blessings on the godly (Psalm 129:5–6).

title: Psalms 129:3–4
content_html: The LORD is righteous: He rescued Israel from Egypt, from many enemy attacks, and from the Babylonian exile.

title: Psalms 129:6–8
content_html: Grass on a rooftop had shallow roots and would scorch easily. A crop was ignored by the harvester when the yield was low. The enemies who had sown hurt among God’s people would gain little.

title: Psalms 130:1–3
content_html: A cry from the depths comes from a place of deep distress (compare Psalm 69:2, 14; Isaiah 51:10; Ezekiel 27:34). Feeling weak, the psalmist prays for God's favor.

title: Psalms 130:1–8
content_html: Beginning with himself as a representative of the community, the psalmist cries for God’s mercy (Psalm 130:1–3) out of deep distress. He places his hope in the Lord (Psalm 130:4–6), trusting that the Lord will rescue in his own time and way. With this confidence, the psalmist invites Israel to wait for the Lord (Psalm 130:7–8).

title: Psalms 130:3
content_html: The psalmist knows that his sins make him guilty and deserving of God’s judgment.

title: Psalms 130:4–6
content_html: The good news comes in God’s readiness to forgive sinners. His grace encourages people to fear him and hope in him.

title: Psalms 130:5
content_html: God's word gives hope (Psalms 119:42–43, 49; 147:11).

title: Psalms 130:7–8
content_html: Israel is encouraged to hope in the Lord (Psalms 9:18; 27:14; 131:3) because the Lord pays the high price for redemption and removes the sin that separates humans from him (see also Psalm 111:9; Exodus 6:6–8; 2 Samuel 7:23–24; Isaiah 43:1–4; Titus 2:14; Hebrews 9:12; 1 Peter 1:18).

title: Psalms 131:1
content_html:
• The heart represents the entire person (see Psalm 27:8).

  • God hates arrogance, which is a foolish way of life (Proverbs 6:16–19).

  • The psalmist does not trouble himself with matters that properly belong to the Lord.

title: Psalms 131:1–3
content_html: Out of his contentment in the Lord, the psalmist invites Israel to seek their rest in God (Psalm 131:3).

title: Psalms 131:2
content_html:
• The psalmist has calmed and quieted himself. He has given up his ambitions and pride (Galatians 2:20).

  • like a weaned child: The psalmist compares himself to a contented child who rests quietly with its mother.

title: Psalms 132:1–18
content_html:
This psalm celebrates the Lord’s faithfulness to David. When David wanted to bring the ark to Jerusalem (Psalm 132:1–5), the Lord agreed and made Jerusalem his earthly home (132:6–10). God’s choice of Jerusalem also shows his choice:

  • of David's family line (Psalm 132:11–13),

  • of Zion (Psalm 132:14–17), and

  • of the priesthood (Psalm 132:9, 16).

title: Psalms 132:6
content_html:
• Ephrathah is another name for Bethlehem (Genesis 35:19; 48:7).

  • Jaar is the singular form of the plural Jearim. The ark was at Kiriath-jearim for 20 years in Samuel’s time (1 Samuel 7:1–2).

title: Psalms 132:6–10
content_html: These verses recall David moving the ark of the covenant to Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6:1–15) and the Lord's promise to David (2 Samuel 7:4–16).

title: Psalms 132:8–10
content_html: These verses are a quotation from Solomon’s prayer at the dedication of the temple (2 Chronicles 6:41–42).

title: Psalms 132:9
content_html: The priests were to show righteousness by the way they live (Psalm 132:16; Malachi 2:6).

title: Psalms 132:11–12
content_html: The LORD swore an oath to David (2 Samuel 7:4–16): God's promise is unconditional, but the covenant depends on conditions (If … then). Those who break God's covenant should expect God's judgment as discipline (see Psalm 89:30–37).

title: Psalms 132:13–17
content_html: The psalmist remembers how God chose Jerusalem and appointed the priests.

title: Psalms 132:17
content_html: anointed one: God chose and established this ruler (see Psalms 1–8) to always provide light for the Lord’s people. This light would shine continuously (see 1 Kings 11:36; 2 Kings 8:19; compare John 8:12; Revelation 21:23–24).

title: Psalms 132:18
content_html: The enemies will feel shame, while the king will be honored (Psalm 132:9, 16).

title: Psalms 133:1
content_html: Brothers are connected by family, by work, or by loyalty. In this psalm, brothers means all members of the community, including men and women, who live in unity.

title: Psalms 133:1–2
content_html: The psalmist speaks about the joy of relationships dedicated to God.

title: Psalms 133:1–3
content_html: In this wisdom psalm (see Psalm 1), peaceful and harmonious relationships show that God is present.

title: Psalms 133:2
content_html:
• The fine oil was used to appoint and set apart Aaron for his role as priest (see Exodus 29:7).

  • head … beard … collar of his robes: The oil set Aaron apart completely for God’s service.

title: Psalms 133:3
content_html:
• The dew represents the richness of God’s blessings (his goodness and care). In Jerusalem, this moisture comes from winds off the Mediterranean Sea. Mount Hermon, which has snow on its peak, is known for its abundant moisture.

  • A blessing is God’s life-giving power. It shows his provision (care for needs) and protection (Psalm 5:12; 41:2). It also comes from his presence (21:6; 67:1). In the end, all nations will receive God’s blessing (Psalms 67; 72:17). Zion is the central place of God's blessing (Psalms 134:3; 147:13).

  • God gives everlasting life to people.

title: Psalms 134:1–2
content_html: Those who serve by night were the priests and Levites (see also Psalm 135:2).

title: Psalms 134:1–3
content_html: In the last of the pilgrims’ songs (Psalms 120–134), the travelers ask the temple workers to praise the Lord. In return, they will receive a long-awaited blessing.

title: Psalms 135:1–21
content_html: God is the Creator and the Lord of history, the “Maker of heaven and earth” (Psalm 134:3). He deserves praise because, unlike idols, he saves his people.

title: Psalms 135:4
content_html: The names Jacob and Israel represent all twelve tribes. The Lord loves his chosen people. They are his treasure among all the world's kingdoms (Exodus 19:5–6).

title: Psalms 135:5
content_html: The Lord is greater than any real or imagined supernatural being (see Psalms 95:3–5; 115:4–8).

title: Psalms 135:6
content_html:
• God has complete authority and power over the whole created order. God does whatever pleases him.

  • The phrase in the heavens and on the earth refers to all of creation.

title: Psalms 135:7
content_html: The psalmist tells of God’s complete power and authority over the weather (Psalms 29; 77:17–18; 78:26; 104:4; 147:8–9; 148:5–6).

title: Psalms 135:8–11
content_html:
• In the tenth and final plague against Egypt, God destroyed the firstborn of each family.

  • The signs and wonders refer to the ten plagues (see Exodus 7–11; Deuteronomy 4:34; Nehemiah 9:10).

title: Psalms 135:8–12
content_html: The Lord had complete authority and power over the exodus and the conquest of the promised land.

title: Psalms 135:13–18
content_html: God’s name and character are very different from idols.

title: Psalms 135:15–18
content_html: Idols lead people to think wrongly because they are only things humans make. They cannot speak, see, hear, or breathe (Isaiah 41:22–24; 44:9, 18; Romans 1:21–23).

title: Psalms 135:15–20
content_html: This entire section is repeated in Psalm 115:4–11.

title: Psalms 135:19–21
content_html: The groups mentioned here formed the covenant community (see Psalm 115:9–11).

title: Psalms 136:1–3
content_html: The psalmist calls the community to praise the Lord, who is good and full of love. He is the true Lord over all people. Verse 1 echoes the poetic line in 2 Chronicles 5:13.

title: Psalms 136:1–26
content_html:
This is the last of the Great Hallel psalms (Psalms 120–136). It uniquely repeats the phrase His loving devotion endures forever in every verse.

The hymn begins and ends with a call to praise (Psalm 136:1–3, 26). It declares the Lord as the Creator of everything and the Redeemer of Israel, both in ancient times (Psalm 136:10–22) and in the recent past (136:23–24).

title: Psalms 136:10–22
content_html: The psalmist tells the early history of Israel's redemption.

title: Psalms 136:23–24
content_html: The psalmist tells the recent story of Israel's redemption, but does not specify the details.

title: Psalms 136:25
content_html: The Lord provides for all of his creatures.

title: Psalms 136:26
content_html:
The phrase God of heaven is a name for the Lord used during and after the exile (when many Israelites were taken from their land). It appears in:

  • Ezra (Ezra 1:2; 5:11, 12; 6:9, 10; 7:12),

  • Nehemiah (Nehemiah 1:5; 2:4, 20), and

  • Daniel (Daniel 2:18, 19, 37, 44).

title: Psalms 137:1–9
content_html: In response to the pain of exile (Psalm 137:1–4), the psalmist decides to remember Jerusalem, even though the memories cause him pain (137:4–6). He also speaks a strong prayer asking God to bring justice on his enemies (Psalm 137:7–9).

title: Psalms 137:2–4
content_html:
Because the music of Jerusalem was connected to the joy of life in the promised land, the exile took away any reason to sing joyful songs. The Babylonians asked the Judeans to sing and dance for them, but the people were so full of grief that they could not do it.

The songs about the Lord celebrate his power and goodness, but his people were now filled with doubt and questions. They hung their harps in the trees, which may show that their joy had come to an end under God’s judgment (Deuteronomy 21:23).

title: Psalms 137:5–6
content_html: The psalmist pledges his loyalty to Jerusalem, the city at the center of God’s reign and the location of fellowship with him.

title: Psalms 137:7–9
content_html:
• The poet saw how the Edomites betrayed the people of Judah, so he calls on God to judge Edom. The long conflict between Edom and Judah reached its worst point near the end of Judah’s history. At that time, the Edomites handed the Judeans over to the Babylonians (Obadiah 1:10–11) and celebrated Jerusalem's fall.

  • This prayer asking God to bring justice uses strong language and can be shocking.

title: Psalms 138:1–3
content_html: The Lord receives praise before the gods of the nations.

title: Psalms 138:1–8
content_html: In this wisdom psalm, the Lord is presented as the God of gods. He rescued his people and has a perfect plan for them as individuals. The nations will join Israel in glorifying God.

title: Psalms 138:1–145.21
content_html: In this final group of psalms of David, psalms of praise and wisdom (Psalms 138–139, 145) form a frame around five psalms of lament (Psalms 140–144).

title: Psalms 138:4–5
content_html: As the Lord's glory fills the earth, people must willingly submit to him. Every king (compare Psalm 2) will join in praise when they learn the Lord's ways of truth and love.

title: Psalms 138:6
content_html: God is holy and great, far beyond human understanding (see Psalms 99:2; 113:4; Isaiah 6:1; 57:15).

title: Psalms 138:6–8
content_html: The exalted God cares for the humble (Psalm 138:6), especially his servant, the psalmist (138:7–8).

title: Psalms 138:8
content_html: The psalmist trusts in the Lord's perfect plans and prays for God's continued involvement. The psalmist sees himself as part of God’s plans (see Psalms 9:10; 139:14).

title: Psalms 139:1–6
content_html: The Lord knows the psalmist well. God has examined him closely, in a thorough examination that can be painful at times (Job 7:17–19).

title: Psalms 139:1–24
content_html:
In this wisdom psalm, the psalmist opens every part of his life to God. He invites God to examine his thoughts, actions, and words. Even though life is difficult, he wants to grow in wisdom so that he does not share in the judgment of the wicked (Psalm 141:5; 142:3; 143:8, 10; 144:3–4).

The psalmist also knows that he cannot escape from the Lord. His stress fades as he remembers that God has cared for him with love, even before he was born (Psalm 139:13–18). He ends by asking God to keep examining him and to guide him on the path of wisdom (Psalm 139:23–24).

title: Psalms 139:2
content_html: The phrase when I sit and when I rise refers to everyday activities (compare Deuteronomy 6:6–9).

title: Psalms 139:3–6
content_html: The Lord's all-knowing and all-powerful nature provides comfort to the godly, but it troubles the wicked (Psalm 2:3).

title: Psalms 139:6
content_html: The Lord’s wonderful understanding lies beyond the psalmist’s ability to comprehend (see Judges 13:18).

title: Psalms 139:7–12
content_html: The psalmist shows wonder at God being everywhere at all times.

title: Psalms 139:8
content_html: if I make my bed in Sheol: God has complete access to all he has created, even the most hidden or hard-to-reach places.

title: Psalms 139:9
content_html:
• rise on the wings of the dawn: This poetic expression describes traveling eastward, as far as the sunrise, if that were possible.

  • the farthest sea: This expression takes the traveler in the opposite direction, far to the west.

title: Psalms 139:13
content_html:
• my inmost being: The delicate inner parts of the body include a person's very being.

  • Even when hidden in his mother’s womb, the psalmist was guided by the Lord.

title: Psalms 139:17
content_html: How precious to me are Your thoughts: God’s careful examination shows his loving care. Life with the Lord brings blessings that are beyond words.

title: Psalms 139:19–24
content_html: The psalmist’s sense of awe leads him to consider the wicked and to reflect on himself. The Lord, as the examiner of all people, judges the wicked for their destructive acts and speech. So, the psalmist prays that he will not be among them.

title: Psalms 139:21
content_html: Do I note hate those who hate You: Rejecting evil shows wisdom (see Psalms 1:1; 5:4–6).

title: Psalms 139:23–24
content_html: As the psalmist prays about the wicked, he also reflects on himself. He does not assume that he is righteous. This prayer has been spoken again and again by God’s people over many generations.

title: Psalms 139:24
content_html: The path of wisdom leads to eternal life (see Psalm 1:6; Proverbs 4:18; 6:23).

title: Psalms 140:1–3
content_html: The psalmist prays for the Lord to rescue him from wicked people.

title: Psalms 140:1–13
content_html: In this personal lament, the psalmist describes God's enemies and their evil plans. He prays for the Lord's protection and rescue, trusting in God's strength and justice. He predicts that fire will judge the evil (Psalm 140:9–11) and that he will be proven right and live in the Lord's presence (140:13).

title: Psalms 140:3
content_html: snakes … vipers: These creatures portray the wicked as very corrupt people (see Proverbs 23:32; Isaiah 14:29; Romans 3:13).

title: Psalms 140:4–5
content_html: The psalmist prays for God's protection (see Psalm 121), asking the Lord to keep him safe from the wicked's many plans.

title: Psalms 140:7
content_html: O GOD the Lord: This title comes from combining the name Yahweh with the title Adonai (which means Master). See also Psalms 68:20; 69:6; 71:16; 73:28; 109:21; 141:8.

title: Psalms 140:9–11
content_html: The psalmist prays for God’s judgment on the wicked.

title: Psalms 140:10
content_html: The psalmist asks for burning coals to fall on his enemies and for them to be thrown into the miry pits, so that they cannot attack again. This language hints at the New Testament teachings on hell (see Matthew 5:22, 29–30; 10:28; 18:9; Mark 9:43–47; Luke 12:5; James 3:6; 2 Peter 2:4).

title: Psalms 141:1–2
content_html:
• The psalmist urgently prays for rescue. He does not have incense or an evening offering (Exodus 29:38–39), but his prayers and devotion represent those elements (see Isaiah 1:13; Revelation 5:8).

  • uplifted hands: This is a posture of prayer (Psalms 28:2; 88:9; 143:6; Exodus 9:29; 1 Timothy 2:8).

title: Psalms 141:1–10
content_html: The psalmist prays for rescue and for wisdom. He also looks ahead to the end of evil. The idea that people receive what they deserve (Psalm 141:10) holds the psalm together.

title: Psalms 141:3–5
content_html:
• Wisdom sometimes requires protection from oneself. People might say or think things that lead to a harmful path (Proverbs 13:3; 21:23).

  • Wisdom accepts correction from godly people (see Proverbs 3:11; 9:8; 15:31; 19:25).

title: Psalms 142:1–3
content_html: Although overwhelmed by his persecutors (Psalm 142:6), the psalmist seeks wisdom from the Lord.

title: Psalms 142:1–7
content_html: when he was in the cave: Compare Psalm 57:1.

title: Psalms 142:7
content_html: He feels trapped and stressed, similar to being in prison (see Psalm 107:10).

title: Psalms 142:3–4
content_html: The psalmist feels deep despair (see Psalm 77:3), and the Lord helps him in his distress. The psalmist trusts in the Lord's wisdom (see Psalm 139) but mourns being trapped by evil people (5:9–10; 140:5; 141:9; 142:3).

title: Psalms 142:5
content_html: Even when alone, the psalmist trusts the Lord, who is his life (see Psalms 9:9; 16:5; 27:13).

title: Psalms 142:6–7
content_html: Though overwhelmed by loneliness and trouble, the psalmist focuses on the Lord’s goodness and protection. He knows that the Lord alone is his Redeemer, so he offers his praise.

title: Psalms 142
content_html: The psalmist faces severe persecution, so he cries to the Lord, and the Lord will rescue him.

title: Psalms 143:1–2
content_html: The psalmist prays for God’s love and mercy.

title: Psalms 143:1–12
content_html: In this lament, the psalmist feels overwhelmed by constant trouble from his enemies, so he turns to the Lord’s love, righteousness, and faithfulness. He remembers what God has done in the past and longs for God to renew his love. He opens himself to God’s wisdom because he knows that God’s instruction leads to life.

title: Psalms 143:2
content_html: Everyone has sinned and is guilty before God (Romans 3:20–24).

title: Psalms 143:3–4
content_html: My spirit grows faint within me: The psalmist admits his desperation as his life seems to slip away under cruel and powerful enemies.

title: Psalms 143:5–6
content_html: Remembering God’s acts from the past reassures the psalmist of God’s faithfulness (Psalms 42:4; 63:6; 77:3, 6; 119:55).

title: Psalms 143:6
content_html: The psalmist’s thirst is a deep longing for God’s redemption (see Psalm 42:1–2).

title: Psalms 143:7
content_html: The psalmist experiences depression. He describes himself as close to death from sorrow (Psalms 31:10; 119:87). He feels worn out (Psalms 39:10; 71:9; 119:81). He is weak from longing (Psalm 84:2). He feels overwhelmed by the burdens of life (Psalm 90:7, 9).

title: Psalms 143:7–10
content_html: The psalmist asks the Lord to renew his love.

title: Psalms 143:8–10
content_html:
• The psalmist prays for wisdom (see Psalms 5:8; 25:4–6; 138; 143:10).

  • Your good Spirit: The Spirit brings God's goodness (see Psalm 23:6).

title: Psalms 143:11
content_html: revive: Here, it means to bring someone out of despair and restore them to life (Psalms 80:19; 85:6; 138:7).

title: Psalms 143:12
content_html: The psalm ends with a prayer for God to show that the psalmist is in the right and to restore his life.

title: Psalms 144:1–2
content_html: my Rock … my steadfast love … my deliverer … my shield: The psalmist’s descriptive names for the Lord reveal his deep love for him.

title: Psalms 144:1–15
content_html:
This lament begins with an exclamation of love for the Lord. The community then reflects on the temporary nature of life (Psalm 144:3–4). The psalmist asks for God's help and anticipates victory (Psalm 144:5–11).

A new song (Psalm 144:9–10) and a prayer for rescue (144:11) introduce the theme of the Lord’s blessings through his care and protection (Psalm 144:12–15).

title: Psalms 144:3–4
content_html:
what is man: The same question in Psalm 8:4–6 leads to the answer that humans are only mortal (they do not live forever), yet God gives them honor and responsibility to rule.

The psalmist focuses on how brief and fragile human life is. He describes it as temporary and passing quickly (see Psalms 90:3, 7–10; 102:11; 109:23; 146:4).

title: Psalms 144:5–8
content_html: The psalmist asks God to act against his enemies (compare Psalm 18:7–19).

title: Psalms 144:12
content_html: The psalmist compares sons and daughters to plants and pillars, which are images of strength and beauty.

title: Psalms 144:12–15
content_html: This prayer for the Lord’s blessing brings the laments of Psalms 140–144 to a close. It describes God’s provision (care for needs) and protection (keeping people safe; see Psalm 127), not only for the psalmist but also for his children and for all who follow God.

title: Psalms 144:13–14
content_html: no going into captivity, and no cry of lament: These images show peace and safety.

title: Psalms 145:1–3
content_html: The psalmist praises God, the Great King who reigns over all other rulers (see Psalm 47:2).

title: Psalms 145:1–21
content_html:
This psalm of praise is the last in this collection of the psalms of David (Psalms 138–145). Praise continues in the last five psalms (Psalms 146–150).

This psalm celebrates the Lord as the King of all (Psalm 145:1–3, 10–13). God's actions show his royal qualities (Psalm 145:4–7). He cares for all creatures (Psalm 145:14–17) and for those who fear him (145:18–20).

title: Psalms 145:4–7
content_html: This litany (a repeated form of praise, often with the same line said again and again) celebrates God’s character and his mighty deeds. Each generation will continue to tell others about God’s power (Psalm 22:30).

title: Psalms 145:8–9
content_html: God consistently demonstrates his character toward all his creation (see Psalm 136:25).

title: Psalms 145:10–13
content_html: These verses form a description of the Lord’s kingdom.

title: Psalms 145:14–20
content_html: The Lord cares for his creatures.

title: Psalms 145:20
content_html: The Lord cares for those who love him and fear him. God-fearers love the Lord and find refuge in him (Psalm 5:11). They seek him as their only true Savior (Psalms 40:16; 70:4). They will experience a good future (Psalm 69:36) and enjoy the peace that God gives (119:165).

title: Psalms 146:1
content_html: This verse is similar to Psalm 103:1, 2, 22; 104:1, 35.

title: Psalms 146:1–10
content_html: The call to praise (Psalm 146:1–2) leads to a warning against foolishness and false confidence (146:3–4). The psalm ends with a blessing for everyone who places their hope in the Lord (Psalm 146:5–7).

title: Psalms 146:1–150.6
content_html: The Psalter ends with five hallelujah psalms, so named because each begins and ends with Praise the LORD! (Hebrew haleluyah).

title: Psalms 146:8–9
content_html: Repeating the Lord’s name (shown as LORD in the Berean Standard Bible) four times emphasizes that he alone heals, lifts burdens, loves, and protects.

title: Psalms 147:1
content_html: This verse is a call to worship.

title: Psalms 147:1–20
content_html: The psalmist calls for the Lord to be praised for restoring and blessing Zion, caring for the poor, displaying his power over nature, and revealing himself to his people.

title: Psalms 147:2–6
content_html: The psalmist remembers how the Lord restored Jerusalem and brought back his people who had suffered in exile.

title: Psalms 147:4–5
content_html:
• The Lord created the heavens.

  • He determines the number of the stars: God's people can feel secure knowing the Lord cares for every detail of his creation. He knows all that belongs to him.

title: Psalms 147:6
content_html: God delivers justice to both the poor and the wicked (Psalms 55:22; 140:12; 145:14; 146:7).

title: Psalms 147:7–11
content_html: Human achievements do not impress God, who commands nature. Instead, he finds joy in those who fear him.

title: Psalms 147:8
content_html: God has complete authority and power over the weather (Psalms 77:17–18; 78:26; 104:4; 148:5–6).

title: Psalms 147:10–11
content_html: the legs of a man: Any physical or mental strength comes from the Lord, like the strength of a horse. These gifts are reasons to praise the Lord, not to boast (Jeremiah 9:23–24).

title: Psalms 147:13
content_html:
• the bars of your gates: These represent protection from enemies.

  • God blesses his people by giving them safety, prosperity, peace, and the things they need (see Psalm 133:3).

title: Psalms 147:15–18
content_html: Simply by speaking his command, the Lord can change the season from harsh winter to refreshing spring (see Psalms 147:8; 148:5–6).

title: Psalms 147:19–20
content_html: word … statutes … judgments: The Lord gave powerful words to Israel so that they might obey him (Psalm 105:45).

title: Psalms 148:1
content_html: When Jesus entered Jerusalem, the crowds welcomed him like a king and shouted blessings, including a phrase from this verse (Matthew 21:9; Mark 11:10; Luke 19:38).

title: Psalms 148:1–14
content_html: This psalm calls for the whole created order to praise the Lord because he has honored his faithful people.

title: Psalms 148:6
content_html: The created order is stable because of the Lord's word (see Psalm 33:9).

title: Psalms 148:8
content_html: In the ancient Near East, people believed that different gods controlled different parts of nature. But the Lord rules over all of nature. Every part of creation serves him and brings him praise by obeying him (Psalms 77:17–18; 104:4; 135:7; 147:8, 15–18; Numbers 11:31).

title: Psalms 148:11–12
content_html: The psalmist calls all humans to offer praise to God, regardless of status, age, or gender.

title: Psalms 148:13–14
content_html:
• Praise of the Lord by all people is right because he is glorious and has blessed his people (see Psalm 147:13; Isaiah 46:13; Ephesians 3:10).

  • His saint are God's trusting and devoted servants (Psalm 86:2).

title: Psalms 149:1–9
content_html: This psalm of praise celebrates the Lord's victory. God is the Maker of Israel (Psalm 149:1–2) and the victorious King who shares his glory with his faithful followers (149:3–9).

title: Psalms 149:4
content_html: God’s people, whom the world ignores and looks down on, will be shown to be right (Matthew 19:30). The humble will overcome those who oppress and persecute them (see Isaiah 61:3).

title: Psalms 149:6–9
content_html: vengeance: The Lord is the one who brings justice against wrongdoing (see Psalms 2:3; 94:1; 102:20; 142:7; 146:7).

title: Psalms 150:1
content_html: The phrase mighty heavens refers to Genesis 1 and God's amazing work in filling the emptiness.

title: Psalms 150:1–6
content_html: Everything that breathes is commanded to praise the Lord as he deserves. The whole Psalter describes the greatness of the Lord, so this psalm is a fitting conclusion.

title: Psalms 150:3–5
content_html:
• The psalmist calls all people to praise God with instruments and dancing.

  • flutes: These are wind instruments (instruments you blow into to make sound). The Bible does not mention them as part of formal temple worship. This suggests they were used more in everyday life or celebrations, rather than in official worship at the temple.

  • clashing cymbals: Temple musicians clapped two bronze bowls together to make a loud sound as part of the music.

title: Psalms 150:6
content_html: praise the LORD!: This refrain is a fitting conclusion to the entire book of Psalms.
